items.explanation: 正是因为...才... (zhèng shì yīn wèi...cái...) is an emphatic pattern: 'precisely because of...only then...' The 正是 (precisely/exactly) intensifies 因为, and 才 emphasizes that the result is specifically triggered by this cause. This is a powerful rhetorical structure: 正是因为困难，我们才要坚持 (Precisely because it's difficult, we must persist). It's frequently used in motivational speeches, editorials, and persuasive writing. The 正是...才 frame demands attention and conviction.

items.question: 他一紧张_____说不出话来。
items.translation: (As soon as he gets nervous, he can't speak.)
items.explanation: 一...就... (yī...jiù...) expresses an immediate or automatic cause-effect: 'as soon as...then...' 一紧张就说不出话 = the moment (he) gets nervous, (he) can't speak. This pattern highlights an instantaneous or habitual trigger-response relationship. Common: 一下雨就堵车 (whenever it rains, traffic jams), 一看书就困 (as soon as opens a book, gets sleepy). The 一...就 pattern is more vivid than 因为...所以 because it captures the immediacy and automaticity of the cause-effect connection.

items.question: 经济发展_____了环境问题。
items.translation: (Economic development caused environmental problems.)
items.explanation: 导致 (dǎo zhì) means 'to lead to / to cause / to result in.' It's a formal cause-effect VERB rather than a conjunction. Structure: cause + 导致 + effect. 经济发展导致了环境问题 = Economic development caused environmental issues. Similar formal cause verbs: 造成 (cause/create problems), 引起 (give rise to), 促使 (prompt/drive). These are essential for academic writing, news reporting, and formal analysis. 导致 usually implies negative consequences; for positive outcomes, use 促进 (promote) or 带来 (bring about).

items.question: _____是这样，我们也不能放弃。
items.translation: (Even though this is the case, we still can't give up.)
items.explanation: 即使 (jí shǐ) means 'even if / even though' — it introduces a concessive cause that does NOT lead to the expected effect. 即使是这样，也不能放弃 = Even if that's the case, still can't give up. This contrasts with 因为...所以 (expected cause→effect). 即使...也... breaks the expected causal chain: the cause exists but the expected consequence is denied. Compare: 虽然...但是 (although...but — factual concession) vs. 即使...也 (even if...still — hypothetical/strong concession). 即使 is stronger and more determined.

items.question: 他说他明天_____来。
items.translation: (He said he would come tomorrow.)
items.explanation: Chinese reported speech is simpler than English — no tense backshift is needed. 他说他明天会来 (He said he would come tomorrow) keeps the original tense. In English, 'will' changes to 'would,' but Chinese 会 stays as 会. The basic reported speech pattern: subject + 说 + reported content. Chinese reports speech almost as a direct quotation without tense changes. This is a major advantage for English speakers learning Chinese: you simply attach 说 and reproduce the original statement. No complex sequence-of-tenses rules to memorize.

items.question: 老师_____我们明天要考试。
items.translation: (The teacher told us we'll have an exam tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 告诉 (gào sù) means 'to tell (someone something).' Structure: subject + 告诉 + person + content. 老师告诉我们 = the teacher told us. Unlike 说 (say, no person needed), 告诉 requires both a person AND content. You cannot say *告诉明天考试 (must include who was told). Compare: 说 (say — general), 告诉 (tell someone), 通知 (notify — formal), 转告 (pass on a message). Each reporting verb has different structural requirements. 告诉 is the most common for reporting to specific people.

items.question: 她问我_____去过中国。
items.translation: (She asked me whether I had been to China.)
items.explanation: Reported questions use 有没有 (yǒu méi yǒu) for yes/no questions. 她问我有没有去过中国 = She asked me whether I had been to China. In Chinese, reported yes/no questions use the A-not-A structure (有没有, 是不是, 去不去) or 是否 (formal). No 'if/whether' marker like English is needed — the A-not-A structure itself signals it's a question. For wh-questions, keep the question word: 她问我去了哪里 (She asked where I went). Chinese reported questions retain question markers rather than changing word order.

items.question: 妈妈让我_____作业。
items.translation: (Mom told me to do my homework.)
items.explanation: 让 (ràng) reports commands/requests: 'told someone to / made someone do.' 妈妈让我做作业 = Mom told me to do homework. The reported command in Chinese uses 让 + person + verb (bare verb, no tense marker). Other command-reporting verbs: 叫 (jiào, tell/order — slightly informal), 要求 (yāo qiú, require — formal), 命令 (mìng lìng, command — very formal). The structure stays simple: Reporter + 让/叫 + person + action. No infinitive 'to' is needed as in English.

items.question: 他_____他不想去。
items.translation: (He said he doesn't want to go.)
items.explanation: 说 (shuō) is the most basic reporting verb. Structure: subject + 说 + content. 他说他不想去 = He said he doesn't want to go. Notice: Chinese does not change the pronouns systematically like English ('I don't want' → 'he doesn't want'). In 他说他不想去, the second 他 refers to the speaker himself. Context determines reference. In practice, this rarely causes confusion because Chinese relies heavily on context. When ambiguity could arise, Chinese speakers add names: 他说小明不想去.

items.question: 她跟我说她_____换工作了。
items.translation: (She told me that she has changed jobs.)
items.explanation: 跟...说 (gēn...shuō) means 'tell someone / say to someone.' 她跟我说 = She told me. This is more conversational than 告诉. The reported content 已经换工作了 uses 已经...了 (already...completed) to report a completed action. Again, no tense backshift: the original statement was 我已经换工作了 (I've already changed jobs), reported exactly the same way. Chinese reported speech preserves the original time reference entirely, making it structurally much simpler than English indirect speech.

items.question: 他问我_____学中文。
items.translation: (He asked me why I study Chinese.)
items.explanation: Reported wh-questions keep the question word in place. 他问我为什么学中文 = He asked me why I study Chinese. The word order doesn't change like in English ('Why do you study' → 'why I study'). Chinese reported questions maintain the same word order as direct questions — the only change is adding the reporting frame (他问我). This applies to all wh-words: 他问我什么时候来 (asked when), 他问我在哪里 (asked where), 他问我怎么去 (asked how). Beautifully simple and consistent.

items.question: 老师_____我们好好复习。
items.translation: (The teacher asked us to review well.)
items.explanation: 叫 (jiào) for reported commands means 'told/ordered someone to.' It's slightly more casual than 让. 老师叫我们好好复习 = The teacher told us to review thoroughly. Nuance differences: 让 (let/allow/tell — neutral), 叫 (call on/tell — slightly more direct), 要求 (require — formal demand), 请 (request — polite). The choice reveals the speaker's attitude toward the command: 老师请我们复习 (teacher kindly requested) vs. 老师叫我们复习 (teacher told us to) vs. 老师要求我们复习 (teacher required us to).

items.question: 他提到他下个月要_____。
items.translation: (He mentioned he's going to get married next month.)
items.explanation: 提到 (tí dào) means 'to mention.' 他提到 = He mentioned. The reported future event uses 要 + verb: 要结婚 (going to get married). Notice 结婚 is a V-O compound (结 + 婚), so no extra 了 is needed here since the action hasn't happened yet. Reporting verbs in Chinese have rich variety: 说 (say), 告诉 (tell), 提到 (mention), 表示 (express/state), 声称 (claim), 透露 (reveal/disclose), 承认 (admit), 否认 (deny). Each adds different nuance to the reported content.

items.question: 据说这家餐厅的菜_____好吃。
items.translation: (It is said that this restaurant's food is very delicious.)
items.explanation: 据说 (jù shuō) means 'it is said / reportedly / according to hearsay.' It introduces information from unspecified sources. 据说这家餐厅很好吃 = It's said this restaurant is delicious. This impersonal reporting form has several variants: 据说 (hearsay), 据报道 (according to reports), 据了解 (according to understanding), 听说 (heard that — personal hearsay). 听说 is more casual and personal (I heard), while 据说 is more general and formal (people say). Both are essential for sharing secondhand information in Chinese.

items.question: 我_____他很快就会回来。
items.translation: (I heard that he'll come back very soon.)
items.explanation: 听说 (tīng shuō) means 'I heard (that)' — the most common way to report hearsay in casual Chinese. Structure: 听说 + reported content. No 'that' conjunction is needed. 听说他很快回来 = I heard he'll be back soon. 听说 is incredibly frequent in daily conversation: 听说你要去日本？(Heard you're going to Japan?), 听说那个电影很好看 (Heard that movie is good). It naturally introduces information you received from others and is essential for everyday Chinese communication.

items.question: 他答应_____帮我搬家。
items.translation: (He promised to help me move.)
items.explanation: 答应 (dā yìng) means 'to promise / to agree to.' Reporting promises: 他答应会帮我搬家 = He promised he would help me move. The 会 indicates future commitment. Other promise/commitment reporting verbs: 保证 (guarantee), 发誓 (swear), 承诺 (pledge — formal). Chinese reported promises are straightforward: reporter + 答应 + content. No tense change needed. 他答应了 can also stand alone meaning 'He agreed/promised' without specifying the content, when context makes it clear.

items.question: 她抱怨说天气_____热。
items.translation: (She complained that the weather is too hot.)
items.explanation: 抱怨说 (bào yuàn shuō) means 'complained (saying).' Notice the pattern: attitude verb + 说 + content. This is a productive Chinese pattern: 笑着说 (said laughingly), 生气地说 (said angrily), 高兴地说 (said happily). The 说 anchors the reported content while the preceding word reveals the speaker's attitude. 抱怨说太热 reports both the complaint AND the content. This 'manner of speaking' + 说 pattern enriches Chinese narrative and is heavily used in storytelling, novels, and daily conversation.

items.question: 报道_____明天气温会下降。
items.translation: (Reports say the temperature will drop tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 称 (chēng) means 'state/claim' — a formal reporting verb used in news and official communication. 报道称 = reports state/claim. This is journalistic Chinese: 报道称...据悉...消息人士表示... (Reports state...it is learned...sources indicate...). For news reading comprehension, knowing formal reporting verbs is essential: 称 (claim/state), 指出 (point out), 强调 (emphasize), 表示 (express), 透露 (disclose/reveal). Each carries different implications about the reliability and nature of the reported information.

items.question: 他建议我们_____坐地铁去。
items.translation: (He suggested that we take the subway.)
items.explanation: 建议 (jiàn yì) means 'to suggest.' Reported suggestions: 他建议我们还是坐地铁去 = He suggested we'd better take the subway. The 还是 adds a nuance of 'it would be better to.' Chinese has multiple suggestion-reporting verbs: 建议 (suggest), 推荐 (recommend), 提议 (propose), 劝 (advise/persuade). Summary of reported speech in Chinese: no tense changes, keep original word order for questions, use appropriate reporting verbs (说/告诉/问/让/叫/建议), and leverage attitude markers to enrich the narrative. Simpler than English but equally expressive.

items.question: 他跑得比我_____多了。
items.translation: (He runs much faster than me.)
items.explanation: 比 (bǐ) + adjective + 多了 is the pattern for 'much more [adjective] than.' 快多了 means 'much faster.' The 多了 intensifier at the end emphasizes a large difference. Without 多了, 他跑得比我快 simply means 'he runs faster than me' — adding 多了 conveys a significant gap. Other intensifiers in comparative structures include 得多 (a lot more) and 多得多 (much much more). This pattern works with any adjective.

items.question: 这个_____那个一样大。
items.translation: (This one is as big as that one.)
items.explanation: 跟...一样 (gēn...yíyàng) means 'the same as' — the basic equality comparison pattern. 跟那个一样大 = as big as that one. The structure is: A + 跟 + B + 一样 + adjective. 跟 can be replaced by 和 (hé) in this pattern with the same meaning. To negate, add 不: 跟那个不一样大 = not as big as that one. This is one of the most essential comparison structures in Chinese, used constantly in everyday conversation.

items.question: 她的中文水平_____来越好了。
items.translation: (Her Chinese level is getting better and better.)
items.explanation: 越来越 (yuè lái yuè) + adjective means 'more and more' or 'getting [adjective]-er and [adjective]-er.' 越来越好 = better and better. This structure expresses progressive change over time. It always uses 越 twice with 来 in the middle. Common examples: 越来越冷 (colder and colder), 越来越难 (harder and harder), 越来越多 (more and more). This is a very high-frequency pattern in both spoken and written Chinese.

items.question: 他是我们班_____高的学生。
items.translation: (He is the tallest student in our class.)
items.explanation: 最 (zuì) + adjective creates the superlative form — 'the most [adjective].' 最高 = tallest/the tallest. Unlike English which adds '-est' or uses 'most,' Chinese consistently uses 最 for all superlatives. No exceptions or irregular forms exist. Examples: 最好 (best), 最快 (fastest), 最重要 (most important). The scope can be specified with 里/中: 我们班里最高的 = the tallest in our class.

items.question: 今天比昨天冷_____。
items.translation: (Today is a bit colder than yesterday.)
items.explanation: 比 + adjective + 一点儿 (yì diǎnr) means 'a little bit more [adjective] than.' The 一点儿 softens the comparison, indicating a small difference. Compare the intensity scale: 一点儿 (a little) < 一些 (somewhat) < 多了 (much more) < 得多 (a lot more). In conversation, 一点儿 is very common for polite or gentle comparisons. Northern Chinese speakers often use the 儿 suffix; Southern speakers may say 一点.

items.question: 这本书没有那本书_____。
items.translation: (This book is not as interesting as that book.)
items.explanation: 没有...那么 + adjective is the negative comparison pattern meaning 'not as [adjective] as.' 没有那本书有意思 = not as interesting as that book. This is the negative counterpart of 跟...一样. Note that 那么 is sometimes omitted in casual speech. The pattern A + 没有 + B + (那么) + adjective is extremely common. 有意思 (yǒu yìsi) literally means 'has meaning/interest' and is the standard way to say 'interesting.'

items.question: 你越努力，成绩_____越好。
items.translation: (The harder you work, the better your grades will be.)
items.explanation: 越...越... (yuè...yuè...) means 'the more...the more...' — a correlative comparative. The first 越 clause states the condition, and 就越 introduces the result. 越努力就越好 = the harder you work, the better. This pattern links two proportional changes. The 就 is often used before the second 越 to strengthen the causal connection. Examples: 越吃越胖 (the more you eat, the fatter), 越想越害怕 (the more you think, the more scared).

items.question: 他的汉字写得比以前_____多了。
items.translation: (He writes Chinese characters much better than before.)
items.explanation: 比以前 (bǐ yǐqián) means 'compared to before.' Combined with adjective + 多了, it expresses significant improvement. 写得比以前好多了 = writes much better than before. Note the structural complement 得 (de) is required after the verb when comparing how an action is performed. The full pattern is: Verb + 得 + 比 + reference + adjective + 多了. This is essential for expressing progress and improvement in Chinese.

items.question: 这两个房间一样_____。
items.translation: (These two rooms are equally big.)
items.explanation: 一样 (yíyàng) + adjective directly expresses equality: 'equally [adjective].' 一样大 = equally big/the same size. When the comparison items are clear from context, A 跟 B can be omitted. This simplified form is very natural in spoken Chinese. Note: 一样 can also stand alone as a predicate: 这两个一样 (these two are the same). The adjective after 一样 specifies which dimension is being compared.

items.question: 她唱歌唱得_____我好。
items.translation: (She sings better than me.)
items.explanation: 比 (bǐ) is the core comparison preposition meaning 'than.' When comparing actions (not just attributes), use: Subject + Verb + 得 + 比 + object + adjective. 唱得比我好 = sings better than me. The verb is often repeated when using the degree complement 得: 她唱歌唱得比我好. This verb repetition is grammatically required when both an object and a degree complement are present. It's a distinctive feature of Chinese grammar.

items.question: 他比我大_____岁。
items.translation: (He is three years older than me.)
items.explanation: 比 + adjective + specific quantity precisely states the difference in a comparison. 比我大三岁 = three years older than me. Chinese can specify exact differences elegantly: 比我高五厘米 (5 cm taller), 比这个贵十块 (10 yuan more expensive). This is more precise than 多了 (much more) or 一点儿 (a little). The quantity comes directly after the adjective. This numeric precision in comparisons is used constantly in daily Chinese conversation.

items.question: 这个菜不如那个菜_____。
items.translation: (This dish is not as delicious as that dish.)
items.explanation: 不如 (bùrú) means 'not as good as' or 'inferior to' — a more literary/formal negative comparison. A + 不如 + B + adjective = A is not as [adjective] as B. 不如那个菜好吃 = not as delicious as that dish. Compared to 没有...那么, 不如 carries a stronger connotation that A is inferior. It can also be used without an adjective: 我不如他 (I'm not as good as him). 不如 is common in both written and spoken registers.

items.question: 他们俩差不多_____高。
items.translation: (The two of them are about the same height.)
items.explanation: 差不多一样 (chà bu duō yíyàng) means 'about the same' — approximate equality. Adding 差不多 before 一样 softens the comparison to mean 'roughly equal' rather than 'exactly the same.' 差不多一样高 = about the same height. 差不多 (literally 'differ not much') is one of the most useful hedging expressions in Chinese. It can be used alone: 差不多 = 'almost/about right.' Combined with 一样, it creates natural, conversational comparisons.

items.question: 这件衣服_____那件好看得多。
items.translation: (This piece of clothing looks much better than that one.)
items.explanation: 比 + adjective + 得多 (de duō) is another way to express 'much more [adjective] than.' 比那件好看得多 = looks much better than that one. The difference between 多了 and 得多: both mean 'much more,' but 得多 sounds slightly more formal/emphatic. They are largely interchangeable: 好多了 ≈ 好得多. The full intensity scale for comparisons: 一点儿 < 一些 < 多了/得多 < 多得多. Mastering these modifiers is key to natural-sounding Chinese.

items.question: 他连我_____不如。
items.translation: (He is not even as good as me.)
items.explanation: 连...都不如 (lián...dōu bùrú) means 'not even as good as' — an emphatic negative comparison. 连我都不如 = not even as good as me (implying the speaker sets a low bar). 连 (lián) adds emphasis meaning 'even,' and 都 reinforces the inclusive sense. This structure expresses strong criticism or surprise. The pattern 连...都/也 is broadly used for emphasis beyond comparisons: 连孩子都知道 (even children know). It's a powerful rhetorical tool in Chinese.

items.question: 这个地方_____漂亮啊！
items.translation: (This place is so beautiful!)
items.explanation: 真 (zhēn) means 'really/truly' and is one of the most natural exclamatory intensifiers. 真漂亮啊 = really beautiful! The sentence-final particle 啊 (a) adds emotional force. While 很 is neutral ('very'), 真 carries genuine surprise or admiration. Compare: 真好吃 (really delicious!), 真有意思 (really interesting!). In exclamatory sentences, 真 is preferred over 很 because it conveys the speaker's emotional reaction rather than a simple factual statement.

items.question: _____好吃了！
items.translation: (It's so delicious!)
items.explanation: 太 (tài)...了 is the classic exclamatory pattern meaning 'so/too [adjective]!' 太好吃了 = so delicious! The 了 at the end is essential — 太好吃 alone sounds incomplete and unnatural. Common expressions: 太好了 (great!), 太贵了 (too expensive!), 太难了 (too hard!). Note that 太...了 can express both positive excitement and negative excess depending on context and tone. In exclamations, it almost always conveys strong emotion rather than literal excess.

items.question: 这个孩子_____聪明啊！
items.translation: (This child is so clever!)
items.explanation: 多 (duō) + adjective + 啊 is an exclamatory pattern meaning 'how [adjective]!' 多聪明啊 = how clever! This mirrors the English 'How clever!' construction. It always requires the final particle 啊. While 真 expresses genuine reaction and 太 expresses degree, 多 specifically creates a rhetorical exclamation of admiration. Compare: 多好啊 (how wonderful!), 多美啊 (how beautiful!). This pattern is common in spoken Chinese and literature.

items.question: 今天的天气_____好啊！
items.translation: (The weather today is so nice!)
items.explanation: 好 (hǎo) as an intensifier means 'so/really' in exclamations — different from its meaning of 'good.' 好好啊 would be redundant, but 好大啊 (so big!), 好冷啊 (so cold!) are natural. 好 + adjective + 啊 is a casual, warm exclamation pattern. It's slightly softer and more conversational than 太...了. Common in daily speech: 好可爱啊 (so cute!), 好厉害啊 (so amazing!). Note: 好 as intensifier is more informal and is rarely used in formal writing.

items.question: 哇，你的房间_____干净！
items.translation: (Wow, your room is so clean!)
items.explanation: Interjections like 哇 (wā, 'wow') often begin exclamatory sentences. Combined with 真 + adjective, they create natural exclamations. 哇，真干净！= Wow, really clean! Common Chinese interjections: 哇 (wow), 哎呀 (oh no/oh my), 天哪 (oh heavens), 啊 (ah). These are essential for natural conversation. Note that 哇 is informal and friendly; in formal contexts, you'd simply use the intensifier pattern without the interjection.

items.question: 这道题_____难了！我做不出来。
items.translation: (This question is too hard! I can't solve it.)
items.explanation: 太...了 in negative contexts means 'too [adjective]' — expressing excessive degree. 太难了 = too hard (beyond acceptable). Context determines whether 太...了 is positive (太好了！= Great!) or negative (太难了！= Too hard!). When followed by a negative consequence (我做不出来 = I can't solve it), it clearly means 'excessively.' This dual nature of 太...了 is important: it can express both delight and complaint. Tone of voice and context are the key differentiators.

items.question: 简直_____不可思议！
items.translation: (It's simply unbelievable!)
items.explanation: 简直 (jiǎnzhí) means 'simply/absolutely' — a strong emphatic adverb. 简直太不可思议 = simply too unbelievable. 简直 elevates any exclamation to an extreme level. It precedes the intensifier: 简直太好了 (absolutely wonderful), 简直不敢相信 (simply can't believe it). This word is common in both spoken and written Chinese when expressing strong emotions. It pairs naturally with 太...了, creating a double-emphasis structure that's very expressive.

items.question: 没想到你_____来了！
items.translation: (I didn't expect you actually came!)
items.explanation: 竟然 (jìngrán) means 'actually/unexpectedly' — expressing surprise. 竟然来了 = actually came (to my surprise). It differs from: 忽然/突然 (suddenly — about speed, not surprise) and 果然 (as expected — opposite meaning). 竟然 is used when something happens contrary to expectation. Other surprise expressions: 居然 (similar to 竟然 but slightly more informal), 想不到 (wouldn't have thought). Exclamatory surprise is a rich area of Chinese expression.

items.question: 这部电影_____感人了！
items.translation: (This movie is so touching!)
items.explanation: 太感人了 (tài gǎnrén le) = so touching/moving! 感人 literally means 'move people' and is an adjective for emotionally moving content. The 太...了 pattern is the most versatile exclamatory form in Chinese. It works with virtually any adjective: 太有趣了 (so interesting), 太可怕了 (so scary), 太幸福了 (so happy). In movie/book reviews, food commentary, and daily reactions, 太...了 is the go-to exclamation pattern for Chinese speakers.

items.question: 这儿的风景_____美了！
items.translation: (The scenery here is so beautiful!)
items.explanation: 太美了 is a classic exclamation about beauty. Note 这儿 (zhèr) = 'here' — a contracted form of 这里 (zhèlǐ). Both are interchangeable, but 这儿 is more common in northern Mandarin while 这里 is more standard/universal. The sentence structure places the topic (这儿的风景) first, followed by the exclamatory predicate (太美了). This topic-comment structure is fundamental to Chinese: state what you're talking about, then comment on it.

items.question: 你怎么_____晚才来！
items.translation: (How come you came so late!)
items.explanation: 这么 (zhème) means 'this/so' when expressing surprise at degree. 这么晚 = this late/so late. 怎么...这么... is a common reproachful exclamation pattern: 'How come [it's] so [adjective]!' Compare: 这么 (this much — close/present), 那么 (that much — distant/hypothetical), 多么 (how — purely exclamatory). 怎么这么贵 (how come so expensive!), 怎么这么难 (how come so hard!). This pattern is very common in informal speech when expressing frustration or surprise.

items.question: 多_____的一幅画啊！
items.translation: (What a beautiful painting!)
items.explanation: 多 + adjective + 的 + noun + 啊 is the full exclamatory pattern for admiring something specific. 多美的一幅画啊 = What a beautiful painting! This mirrors English 'What a...!' The measure word 幅 (fú) is specifically for paintings and calligraphy. Chinese measure words in exclamations follow the same rules as in regular sentences. More examples: 多聪明的孩子啊 (What a clever child!), 多好的主意啊 (What a great idea!).

items.question: 她居然_____了第一名！
items.translation: (She actually got first place!)
items.explanation: 居然 (jūrán) expresses surprise, similar to 竟然 but slightly more colloquial. 拿了第一名 = got/took first place. 拿 literally means 'to take/grab' and is used for winning prizes/places. Exclamatory sentences with 居然/竟然 emphasize that the result defied expectations. The 了 after the verb marks completion — she has already achieved it. Compare: 居然赢了 (actually won!), 居然考了一百分 (actually scored 100!). Both 居然 and 竟然 are essential for expressing surprise in Chinese.

items.question: 天哪，_____下这么大的雨！
items.translation: (Oh heavens, it's raining so heavily!)
items.explanation: 天哪 (tiān na) is an interjection meaning 'oh heavens/oh my god.' 竟然下这么大的雨 = it's actually raining this heavily (unexpectedly). Chinese interjections set emotional tone: 天哪 (surprise), 哎呀 (dismay), 天啊 (shock), 我的天 (my god — modern slang). Note 这么大的雨 = 'rain this big' — Chinese describes rain quantity with 大 (big) and 小 (small) rather than 'heavy/light' as in English. This is a common source of learner errors.

items.question: 你做的菜_____好吃啊！
items.translation: (The food you made is so delicious!)
items.explanation: 真好吃啊 expresses genuine admiration for someone's cooking — a very common compliment in Chinese culture. The structure 你做的菜 (the dishes you made) uses 的 to create a descriptive phrase modifying 菜 (dishes). Summary of exclamatory intensifiers: 真 (genuine admiration, warm), 太...了 (strong degree, versatile), 好 (casual, conversational), 多...啊 (rhetorical admiration), 简直 (absolute emphasis). Mix and match these to create natural, emotionally rich exclamations in Chinese conversation.

items.question: 请_____坐。
items.translation: (Please sit down.)
items.explanation: 请 (qǐng) + verb is the polite imperative form, meaning 'please [do something].' 请坐 = please sit down. Note that 坐 appears twice in this common expression — 请坐坐, but 请坐 alone is the standard form. 请 transforms any command into a polite request. Common polite imperatives: 请进 (please come in), 请说 (please speak), 请等一下 (please wait a moment). Using 请 is essential in formal situations, with strangers, and in service contexts.

items.question: _____说话！
items.translation: (Don't speak!/Be quiet!)
items.explanation: 别 (bié) + verb is the negative imperative: 'don't [do something].' 别说话 = don't speak/be quiet. 别 is the spoken form; its formal written equivalent is 不要 (búyào) or 勿 (wù). Compare: 别走 (don't go), 别担心 (don't worry), 别客气 (don't be polite = you're welcome). 别 is softer than 不要, which can sound more forceful. In urgent situations, 不要 or 别 with rising tone convey stronger prohibition.

items.question: 快_____！要迟到了。
items.translation: (Hurry up! We're going to be late.)
items.explanation: 快点儿 (kuài diǎnr) = hurry up — one of the most common imperatives. 快 means 'fast/quick,' and 点儿 means 'a bit.' Together they create an urgent but not harsh command. Similar patterns: 慢点儿 (slow down), 小心点儿 (be more careful), 认真点儿 (be more serious). The 点儿 softens the command slightly — without it, 快！alone sounds very abrupt. The follow-up clause 要迟到了 uses 要...了 to express 'about to' — imminent action.

items.question: 你们_____安静！
items.translation: (You all be quiet!)
items.explanation: Direct imperatives can use the verb/adjective alone without any softening particle. 安静 (ānjìng) = be quiet. Adding 你们 (you all) specifies the addressees. Without any softener like 请 or 一下, this is a firm, authoritative command — appropriate for teachers, parents, or urgent situations. To soften: 请安静一下 (please be quiet for a moment). The choice between direct and softened imperatives depends on authority level, urgency, and relationship.

items.question: 把门_____上。
items.translation: (Close the door.)
items.explanation: 把 (bǎ) + object + verb + complement is a common imperative pattern for giving specific instructions about handling objects. 把门关上 = close the door (take the door and close it shut). The 上 is a directional complement indicating completion/closure. 把 imperatives are very common in daily life: 把灯关了 (turn off the light), 把书拿来 (bring the book), 把手洗干净 (wash your hands clean). The 把 construction emphasizes the specific disposal or handling of the object.

items.question: 你给我_____这里！
items.translation: (You stay right here! / Don't you dare leave!)
items.explanation: 你给我 (nǐ gěi wǒ) + verb is an angry/forceful imperative pattern. 给我 literally means 'for me' but functions as an emphatic command marker conveying authority or anger. 给我待在这里 = stay here (and I mean it). Compare: 你给我出去 (get out!), 你给我安静 (shut up!). This is a strong imperative used by parents, authority figures, or in emotional situations. It would be rude between equals. Understanding register is crucial for appropriate Chinese usage.

items.question: 我们走_____！
items.translation: (Let's go!)
items.explanation: Verb + 吧 (ba) creates a suggestion or soft imperative: 'let's [do something].' 走吧 = let's go. 吧 is one of the most important sentence-final particles in Chinese. In imperatives, it softens commands into suggestions: 吃吧 (go ahead and eat), 开始吧 (let's begin), 坐吧 (please sit). The first-person plural subject 我们 makes it explicitly inclusive. Without 吧, 我们走 is a flat statement ('we go'); with 吧, it becomes a warm invitation or suggestion.

items.question: 不要_____手机了，该睡觉了。
items.translation: (Stop playing with your phone, it's time to sleep.)
items.explanation: 不要 (búyào) + verb + 了 = 'stop [doing something].' 不要玩手机了 = stop playing with your phone. The 了 here marks a change — stopping an ongoing activity. Without 了, 不要玩手机 means 'don't play with your phone' (general prohibition). With 了, it specifically addresses the current activity. 该...了 (gāi...le) means 'it's time to...' — 该睡觉了 = time to sleep. This combination (stop + time for) is very common in parenting and daily reminders.

items.question: 你去把那本书_____过来。
items.translation: (Go bring that book over here.)
items.explanation: 拿过来 (ná guòlái) = bring over — verb + directional complement. The 过来 indicates movement toward the speaker. Imperatives often chain actions: 去 (go) + 把...拿过来 (bring...over). Directional complements are essential in imperatives: 拿过来 (bring here), 拿过去 (take there), 拿出来 (take out), 拿进去 (take inside). The 把 construction specifies exactly what object to handle. This type of specific, multi-action imperative is extremely common in Chinese.

items.question: 大家_____注意听！
items.translation: (Everyone pay attention and listen!)
items.explanation: 请注意 (qǐng zhùyì) = please pay attention — a formal imperative used in announcements, classrooms, and public settings. 大家 (dàjiā) = everyone, addressing a group. 注意听 chains two verbs: pay attention + listen. In formal Chinese imperatives, 请 + specific action verb is the standard pattern. Public announcements often use: 请注意安全 (please pay attention to safety), 请保持安静 (please maintain silence). These formal patterns are important for understanding public Chinese.

items.question: 千万_____忘了带护照！
items.translation: (Whatever you do, don't forget to bring your passport!)
items.explanation: 千万别 (qiān wàn bié) = whatever you do, don't — an emphatic negative imperative. 千万 literally means 'ten million' and functions as an intense emphasis word meaning 'absolutely/by all means.' 千万别忘了 = absolutely don't forget. Similarly: 千万别迟到 (absolutely don't be late), 千万小心 (be extremely careful). 千万 elevates any imperative to critical importance. It's used when the consequences of not following the instruction would be serious.

items.question: 你先_____一下，我马上来。
items.translation: (Wait a moment first, I'll be right there.)
items.explanation: Verb + 一下 (yíxià) is the gentlest imperative form, meaning 'do [something] briefly.' 等一下 = wait a moment. 一下 literally means 'one time' but functions to soften commands into casual, friendly requests. Common patterns: 看一下 (take a look), 试一下 (try it), 说一下 (tell me briefly). Adding 先 (xiān, 'first') suggests sequence: do this first, then something else will happen. 马上 (mǎshàng) = immediately/right away.

items.question: 谁都不_____动！
items.translation: (Nobody move!)
items.explanation: 不许 (bùxǔ) = not allowed/forbidden — a strong prohibition. 谁都不许动 = nobody is allowed to move. 不许 is more forceful than 别 and implies authority and consequences. Compare prohibition strength: 别 (don't) < 不要 (do not) < 不许 (not allowed) < 不准 (forbidden) < 禁止 (prohibited — official). 谁都 (shéi dōu) = everyone/nobody (depending on positive/negative context). In negative sentences, 谁都不 = nobody. This pattern is used in security, emergency, and authority contexts.

items.question: 赶快把作业_____完！
items.translation: (Hurry up and finish your homework!)
items.explanation: 赶快 (gǎnkuài) = hurry up/quickly — an urgency adverb stronger than 快. 把作业做完 = finish (doing) the homework. The resultative complement 完 (wán) after the verb indicates completion. 赶快 + 把 + object + verb + complement is a very direct, urgent imperative pattern. Parents commonly say: 赶快吃完饭 (hurry and finish eating), 赶快穿好衣服 (hurry and get dressed). The 把 construction ensures the object is fully dealt with.

items.question: 你最好_____去医院看看。
items.translation: (You'd better go see a doctor at the hospital.)
items.explanation: 你最好还是 (nǐ zuìhǎo háishi) = you'd better (it would be best if you) — the advisory imperative. 最好 means 'best' and creates a strong suggestion rather than a direct command. 还是 adds a nuance of 'after considering alternatives.' Summary of imperative strength in Chinese: 请...吧 (polite request) → verb + 一下 (gentle suggestion) → 最好/应该 (advisory) → direct verb (neutral command) → 快/赶快 (urgent) → 别/不要 (prohibition) → 不许/不准 (strict prohibition) → 千万别 (emphatic prohibition).

name: 不 vs 没 (Bù vs Méi)

items.question: 我今天_____想吃饭。
items.translation: (I don't feel like eating today.)
items.explanation: 不 (bù) negates willingness, desire, or habitual actions. 不想 = don't want to/don't feel like. 不 is used for: (1) present/future negation, (2) subjective unwillingness, (3) habitual negation, (4) negating adjectives. Compare: 我不想吃 (I don't want to eat — choice) vs 我没吃 (I didn't eat — fact). This distinction between 不 (subjective/volitional) and 没 (objective/factual) is one of the most important grammar points in Chinese.

items.question: 他昨天_____来上课。
items.translation: (He didn't come to class yesterday.)
items.explanation: 没 (méi) negates completed past actions — factual negation of something that didn't happen. 没来 = didn't come. When a specific past time (昨天) is mentioned and you're stating a fact about what happened (or didn't), use 没. Key rule: 没 + verb = didn't do [verb]. Never combine 没 with 了 for simple past negation — 没来 is correct, not *没来了. However, 没 can combine with 过: 没来过 = has never come.

items.question: 我_____是中国人。
items.translation: (I am not Chinese.)
items.explanation: 不 negates the verb 是 (shì, to be) — always 不是, never *没是. This is an absolute rule in Chinese. 不 is the only negator used with 是: 不是学生 (not a student), 不是我的 (not mine), 不是这样 (not like this). Similarly, 不 is always used with adjectives: 不好 (not good), 不大 (not big), 不贵 (not expensive). These patterns reflect 不's role as the subjective/state negator versus 没's role as the factual/event negator.

items.question: 这本书我还_____看完。
items.translation: (I still haven't finished reading this book.)
items.explanation: 还没 (hái méi) = still haven't — expressing an incomplete action. 还没看完 = still haven't finished reading. 还没 implies the action is expected to happen eventually but hasn't yet. It's a very common pattern: 还没吃 (haven't eaten yet), 还没到 (haven't arrived yet), 还没准备好 (haven't prepared yet). Note: 还不 (hái bù) exists but means something different — 'still don't/won't': 他还不来 = he still won't come (refusal).

items.question: 她_____喜欢吃辣的。
items.translation: (She doesn't like eating spicy food.)
items.explanation: 不 negates psychological verbs and preferences: 不喜欢 (don't like), 不想 (don't want), 不愿意 (unwilling), 不知道 (don't know), 不认识 (don't recognize). These all express states of mind, which are negated with 不, not 没. Compare: 她不喜欢辣的 (she doesn't like spicy — permanent preference) vs 她没吃辣的 (she didn't eat spicy — specific past event). Understanding this subjective vs objective distinction is key to mastering Chinese negation.

items.question: 你怎么_____告诉我？
items.translation: (Why didn't you tell me?)
items.explanation: 没 negates a specific past action that should have happened but didn't. 没告诉我 = didn't tell me (a factual past event). The question word 怎么 (zěnme) here means 'how come/why' (expressing surprise or complaint). Compare: 你怎么不告诉我 (why don't/won't you tell me — asking about unwillingness) vs 你怎么没告诉我 (why didn't you tell me — asking about a past omission). Both are grammatically correct but carry different meanings and temporal references.

items.question: 明天我_____去上班。
items.translation: (I'm not going to work tomorrow.)
items.explanation: 不 is used for future negation — expressing a plan or decision not to do something. 不去 = won't go/am not going. When talking about future actions, always use 不: 明天不来 (won't come tomorrow), 下周不上课 (no class next week). 没 cannot negate future actions because it only deals with completed or factual events. This temporal distinction helps clarify: 不 = present/future + subjective; 没 = past + objective.

items.question: 这里的冬天_____太冷。
items.translation: (The winters here are not too cold.)
items.explanation: 不 negates adjectives — always 不, never 没. 不太冷 = not too cold. 不 + adjective describes a state: 不大 (not big), 不远 (not far), 不难 (not difficult), 不贵 (not expensive). This is because adjectives in Chinese function as stative verbs (describing conditions), and 不 is the negator for states and qualities. Important exception: 没那么冷 (not that cold) uses 没 because it's negating a comparison (没有那么), not the adjective directly.

items.question: 我_____有钱买那辆车。
items.translation: (I don't have money to buy that car.)
items.explanation: 没有 (méiyǒu) = don't have — 有 (yǒu) is uniquely negated with 没, never 不. 没有钱 = don't have money. This is the one verb that breaks the subjective/objective pattern: regardless of tense or willingness, 有 always takes 没. 没有 can be shortened to 没 in casual speech. Examples: 没有时间 (don't have time), 没有问题 (no problem), 没有人 (nobody). Note: 没有 also serves as the negative comparison word: 没有他高 (not as tall as him).

items.question: 他_____会说法语。
items.translation: (He can't speak French.)
items.explanation: 不 negates ability and skill verbs: 不会 = can't/doesn't know how to. 会 (huì) = can/know how to. 不会说法语 = can't speak French. Other ability negations: 不能 (cannot — permission/circumstance), 不可以 (may not — permission). Compare: 他不会游泳 (he can't swim — never learned) vs 他没游泳 (he didn't swim — specific instance). The 不 vs 没 choice reveals whether you're talking about ability/state or specific actions.

items.question: 你_____听懂他说的话吗？
items.translation: (Did you understand what he said?)
items.explanation: 没听懂 = didn't understand (a specific instance). When asking about a completed event or result, use 没. 听懂 (tīngdǒng) = listen-understand, a resultative verb compound. In questions about past events, 没 is used: 你没听懂吗？ (didn't you understand?). Compare: 你不听懂 is grammatically odd because 听懂 is a result, not an ongoing state. With resultative complements (verb + result), 没 is the default negator because results are factual events.

items.question: 这个字我_____认识。
items.translation: (I don't recognize this character.)
items.explanation: 不认识 = don't recognize — a state of not knowing. 认识 (rènshi) = to know/recognize. Since this describes an ongoing state (the character is unfamiliar), use 不. Compare: 我不认识他 (I don't know him — ongoing state) vs 我没认识他 is unnatural (认识 as a state verb resists 没). However, 我们还没认识 (we haven't met yet) is acceptable because it refers to the uncompleted event of meeting. Context determines whether a verb acts as state or event.

items.question: 我已经很久_____见到他了。
items.translation: (I haven't seen him for a long time.)
items.explanation: 没 + verb + 了 here expresses a continuing state from the past. 很久没见了 = haven't seen for a long time. This is a special usage where 了 is a sentence-final particle indicating a changed situation, not a completed action. 很久没...了 is a very common pattern: 很久没吃了 (haven't eaten in a while), 很久没联系了 (haven't been in touch for a long time). The combination of 没 (negation of occurrence) + 了 (current relevance) creates the present perfect meaning.

items.question: 你_____应该这样做。
items.translation: (You shouldn't do it this way.)
items.explanation: 不 negates modal/auxiliary verbs: 不应该 = shouldn't, 不能 = can't, 不可以 = may not, 不会 = won't/can't, 不想 = don't want to, 不愿意 = unwilling. All modal verbs take 不 because they express states of obligation, ability, willingness, or permission — all subjective qualities. This is a reliable rule: if the verb expresses modality, use 不. Only the result or actual occurrence of the action takes 没: 没做 (didn't do), but 不应该做 (shouldn't do).

items.question: 我_____觉得这个主意好。
items.translation: (I don't think this idea is good.)
items.explanation: 不觉得 = don't think/feel — subjective opinion. 觉得 (juéde) = to think/feel, an opinion verb always negated with 不. Summary of 不 vs 没 rules: 不 negates: adjectives, 是, psychological verbs (想/喜欢/觉得), modal verbs (会/能/应该), habitual actions, future plans, willingness. 没 negates: completed past actions, 有, ongoing durations (很久没...). Key test: Is it a fact about what happened (没) or a state/attitude/quality (不)? Mastering this distinction is a milestone in Chinese fluency.

name: 就 vs 才 (Jiù vs Cái)

items.question: 他八点_____到了。
items.translation: (He arrived as early as 8 o'clock.)
items.explanation: 就 (jiù) before a verb indicates something happened earlier, faster, or more easily than expected. 八点就到了 = arrived as early as 8 (implying it was early). 就 expresses the speaker's feeling that the action was prompt or quick. Compare: 他八点才到 = He didn't arrive until 8 (implying it was late). This subjective evaluation is the core difference between 就 and 才 — same event, opposite attitudes. 就 pairs with 了: 就到了 (quickly arrived).

items.question: 他十一点_____到。
items.translation: (He didn't arrive until 11 o'clock.)
items.explanation: 才 (cái) indicates something happened later, slower, or with more difficulty than expected. 十一点才到 = didn't arrive until 11 (implying it was late). Key grammar difference: 才 does NOT take 了 after the verb — 才到 is correct, not *才到了. This is a fundamental rule: 就 + verb + 了; 才 + verb (no 了). Why? Because 就 emphasizes completion (hence 了), while 才 emphasizes delay/difficulty, and the delayed result is not viewed as 'completed' in the speaker's mind.

items.question: 我一回家_____开始做作业了。
items.translation: (I started doing homework as soon as I got home.)
items.explanation: 一...就... (yī...jiù...) = 'as soon as...then...' — one of the most important Chinese grammar patterns. 一回家就开始 = as soon as [I] got home, [I] started. 一 marks the trigger event, 就 marks the immediate consequence. More examples: 一下雨就冷 (as soon as it rains, it gets cold), 一说就懂 (understand as soon as it's explained). This pattern emphasizes immediate, automatic connection between events. It's widely used in both spoken and written Chinese.

items.question: 学了三年_____能听懂中文新闻。
items.translation: (Only after studying for three years could [I] understand Chinese news.)
items.explanation: 才 after a duration/condition emphasizes that it took a long time or great effort to achieve something. 学了三年才能听懂 = only after 3 years of study could [I] understand. This implies difficulty and a long process. If we used 就: 学了三年就能听懂 = after only 3 years, [I] could understand (implying it was fast/easy). Same fact, opposite evaluations. 才 with conditions: 吃了药才好 (only after taking medicine did [I] get better).

items.question: 他很聪明，什么都一学_____会。
items.translation: (He's very smart, he learns everything as soon as he tries.)
items.explanation: 一学就会 = learns as soon as he tries — using the 一...就... pattern to emphasize quickness and ease. 就 here conveys that the result comes effortlessly. This pattern is common in describing talent or aptitude: 一看就懂 (understands just by looking), 一听就明白 (gets it immediately upon hearing). The 什么都 (shénme dōu) = 'everything' reinforces the pattern. 就 in these contexts celebrates speed and natural ability.

items.question: 你怎么现在_____来？
items.translation: (How come you're only coming now?)
items.explanation: 才 expresses 'only now/only then' — lateness with implied criticism. 现在才来 = only coming now (you're late!). 怎么...才 is a common complaint pattern. Compare: 你怎么现在就来了 = How come you're already here now (you're early!). The 才 vs 就 choice in questions reveals the speaker's attitude: 才 suggests disapproval of lateness; 就 suggests surprise at earliness. This attitudinal distinction makes 才/就 essential for expressing feelings in Chinese.

items.question: 我们明天_____出发吧。
items.translation: (Let's set off tomorrow then.)
items.explanation: 就 in future/planning contexts means 'then/in that case' — indicating a decided course of action. 明天就出发 = let's depart tomorrow (it's decided). This is 就's function as a decision marker or logical connector. Examples: 那就这样吧 (then let's leave it at that), 我们就在这里吃吧 (let's just eat here then). 就 makes the plan feel natural and resolved, as if the decision flows logically from the circumstances. This usage is extremely common in daily planning conversations.

items.question: 他_____是不肯说。
items.translation: (He just refuses to say.)
items.explanation: 就是 + verb/不verb expresses stubbornness or insistence — 'just/simply.' 就是不肯说 = simply refuses to say. This usage of 就 emphasizes determination or stubbornness: 就是不去 (just won't go), 就是要买 (insists on buying), 就是不信 (simply won't believe). 就是 here is different from 就是 meaning 'that is/namely.' Context distinguishes: before a verb phrase, it means 'insistently/simply'; before a noun, it means 'is precisely.' This emphatic 就 is very common in arguments and emotional speech.

items.question: 等大家都到了，我们_____开始。
items.translation: (Once everyone has arrived, we'll start.)
items.explanation: 才 after a condition means 'only then/not until' — the action won't happen before the condition is met. 都到了才开始 = only after everyone arrives will we start. This conditional 才 is essential for setting prerequisites: 做完作业才能看电视 (only after finishing homework can you watch TV), 下雨了才会凉快 (it won't cool down until it rains). Compare: 都到了就开始 = as soon as everyone arrives, we'll start (emphasizing promptness rather than prerequisite).

items.question: 他三岁_____会读书了。
items.translation: (He could already read at age three.)
items.explanation: 就 with age/quantity emphasizes 'as early as' or 'already.' 三岁就会读书了 = could read as early as age 3 (impressive!). The 了 after the verb confirms the pattern: 就 + verb + 了. Compare: 三岁才会读书 = didn't learn to read until age 3 (slow). This age-related usage is common in Chinese storytelling: 他五岁就开始学钢琴了 (started learning piano at just 5), 她二十岁就当经理了 (became a manager at only 20).

items.question: 你_____知道了啊？这么快！
items.translation: (You already know? That fast!)
items.explanation: 就 expressing 'already' with surprise at speed: 就知道了 = already know (so fast!). The follow-up 这么快 confirms the speaker's amazement. 就 pairs naturally with surprise or admiration about speed: 你就做完了？(you're done already?), 他就走了？(he left already?). In questions, 就 implies the speaker finds the speed remarkable. Note the 了 — 就...了 always marks that the rapid event has been completed.

items.question: 想了半天_____想出一个办法。
items.translation: (Thought for a long time before finally coming up with one solution.)
items.explanation: 才 after a duration emphasizes difficulty and effort: 想了半天才想出 = thought for a long time before finally thinking of [one]. 半天 (bàntiān) literally 'half a day' but commonly means 'a long time.' 才 emphasizes the struggle and the hard-won result. In contrast: 想了一会儿就想出... (thought for just a moment and came up with...) emphasizes ease. 才 after effort phrases: 找了好久才找到 (searched for ages before finding), 等了两个小时才来 (waited 2 hours before coming).

items.question: 我_____要两杯咖啡。
items.translation: (I only want two cups of coffee.)
items.explanation: 就 before a quantity means 'only/just' — minimizing the amount. 就要两杯 = only want two cups (it's not much). This 就 indicates sufficiency or smallness: 就三个人 (only three people), 就一块钱 (just one yuan), 就吃了一点儿 (only ate a little). Compare: 才三个人 also means 'only three people' but with stronger disappointment or frustration. Both 就 and 才 can mean 'only' with quantities, but 就 is neutral/content while 才 implies dissatisfaction.

items.question: 这里离学校很近，走路五分钟_____到了。
items.translation: (It's very close to school, just a 5-minute walk.)
items.explanation: 就 emphasizing convenience and speed: 五分钟就到了 = arrive in just 5 minutes (it's quick!). This combines the 'short time' sense with the speed/ease sense. 很近 (very close) sets up the expectation, and 就 confirms it. Compare: 五分钟才到 would mean 'it takes a whole 5 minutes to get there' (implying it's slow/far). Same five minutes, completely different attitudes!

items.question: 你不说我_____不知道呢。
items.translation: (If you hadn't said, I wouldn't have known.)
items.explanation: 还 (hái) here means 'still' — 还不知道 = still wouldn't know. This contrasts with 就 and 才 uses. Summary of 就 vs 才: 就 = earlier/faster/easier than expected (uses 了), expresses satisfaction, surprise at speed, or decisiveness. 才 = later/slower/harder than expected (no 了), expresses frustration, delay, or conditions. Both can mean 'only' with quantities (就 is neutral, 才 is dissatisfied). Master this pair to express nuanced attitudes about timing and expectation in Chinese.

items.question: 你也来_____？
items.translation: (You're coming too?)
items.explanation: 啊 (a) at the end of a question softens the tone and expresses mild surprise or excitement. 你也来啊？ shows pleasant surprise that the listener is also coming. Without 啊, the question would sound more neutral or even blunt. 啊 is one of the most versatile particles — it can express surprise (你也来啊？), agreement (好啊！), or urging (快啊！). Its pronunciation changes based on the preceding vowel: 啊→呀 after i/ü, 啊→哇 after u, 啊→呐 after n.

items.question: 他怎么还没到_____？
items.translation: (Why hasn't he arrived yet?)
items.explanation: 呢 (ne) in questions adds a sense of wondering or concern. 他怎么还没到呢？ expresses that the speaker is genuinely puzzled about the delay. 呢 makes the question feel more personal and engaged rather than just factual. It's commonly used with 怎么 (how/why) questions to emphasize puzzlement. Compare: 还没到 (hasn't arrived — statement) vs 还没到呢 (hasn't arrived yet — with ongoing concern). 呢 also appears in follow-up questions: 你呢？(And you?).

items.question: 我们走_____。
items.translation: (Let's go.)
items.explanation: 吧 (ba) at the end of a statement creates a suggestion or soft command. 我们走吧 means 'let's go' — it invites agreement rather than demanding action. Without 吧, 我们走 would sound abrupt or like a command. 吧 has two main uses: (1) making suggestions (我们走吧 = let's go), and (2) expressing uncertainty/guessing (他是学生吧 = he's a student, I guess). The tone of voice distinguishes these two uses in spoken Chinese.

items.question: 这件事很简单_____！
items.translation: (This matter is very simple [obviously]!)
items.explanation: 嘛 (ma, neutral tone) expresses that something should be obvious. 很简单嘛！ means 'it's simple, obviously!' — the speaker feels the point is self-evident. Don't confuse 嘛 with 吗 (mǎ, question particle). 嘛 often carries a slightly impatient or persuasive tone: 你说嘛 = just say it (coaxing). It can also express reasoning: 他是老师嘛，当然知道 = he's a teacher [after all], of course he knows. 嘛 is more colloquial and common in spoken Chinese.

items.question: 下雨_____，别出去了。
items.translation: (It's raining [you know], don't go out.)
items.explanation: 呢 (ne) in declarative sentences emphasizes an ongoing state. 下雨呢 stresses that it IS raining right now — used to remind or warn the listener of a current situation. This is different from the question use of 呢. In statements, 呢 highlights continuation: 他在睡觉呢 = he's sleeping (right now, don't disturb). It adds a 'pay attention to this fact' nuance. Common pattern: [situation]呢，[advice/consequence]. 下雨呢，带伞吧 = It's raining, bring an umbrella.

items.question: 好_____好_____，我知道了。
items.translation: (OK OK, I got it.)
items.explanation: 了 (le) repeated after 好 creates 好了好了, expressing mild impatience or resignation — 'alright already, I get it.' The doubled 好了 softens what could be an annoyed response. 了 here marks a change of state: the speaker is now agreeing or giving in. This doubling pattern is very natural in colloquial Chinese: 行了行了 (OK OK), 够了够了 (enough enough), 算了算了 (forget it, forget it). It shows the speaker wants to end the discussion.

items.question: 你不信_____？我说的是真的。
items.translation: (You don't believe [me]? What I said is true.)
items.explanation: 啊 (a) in this question context expresses disbelief or challenge. 你不信啊？ means 'you really don't believe me?' with a tone of mild indignation. The speaker is surprised and slightly hurt by the disbelief. Compare the particles in questions: 你不信吗？(neutral question), 你不信啊？(surprised/challenged), 你不信吧？(confirming doubt). 啊 makes the question rhetorical — the speaker expects the listener to believe them.

items.question: 这个菜好吃_____！
items.translation: (This dish is so delicious!)
items.explanation: 极了 (jí le) is a complement meaning 'extremely' — placed after adjectives for strong emphasis. 好吃极了 = extremely delicious. While technically a complement rather than a pure particle, 极了 functions similarly at sentence end. The pattern is: adjective + 极了. Common examples: 高兴极了 (extremely happy), 漂亮极了 (extremely beautiful), 难受极了 (extremely uncomfortable). It's stronger than 很 (very) and comparable to 非常 (extremely) but more colloquial and emphatic.

items.question: 那当然_____！
items.translation: (Of course! / That goes without saying!)
items.explanation: 啦 (la) is a fusion of 了 + 啊, combining the sense of completed/changed state (了) with the exclamatory tone (啊). 那当然啦！ is an enthusiastic 'of course!' — more expressive than 那当然了 (matter-of-fact) or 那当然啊 (surprised agreement). 啦 is very common in casual speech: 好啦 = OK then (with warmth), 来啦 = coming! (cheerful response), 知道啦 = I know already (slightly playful). It adds a friendly, casual energy to any statement.

items.question: 你是日本人_____？
items.translation: (You're Japanese, right?)
items.explanation: 吧 (ba) in a question signals that the speaker is making an educated guess and seeking confirmation. 你是日本人吧？ means 'you're Japanese, right? (I think so).' It's softer than 吗 (neutral question) and implies the speaker already has an assumption. Compare: 你是日本人吗？(no assumption, genuine question) vs 你是日本人吧？(I think you are, confirm please). This guessing function of 吧 is essential in Chinese — it shows politeness by not being too direct.

items.question: 算_____，我不想争了。
items.translation: (Forget it, I don't want to argue anymore.)
items.explanation: 算了 (suàn le) is a fixed expression meaning 'forget it / never mind.' 了 here marks a change of decision — the speaker is now giving up or letting go. This is one of the most useful 了 patterns in daily Chinese. Similar expressions: 别说了 (stop talking about it), 不要了 (don't want it anymore), 走了 (leaving now). In each case, 了 signals that something has changed — a new decision, a completed action, or a shift in the situation.

items.question: 快点_____，要迟到了！
items.translation: (Hurry up, we're going to be late!)
items.explanation: 啊 (a) after an imperative adds urgency. 快点啊！ is more pressing than plain 快点, as if saying 'hurry, come on!' The urgency is friendly rather than harsh. Without 啊, 快点 is a straightforward command; with 啊, it conveys emotional involvement — the speaker is worried about being late and urging the listener. Similar patterns: 小心啊 (be careful!), 加油啊 (come on, keep going!), 别忘了啊 (don't forget!). 啊 makes commands feel more human and caring.

items.question: 你说得_____对_____。
items.translation: (What you said is indeed right.)
items.explanation: 挺...的 (tǐng...de) is a sentence pattern that frames the adjective to mean 'quite/rather [adjective].' 你说得挺对的 = what you said is quite right. The 的 at the end is essential — it completes the pattern and softens the statement. Without 的, 挺对 sounds incomplete. This pattern expresses moderate agreement or assessment: 挺好的 (quite good), 挺难的 (rather difficult), 挺有意思的 (quite interesting). It's less emphatic than 很 but more conversational.

items.question: 你猜他去哪儿_____？
items.translation: (Guess where he went?)
items.explanation: 了 (le) at the end of this question indicates a completed action — he already went somewhere. 他去哪儿了？ = where did he go (already)? The 了 is essential here for past reference. Without it, 他去哪儿 could mean 'where is he going?' (present/future). With 你猜 (you guess) at the beginning, the sentence becomes playful — the speaker knows the answer and is teasing the listener. Compare: 你去哪儿了？(where did you go?) vs 你去哪儿？(where are you going?).

items.question: 这不是很好_____！
items.translation: (Isn't this great! / This is great!)
items.explanation: 嘛 (ma) after a rhetorical statement emphasizes that the point is obvious. 这不是很好嘛！ = isn't this great (obviously it is)! The negative question 不是...嘛 is actually a strong affirmative — a rhetorical device. The speaker is pointing out something the listener should already recognize. This pattern is very common in persuasion: 你不是喜欢他嘛 = you DO like him (don't deny it). 嘛 reinforces the 'this is obvious' attitude and makes the statement feel like friendly reasoning.

items.question: 我听_____了，请再说一遍。
items.translation: (I didn't hear clearly, please say it again.)
items.explanation: 听不清楚 means 'cannot hear clearly' — 清楚 (qīngchu, clear) is the resultative complement showing the intended result of hearing. The 不 between the verb and complement creates the 'unable to achieve result' pattern. Compare: 听清楚了 (heard clearly), 听不清楚 (can't hear clearly). Other common complements with 清楚: 看清楚 (see clearly), 说清楚 (explain clearly), 写清楚 (write clearly). The complement describes the result of the action, not the manner.

items.question: 这本书我看_____了。
items.translation: (I've finished reading this book.)
items.explanation: 完 (wán) as a resultative complement means 'finish/complete.' 看完了 = finished reading. This is one of the most common resultative compounds. The pattern is: verb + 完 + 了. Examples: 吃完了 (finished eating), 做完了 (finished doing), 写完了 (finished writing). The 了 after 完 confirms the action is completed. Without 了, 看完 just means 'finish reading' as a concept. Question form: 你看完了吗？(Have you finished reading?).

items.question: 钥匙找_____了！在桌子上。
items.translation: (Found the key! It's on the table.)
items.explanation: 到 (dào) as a resultative complement means 'reach/achieve the goal.' 找到了 = found (successfully reached the goal of searching). 到 implies the action achieved its target. Common combinations: 买到 (managed to buy), 收到 (received), 看到 (saw/spotted), 听到 (heard/caught), 想到 (thought of/came up with). Negative form: 找不到 = can't find. The difference between 到 and 见: 看到 emphasizes achieving the result; 看见 is more about the visual perception.

items.question: 那个字写_____了，请改一下。
items.translation: (That character was written wrong, please fix it.)
items.explanation: 错 (cuò) as a resultative complement means 'wrongly.' 写错了 = wrote incorrectly. The complement shows the unintended result of the action. Common 错 compounds: 说错 (said wrong), 听错 (heard wrong/misheard), 做错 (did wrong), 买错 (bought the wrong one), 走错 (went the wrong way). This is very practical for daily corrections. Negative potential: 不会写错 = won't write it wrong. Already happened: 写错了 (wrote it wrong already).

items.question: 饭做_____了，可以吃了。
items.translation: (The food is ready, we can eat now.)
items.explanation: 好 (hǎo) as a resultative complement means 'properly completed/ready.' 做好了 = finished making (and it's ready). While 完 just means 'finished,' 好 adds the nuance of 'done well/properly prepared.' Compare: 做完了 (finished cooking — might not be good) vs 做好了 (cooking is done and ready to serve). Other examples: 准备好了 (prepared/ready), 想好了 (thought it through), 安排好了 (arranged properly). 好 implies satisfactory completion.

items.question: 我没听_____他说什么。
items.translation: (I didn't hear what he said.)
items.explanation: 见 (jiàn) as a resultative complement means 'perceive/sense.' 听见 = hear (perceive sound). 没听见 = didn't hear. 见 is specifically used with sensory verbs: 看见 (see), 听见 (hear). The difference between 听见 and 听到: 听见 emphasizes the sensory perception (the sound reached your ears), while 听到 emphasizes achieving the result of listening. In practice, they're often interchangeable, but 听见 is more colloquial and 听到 slightly more formal.

items.question: 孩子们跑_____了。
items.translation: (The children ran away.)
items.explanation: 走 (zǒu) as a resultative complement means 'away.' 跑走了 = ran away. The complement describes the result of running — the person left/disappeared. Common 走 compounds: 拿走 (took away), 带走 (brought/carried away), 开走 (drove away), 飞走 (flew away), 搬走 (moved away). 走 as a complement always implies departure or removal from the current location. Pattern: verb + 走 + 了 for completed departure.

items.question: 杯子摔_____了。
items.translation: (The cup broke from falling.)
items.explanation: 碎 (suì) as a resultative complement means 'shattered/broken into pieces.' 摔碎了 = dropped and shattered. The complement tells us the specific result of the dropping — it broke into fragments. Compare: 摔坏了 (dropped and broke/damaged — may not be shattered), 摔碎了 (dropped and shattered — broken into pieces). Other 碎 compounds: 打碎 (hit and shattered), 撕碎 (tore to shreds). Chinese is very precise about describing the type of breakage.

items.question: 请把门关_____。
items.translation: (Please close the door tight/properly.)
items.explanation: 关好 means 'close properly/securely.' 好 as a complement here ensures the door is fully and properly closed. Compare: 关上 (close it — action focus), 关好 (close properly — result focus), 关住 (close and keep closed — maintain state). In this context, 把门关好 is a polite request to make sure the door is properly shut. The 把 construction with resultative complements is very natural — 把 + object + verb + complement: 把窗户关好 (close the window properly).

items.question: 你看_____那个人是谁了吗？
items.translation: (Did you see clearly who that person is?)
items.explanation: 看清 (kàn qīng) means 'see clearly.' 看清了 = saw clearly/identified. 清 as a complement means the action achieved clarity. Compare the subtle differences: 看见 (saw — perceived), 看到 (saw — achieved seeing), 看清 (saw clearly — identified details). Other 清 compounds: 听清 (heard clearly), 问清 (asked and clarified), 搞清 (figured out/clarified). 清 can be expanded to 清楚: 看清楚 = see very clearly. This is indispensable for police reports, witness accounts, or just daily life.

items.question: 衣服洗_____了。
items.translation: (The clothes are washed clean.)
items.explanation: 干净 (gānjìng) as a resultative complement means 'clean.' 洗干净了 = washed clean. Two-syllable complements like 干净 are common: 擦干净 (wiped clean), 打扫干净 (cleaned up thoroughly), 收拾干净 (tidied up clean). The complement tells us the result state of the object after the action. Compare: 洗完了 (finished washing — might still be dirty) vs 洗干净了 (washed and now clean). This distinction is practical and important in daily Chinese.

items.question: 他想_____了一个好办法。
items.translation: (He thought of a good solution.)
items.explanation: 出 (chū) as a resultative complement means 'out/forth' — indicating emergence of a result. 想出了 = thought up/came up with. The idea 'came out' from thinking. Common 出 compounds: 看出 (figured out by looking), 说出 (spoke out/expressed), 做出 (produced/made), 找出 (found out/discovered), 提出 (raised/proposed). 出 always implies something emerging or being produced. Compare: 想到 (thought of — it came to mind) vs 想出 (came up with — actively produced the idea).

items.question: 雨把衣服淋_____了。
items.translation: (The rain got the clothes wet.)
items.explanation: 湿 (shī) as a resultative complement means 'wet.' 淋湿了 = got wet from rain. The 把 construction (雨把衣服淋湿了) shows that the rain caused the clothes to become wet. Common 湿 compounds: 弄湿 (made wet), 打湿 (splashed wet). Related moisture complements: 淋透了 (soaked through — more extreme), 淋湿了 (got wet — general). Chinese resultative compounds are remarkably precise about describing the exact state change caused by actions.

items.question: 这道题太难了，我做不_____。
items.translation: (This problem is too hard, I can't solve it.)
items.explanation: 做不出来 means 'cannot produce/solve.' 出来 as a directional complement in the potential form indicates the result 'coming out' — the answer cannot emerge. The negative potential form: verb + 不 + complement. Compare: 做出来了 (solved it), 做不出来 (can't solve it), 做得出来 (can solve it — positive potential). 出来 with cognition verbs: 想不出来 (can't think of it), 看不出来 (can't tell/figure out), 说不出来 (can't express it).

items.question: 把灯打_____，太暗了。
items.translation: (Turn on the light, it's too dark.)
items.explanation: 开 (kāi) as a resultative complement means 'open/on.' 打开 = turn on/open. This is one of the most essential compounds in daily Chinese. 打开灯 (turn on the light), 打开门 (open the door), 打开窗户 (open the window), 打开书 (open the book). The opposite is 关上: 关上灯 (turn off the light). Other 开 compounds: 拉开 (pull open), 推开 (push open), 拆开 (take apart/unwrap), 解开 (untie/unlock). 开 always implies separating or activating.

items.question: 你_____按时交作业。
items.translation: (You must submit homework on time.)
items.explanation: 必须 (bìxū) means 'must/have to' — the strongest obligation word in Chinese. 你必须按时交作业 = you must submit homework on time. 必须 implies there's no choice — it's mandatory. Compare the obligation scale: 必须 (must) > 应该 (should) > 最好 (had better). 必须 is used for rules, laws, and non-negotiable requirements. Negative: 不必 (don't need to) — note it's NOT 不必须. 不必 is a softer negation meaning 'no need,' while 不用 is even more colloquial.

items.question: 学生_____遵守学校的规定。
items.translation: (Students should abide by school rules.)
items.explanation: 应该 (yīnggāi) means 'should/ought to.' It expresses moral obligation or reasonable expectation, softer than 必须. 学生应该遵守规定 = students should follow rules. 应该 implies it's the right thing to do but doesn't force compliance. Other uses: 应该有 (there should be), 应该知道 (should know), 应该没问题 (should be no problem — expressing expectation/probability). Synonym: 应当 (yīngdāng) — more formal and often used in written Chinese or official contexts.

items.question: 今天很冷，你_____多穿点衣服。
items.translation: (It's cold today, you'd better wear more clothes.)
items.explanation: 最好 (zuìhǎo) means 'had better/it's best to.' It gives strong advice rather than a strict requirement. 你最好多穿点 = you'd better wear more. 最好 is a gentle way to express what someone ought to do — it's a recommendation, not a command. Pattern: subject + 最好 + verb phrase. Examples: 你最好早点儿来 (you'd better come early), 最好别去 (better not go), 我们最好准备一下 (we'd better prepare). It's very useful for giving friendly advice.

items.question: 开车的时候_____系安全带。
items.translation: (When driving, one must fasten the seatbelt.)
items.explanation: 得 (děi) means 'have to/must' in colloquial Chinese. It's the spoken equivalent of 必须 but less formal. 开车得系安全带 = when driving you have to wear a seatbelt. 得 is pronounced děi (third tone) when meaning 'must' — different from 得 (de) as a structural particle or 得 (dé) meaning 'get.' Negative form: 不用 (don't need to) — NOT 不得 (which means 'cannot/must not' and is a different word). 得 is extremely common in daily conversation: 我得走了 (I gotta go).

items.question: 你不_____担心，一切都会好的。
items.translation: (You don't need to worry, everything will be fine.)
items.explanation: 不用 (bùyòng) means 'don't need to/no need.' It negates obligation — telling someone they're free from a duty. 不用担心 = no need to worry. 不用 is the most natural way to release someone from obligation in spoken Chinese. Compare: 不用 (no need — colloquial), 不必 (unnecessary — slightly formal), 不需要 (not needed — neutral). Polite use: 不用谢 (no need to thank me), 不用客气 (no need to be polite). Pattern: 不用 + verb.

items.question: 我们_____尊重老人。
items.translation: (We ought to respect the elderly.)
items.explanation: 应当 (yīngdāng) is the formal equivalent of 应该, meaning 'ought to/should.' 我们应当尊重老人 has a more formal, written tone — suitable for essays, speeches, or official contexts. In daily conversation, 应该 is preferred. Compare: 应当 (formal written) ≈ 应该 (general use). Legal/official Chinese frequently uses 应当: 公民应当遵守法律 (citizens ought to obey the law). Both carry moral obligation but 应当 sounds more authoritative.

items.question: 明天_____要下雨，带把伞吧。
items.translation: (It's probably going to rain tomorrow, bring an umbrella.)
items.explanation: 可能 (kěnéng) means 'possibly/might.' 明天可能要下雨 = it might rain tomorrow. While not expressing obligation directly, 可能 + suggestion is a common pattern for soft advice. Compare probability words: 一定 (definitely) > 肯定 (certainly) > 可能 (possibly) > 也许 (perhaps) > 不一定 (not necessarily). 可能 can be an adjective (是可能的 = is possible), adverb (可能下雨 = might rain), or noun (有可能 = there's a possibility).

items.question: 这件事你_____得自己做。
items.translation: (This matter — you definitely have to do it yourself.)
items.explanation: 一定 (yīdìng) means 'definitely/certainly must.' 你一定得自己做 = you absolutely must do it yourself. 一定 adds emphasis to obligation — it strengthens whatever follows. Note: 一定 can combine with 得(děi): 一定得 = absolutely must. 一定 alone can express strong prediction: 明天一定会好 (tomorrow will definitely be fine) or firm insistence: 你一定要来 (you absolutely must come). It's versatile — marking certainty, insistence, or strong obligation.

items.question: 这里不_____抽烟。
items.translation: (Smoking is not allowed here.)
items.explanation: 不许 (bùxǔ) means 'not allowed/prohibited.' 不许抽烟 = smoking is not allowed. 许 (xǔ) means 'permit,' so 不许 is 'not permitted.' It's a direct prohibition, stronger than 不要 (don't). Compare prohibition strength: 不许 (forbidden) > 不准 (not permitted) > 不要 (don't) > 别 (don't — casual). 不许 sounds authoritative — used by parents, teachers, signs: 不许停车 (no parking), 不许拍照 (no photography). In contrast, 别 is friendly: 别担心 (don't worry).

items.question: 你_____要注意安全。
items.translation: (You must pay attention to safety.)
items.explanation: 一定要 (yīdìng yào) means 'must/absolutely should.' 你一定要注意安全 = you must pay attention to safety. The 一定 + 要 combination is the emphatic imperative — it conveys the speaker's strong insistence. Compare: 要注意 (should pay attention), 一定要注意 (absolutely must pay attention). This pattern is especially common when parents or friends express concern: 一定要小心 (be very careful), 一定要吃饭 (make sure to eat). It carries warmth along with insistence.

items.question: 你不_____每天都来。
items.translation: (You don't have to come every day.)
items.explanation: 不必 (bùbì) means 'don't need to/not necessary' — slightly more formal than 不用. 你不必每天都来 = you don't need to come every day. 不必 negates 必须 but is softer — it releases obligation politely. Compare: 不必 (not necessary — polite), 不用 (no need — casual), 不需要 (not required — neutral/official). 不必 is often used to reassure: 不必紧张 (no need to be nervous), 不必客气 (no need for formality), 不必担心 (no need to worry).

items.question: 这个问题_____马上解决。
items.translation: (This problem needs to be solved immediately.)
items.explanation: 需要 (xūyào) means 'need to/require.' 这个问题需要马上解决 = this problem needs immediate resolution. 需要 is neutral and objective — it states a requirement without emotional pressure. Compare: 需要 (need — factual), 必须 (must — commanding), 应该 (should — advisory). 需要 can also be a noun: 满足需要 (meet needs). As a verb: 你需要休息 (you need rest). It's professional and non-confrontational, making it ideal for workplace communication.

items.question: 做人_____诚实。
items.translation: (One should be honest in life.)
items.explanation: 要 (yào) here means 'should/must' — expressing general moral obligation. 做人要诚实 = in being a person, one should be honest. This is a proverb-like usage of 要 for life principles. 要 is the most versatile obligation word: it can mean 'want' (我要水 = I want water), 'will' (要下雨 = it will rain), or 'should/must' (要努力 = must work hard). Context determines meaning. In moral/general truths, 要 = should: 做事要认真 (one should be serious in work).

items.question: 你不_____这么早离开。
items.translation: (You don't have to leave this early.)
items.explanation: 不用 (bùyòng) here reassures someone that early departure isn't necessary. 你不用这么早离开 = you don't need to leave so early. This is a hospitable expression — the speaker wants the listener to stay longer. 不用 is the go-to phrase for releasing someone from perceived obligations in social situations: 不用急 (no need to rush), 不用换鞋 (no need to change shoes — at someone's home), 不用带礼物 (no need to bring gifts). It creates a welcoming, pressure-free atmosphere.

items.question: 员工_____准时上班。
items.translation: (Employees are required to arrive at work on time.)
items.explanation: 须 (xū) means 'must/required to' — formal and typically found in written rules or official contexts. 员工须准时上班 is a rule-style statement. 须 is short for 必须 and appears in regulations: 旅客须持票入场 (passengers must hold tickets to enter). Compare formality: 须 (official document) > 必须 (strong spoken) > 得(děi) (casual spoken). You'll see 须 on signs, in contracts, and in workplace policies. It's not used in casual conversation but essential to understand for reading official Chinese.

items.question: 你_____说中文吗？
items.translation: (Can you speak Chinese?)
items.explanation: 会 (huì) expresses learned ability — skills acquired through study or practice. 你会说中文吗？ = can you speak Chinese (have you learned it)? 会 is used for languages, instruments, sports, cooking, and other learned skills: 会游泳 (can swim), 会开车 (can drive), 会弹钢琴 (can play piano). Compare: 会 (learned skill) vs 能 (physical/circumstantial ability) vs 可以 (permission). A child might say 我不会 (I don't know how) — implying they haven't learned yet.

items.question: 我感冒了，不_____去上班。
items.translation: (I have a cold, I can't go to work.)
items.explanation: 能 (néng) expresses ability based on circumstances, physical capacity, or conditions. 不能去上班 = can't go to work (because of illness — a circumstantial barrier). Compare: 不会开车 (can't drive — never learned) vs 不能开车 (can't drive — injured/drunk/no license). 能 focuses on whether conditions allow the action. Other examples: 你能来吗？(can you come? — are you available?), 我能帮你 (I can help you — I'm in a position to help). 能 is about capacity in context.

items.question: 这里_____停车吗？
items.translation: (Can I park here? / Is parking allowed here?)
items.explanation: 可以 (kěyǐ) expresses permission — asking if something is allowed. 这里可以停车吗？ = is parking allowed here? Compare: 可以 (permitted), 能 (physically/circumstantially possible), 会 (know how to). A driving test question: 你会开车吗？(can you drive? — learned skill). A friend's question: 你能开车吗？(can you drive? — are you available/sober). A parking lot sign: 可以停车 (parking allowed). Negative: 不可以 = not allowed (formal), 不行 = no/won't work (casual).

items.question: 她_____弹一手好钢琴。
items.translation: (She can play piano very well.)
items.explanation: 能 (néng) here emphasizes the degree of ability — she is capable of playing well. While 会弹钢琴 means 'knows how to play piano,' 能弹一手好钢琴 emphasizes the level of skill she can achieve. When describing HOW WELL someone can do something, 能 is preferred over 会. Compare: 会说中文 (can speak Chinese — yes/no), 能说流利的中文 (can speak fluent Chinese — degree). 能 with degree complements: 能跑很快 (can run very fast), 能吃很多 (can eat a lot).

items.question: 他三岁就_____读书了。
items.translation: (He could already read at age three.)
items.explanation: 会 (huì) here describes a learned ability achieved early. 三岁就会读书 = could read by age three. The 就 emphasizes how early this ability was acquired. 会 is the correct choice because reading is a learned skill. Common childhood milestones with 会: 会走路了 (can walk now), 会说话了 (can talk now), 会写字了 (can write now). The pattern 会...了 marks the moment a new ability is acquired: 我终于会游泳了！(I can finally swim!).

items.question: 你的腿好了吗？_____走路了吗？
items.translation: (Is your leg better? Can you walk now?)
items.explanation: 能 (néng) is used for physical ability — asking if recovery allows walking. 能走路了吗？ = can you walk now (is your body able to)? After injury/illness, 能 is always used because the question is about physical capacity, not learned skill. Nobody would ask 会走路吗 (can you walk? — do you know how) to an adult recovering from surgery. Medical context examples: 能吃东西吗？(can you eat?), 能动吗？(can you move?), 能看见吗？(can you see?).

items.question: 我_____帮你搬这个箱子。
items.translation: (I can help you move this box.)
items.explanation: 可以 (kěyǐ) here means 'I'm able/willing to' — offering help. 我可以帮你 = I can help you. When offering to do something, 可以 expresses willingness and availability. Compare offers: 我可以帮你 (I can help — I'm willing/available), 我能帮你 (I can help — I have the ability/capacity). In practice, both work for offers, but 可以 sounds more volunteering while 能 sounds more about capability. Polite offers typically use 可以: 我可以带你去 (I can take you there).

items.question: 这个孩子真聪明，什么都_____学会。
items.translation: (This child is so smart, they can learn everything.)
items.explanation: 能 (néng) expresses inherent capability here. 什么都能学会 = can learn everything (has the capacity). When talking about someone's potential or talent, 能 is preferred. Note the word 学会 (xuéhuì) = learn + master — a resultative compound meaning 'learn successfully.' Compare: 学 (study), 学会 (successfully learn/master). 能学会 = has the ability to master. Pattern with 什么都: 什么都能做 (can do everything), 什么都能吃 (can eat everything) — expressing universal capability.

items.question: 请问，我_____试穿这件衣服吗？
items.translation: (Excuse me, may I try on this piece of clothing?)
items.explanation: 可以 (kěyǐ) is the standard way to ask permission politely. 我可以试穿吗？ = may I try it on? In service situations (shops, restaurants, offices), 可以 is the natural permission word. Common permission requests: 可以进来吗？(may I come in?), 可以坐这儿吗？(may I sit here?), 可以拍照吗？(may I take photos?). The positive response is: 可以 (yes, you may) or 当然可以 (of course). Negative: 不可以 (not allowed) or 对不起，不行 (sorry, no).

items.question: 他不_____做饭。
items.translation: (He can't cook. / He doesn't know how to cook.)
items.explanation: 不会 (bù huì) means 'can't (due to lack of skill).' 不会做饭 = can't cook (never learned). This is one of the most common uses of 会 in the negative. When someone says 不会, they're admitting they lack the knowledge or skill. Useful daily expressions: 不会说英语 (can't speak English), 不会用电脑 (can't use a computer), 不会跳舞 (can't dance). 不会 can also mean 'won't happen': 不会下雨 (it won't rain) — a prediction use that's context-dependent.

items.question: 你一个人_____搬动这张桌子吗？
items.translation: (Can you move this table by yourself?)
