items.explanation: 能 (néng) here asks about physical strength/capacity to move a heavy object. 能搬动吗？uses the resultative complement 动 (dòng, to move/budge) — asking if the person has enough strength to make the table budge. The potential form 搬得动/搬不动 (can/can't move it) is extremely useful: 这个太重了，我搬不动 (this is too heavy, I can't move it). 能 is always used for physical strength questions: 你能举起来吗？(can you lift it?).

items.question: 你_____用筷子吗？
items.translation: (Can you use chopsticks?)
items.explanation: 会 (huì) for using chopsticks — a learned skill. 你会用筷子吗？ = do you know how to use chopsticks? This is a classic 会 question because chopstick use requires practice and learning. A foreign visitor might hear this a lot! Compare: 会用筷子 (knows how — learned), 能用筷子吗 (can you use chopsticks — maybe injured hand?), 可以用筷子吗 (may I use chopsticks — asking permission). The distinction matters: asking a foreigner 能 instead of 会 might imply they're physically incapable!

items.question: 生病的时候不_____喝酒。
items.translation: (You cannot drink alcohol when sick.)
items.explanation: 不能 (bù néng) here means 'cannot/must not' due to medical reasons. 不能喝酒 = cannot drink alcohol (health doesn't allow it). When doctors give prohibitions, 不能 is standard: 不能吃辣的 (can't eat spicy food), 不能运动 (can't exercise), 不能熬夜 (can't stay up late). Compare: 不能 (can't — circumstances prevent it), 不可以 (may not — rules prohibit it), 不要 (don't — advice). Medical 不能 carries the weight of physical necessity.

items.question: 她很有语言天赋，_____说五种语言。
items.translation: (She has great language talent, she can speak five languages.)
items.explanation: 能 (néng) here emphasizes remarkable capability — speaking five languages showcases talent. While 会说五种语言 (knows five languages) is also correct, 能说五种语言 emphasizes her impressive ability level. When highlighting achievements or exceptional skills, 能 is more impactful than 会. Compare: 会说中文 (can speak Chinese — basic fact) vs 能说流利的中文 (can speak fluent Chinese — impressive ability). 能 with quantifiers shows capacity: 能吃三碗饭 (can eat three bowls).

items.question: 等一下你_____走了。
items.translation: (In a moment you can/may leave.)
items.explanation: 可以 (kěyǐ) grants permission — you may leave soon. 你可以走了 = you're free to go now. This is a permission statement from someone in authority (doctor, teacher, boss). 可以...了 indicates that whatever was preventing the action is now resolved: 可以吃了 (you can eat now — food is ready), 可以开始了 (you may begin now — preparation is done), 可以回家了 (you may go home — work/class is over). Adding 等一下 softens it: 'in just a moment' you'll be free.

items.question: 我_____去中国旅行。
items.translation: (I want to travel to China.)
items.explanation: 想 (xiǎng) means 'want to/would like to.' 我想去中国旅行 = I want to travel to China. 想 is the most common and natural way to express desires in Chinese. It's polite and not too strong: 想吃 (want to eat), 想去 (want to go), 想看 (want to see). Compare desire strength: 非常想 (really want to) > 想 (want to) > 有点想 (kind of want to). 想 can also mean 'miss': 我想你 (I miss you) — same word, different meaning determined by context.

items.question: 我_____能早点退休。
items.translation: (I wish I could retire early.)
items.explanation: 希望 (xīwàng) means 'hope/wish' — expressing a desire for something that may or may not happen. 我希望能早点退休 = I hope I can retire early. 希望 is more formal and aspirational than 想. It implies uncertainty about the outcome. Compare: 想 (want — personal desire), 希望 (hope — aspiration with uncertainty). Common patterns: 希望你 (I hope you...), 希望一切顺利 (hope everything goes smoothly). 希望 can be a noun too: 他是我们的希望 (he is our hope).

items.question: 我_____要一杯咖啡。
items.translation: (I want a cup of coffee.)
items.explanation: 要 (yào) means 'want' — more direct and immediate than 想. 我要一杯咖啡 = I want a cup of coffee (ordering). 要 is the standard word when ordering food/drinks or making immediate requests. Compare: 我要咖啡 (I want coffee — ordering now, direct), 我想喝咖啡 (I'd like to drink coffee — expressing desire). 要 can sound demanding if overused: 我要！我要！(I want it! I want it!) sounds childish. In ordering contexts, 要 is perfectly polite: 要一个汉堡 (I'll have a hamburger).

items.question: 她_____当医生。
items.translation: (She wishes to become a doctor.)
items.explanation: 想要 (xiǎng yào) means 'want to' — combining 想 (want/wish) and 要 (want/will). 她想要当医生 = she wants to become a doctor. 想要 is slightly stronger and more determined than plain 想: 想去 (would like to go) vs 想要去 (want to go — more determined). 想要 is often used for life goals, career aspirations, and important desires: 想要成功 (want to succeed), 想要学中文 (want to learn Chinese), 想要买房子 (want to buy a house). It signals serious intention.

items.question: 我真_____时间能停下来。
items.translation: (I truly wish time could stop.)
items.explanation: 希望 (xīwàng) here expresses an impossible wish — time can't actually stop, but the speaker wishes it could. 真希望 (zhēn xīwàng) = truly wish — adding 真 intensifies the longing. This is a common pattern for expressing wistful, hypothetical desires: 真希望我会飞 (I really wish I could fly), 真希望没有考试 (I really wish there were no exams). After 真希望, the content is often unrealistic or difficult to achieve, making it poetic and emotional.

items.question: 我多么_____见到你啊！
items.translation: (How much I want to see you!)
items.explanation: 想 (xiǎng) with 多么 (duōme, how much) creates an exclamatory wish. 多么想见到你 = how much I want to see you! This is heartfelt and emotional. The pattern 多么想 + verb expresses deep longing: 多么想回家 (how I want to go home), 多么想吃妈妈做的菜 (how I want to eat mom's cooking). This is more literary/poetic than plain 想, often found in songs, letters, and emotional moments. The 啊 at the end adds exclamatory feeling.

items.question: 他_____意帮你搬家。
items.translation: (He is willing to help you move.)
items.explanation: 愿意 (yuànyi) means 'willing to/be glad to.' 他愿意帮你搬家 = he is willing to help you move. 愿意 focuses on willingness rather than desire — it's about consent and readiness. Critical in formal contexts: 你愿意嫁给他吗？(Are you willing to marry him? — wedding vow). Compare: 想 (want to — desire), 愿意 (willing to — consent), 乐意 (happy to — enthusiasm). 不愿意 = unwilling: 我不愿意加班 (I'm unwilling to work overtime). 愿意 implies a conscious choice.

items.question: 但愿明天_____是个好天气。
items.translation: (I hope tomorrow will be good weather.)
items.explanation: 但愿 (dànyuàn) means 'if only/I can only hope' — a literary way to express a wish with uncertainty. 但愿...是 = I hope...will be. 但愿 is more literary than 希望 and carries a stronger sense of 'I can only wish' — implying the speaker knows they can't control the outcome. Common expressions: 但愿如此 (I hope so), 但愿一切顺利 (I hope everything goes smoothly). 但愿 is often used in writing, poetry, and formal speech. It sounds more elegant than 希望 in appropriate contexts.

items.question: 我_____学好中文。
items.translation: (I'm determined to learn Chinese well.)
items.explanation: 一定要 (yīdìng yào) expresses strong determination — 'I absolutely will/must.' 我一定要学好中文 = I'm determined to learn Chinese well. While technically an obligation expression, 一定要 when used with '我' becomes a self-imposed commitment — expressing determination rather than external obligation. Compare: 你一定要来 (you must come — obligation on others) vs 我一定要成功 (I must succeed — personal determination). It's a powerful self-motivation pattern used in goals and resolutions.

items.question: 我_____在要是在海边就好了。
items.translation: (If only I were at the beach right now, that would be great.)
items.explanation: 现在 (xiànzài) = right now. The pattern ...要是...就好了 expresses a counterfactual wish: 'if only [situation], that would be great.' 现在要是在海边就好了 = if only I were at the beach now. This is Chinese's way of expressing the English 'I wish I were...' The 就好了 at the end means 'then it would be good.' Common patterns: 要是能去就好了 (if only I could go), 要是你在就好了 (if only you were here). It expresses gentle longing for something currently impossible.

items.question: 祝你生日_____！
items.translation: (Wish you a happy birthday!)
items.explanation: 祝 (zhù) means 'wish (someone something).' 祝你生日快乐 = wish you happy birthday. 祝 is the standard verb for formal wishes and blessings: 祝你新年快乐 (Happy New Year), 祝你身体健康 (wishing you good health), 祝你工作顺利 (wishing you smooth work), 祝你好运 (wishing you good luck). The pattern is: 祝 + person + [good thing]. 快乐 (kuàilè) specifically means 'happy/joyful' and collocates naturally with 生日 (birthday).

items.question: 他_____不得马上回家。
items.translation: (He can't wait to go home immediately.)
items.explanation: 巴不得 (bābude) means 'can't wait to/wish eagerly.' 巴不得马上回家 = can't wait to go home right away. It expresses an extremely strong, urgent desire. 巴不得 is colloquial and vivid — it conveys impatience and longing: 巴不得明天就放假 (can't wait for the holiday tomorrow), 巴不得立刻见到你 (dying to see you right now). It's stronger than 想 or 希望 — it implies the person is practically bursting with desire. Used in casual speech, not formal writing.

items.question: 我_____来这里工作。
items.translation: (I'm not willing to come work here.)
items.explanation: 不愿意 (bù yuànyì) means 'unwilling to/don't want to.' 不愿意来 = unwilling to come. It's a firm but polite refusal that emphasizes personal choice. Compare refusals: 不想 (don't want to — casual), 不愿意 (unwilling — firmer, implies principle), 不能 (can't — implies external barrier), 不要 (don't want — childish/emphatic). 不愿意 is appropriate for serious decisions: 不愿意结婚 (unwilling to marry), 不愿意放弃 (unwilling to give up). It respects the person's agency.

items.question: 我_____望长大以后当老师。
items.translation: (I aspire to become a teacher when I grow up.)
items.explanation: 渴望 (kěwàng) means 'yearn for/aspire to' — a strong, deep desire. 渴望当老师 = aspire to become a teacher. 渴 literally means 'thirsty' — so 渴望 is 'thirsty for' something, conveying intense longing. It's more literary and passionate than 希望: 渴望自由 (yearn for freedom), 渴望和平 (yearn for peace), 渴望成功 (yearn for success). 渴望 is commonly used in essays, speeches, and emotional writing. In casual conversation, 特别想 (really want to) would be more natural.

items.question: 她_____着有一天能出国留学。
items.translation: (She looks forward to studying abroad one day.)
items.explanation: 盼着 (pànzhe) means 'look forward to/long for.' 盼着有一天能出国 = looking forward to the day she can go abroad. 盼 implies patient, sustained hope — waiting for something good to come. Related expressions: 盼望 (long for — formal), 期盼 (anticipate — literary), 盼着...呢 (looking forward to — colloquial). 盼 carries warmth and emotional investment: 妈妈盼着你回来 (mom is looking forward to your return). It suggests the wait is emotional, not just practical.

name: Advanced Number Expressions (数字表达进阶)

items.question: 这本书_____块钱。
items.translation: (This book costs thirty-five yuan.)
items.explanation: Chinese numbers follow a strict place-value system: tens digit + 十 + ones digit. 三十五 = 3×10 + 5 = 35. This is consistent and logical: 二十一 (21), 四十八 (48), 九十九 (99). Unlike English where teens are irregular (thirteen, fifteen), Chinese is perfectly systematic. For prices, 块 (kuài) is the colloquial word for 元 (yuán, the formal currency unit).

items.question: 我们班_____个学生。
items.translation: (Our class has one hundred students.)
items.explanation: 百 (bǎi) means 'hundred.' Unlike 十 where 一十 is rarely said (just 十), 百 requires the leading 一: 一百 (100), 二百 (200), 三百 (300). Chinese counts in groups of four digits (万 = 10,000) not three (thousand) like English. Critical difference: 一百零三 (103) needs 零 to mark the empty tens place, while 一百三十 (130) doesn't need 零.

items.question: 中国有十四_____人口。
items.translation: (China has a population of 1.4 billion.)
items.explanation: 亿 (yì) = 100,000,000 (one hundred million). Chinese has a unique number unit 万 (wàn = 10,000) and 亿 (yì = 100 million) that English lacks. So 14亿 = 1.4 billion. This is one of the trickiest parts of Chinese numbers for English speakers: you must mentally convert between the two systems. 十万 = 100,000, 百万 = 1,000,000, 千万 = 10,000,000.

items.question: 这个城市有两_____万人。
items.translation: (This city has two hundred thousand people.)
items.explanation: 两十万 = 2 × 10 × 10,000 = 200,000. Note: 两 (liǎng) is used instead of 二 (èr) before measure words and large units. 两百 (200), 两千 (2000), 两万 (20,000). The rule: use 两 before measure words (两个人) and units (两百), use 二 in math (二加三) and sequential counting (第二). This 两/二 distinction trips up many learners.

items.question: 她今年_____岁了。
items.translation: (She is twenty-one years old this year.)
items.explanation: 二十一 = 21. For age, use 岁 (suì) — the classifier for years of age. Note: for 21 we use 二十一 not 两十一, because 二 is used in the tens place of compound numbers (20, 200 in counting). The pattern: 二十 (20), but 两百 (200). However, 二百 is also acceptable in some dialects. In Taiwan, 廿 (niàn) is sometimes used for 20.

items.question: 我等了_____个半小时。
items.translation: (I waited for two and a half hours.)
items.explanation: 两个半小时 = two and a half hours. Here 两 is required before the measure word 个. 半 (bàn) means 'half' and is placed after the noun/measure word: 两个半 (two and a half), 三天半 (three and a half days). Alternative phrasing: 两个半钟头. Note: 俩 (liǎ) means 'two' and already includes 个, so 俩人 = 两个人 but you cannot say 俩个.

items.question: 苹果三块五_____一斤。
items.translation: (Apples are 3.50 yuan per jin.)
items.explanation: 毛 (máo) is the colloquial term for 角 (jiǎo) = 0.1 yuan. Chinese currency units: 元/块 (yuan, 1), 角/毛 (jiao, 0.1), 分 (fen, 0.01). So 三块五毛 = 3.50 yuan. In spoken Chinese, the last unit is often dropped: 三块五 (omitting 毛). Similarly: 五毛三 means 五毛三分 (0.53 yuan). 斤 (jīn) is a Chinese unit of weight = 500 grams.

items.question: 今天是二〇二五年_____月三日。
items.translation: (Today is March 3rd, 2025.)
items.explanation: Dates in Chinese: 年 (nián, year) + 月 (yuè, month) + 日/号 (rì/hào, day). Months are simply numbered: 一月 (January), 二月 (February), 三月 (March)... 十二月 (December). No special month names to memorize! Days use plain numbers too: 三日 or 三号. 号 is more colloquial, 日 more formal. Years are read digit by digit: 二〇二五年 (2025).

items.question: 现在是下午_____点十五分。
items.translation: (It is now 3:15 PM.)
items.explanation: Time telling: 点 (diǎn) = o'clock, 分 (fēn) = minutes. 三点十五分 = 3:15. Chinese uses 上午 (shàngwǔ, AM) and 下午 (xiàwǔ, PM) BEFORE the time, not after. Shortcuts: 三点半 (3:30), 三点一刻 (3:15, using 刻 = quarter), 差五分三点 (five to three = 2:55). The 24-hour clock is common in formal contexts.

items.question: 这条路有_____公里长。
items.translation: (This road is several hundred kilometers long.)
items.explanation: 几 (jǐ) before a unit means 'several/some (unspecified number)': 几百 (several hundred), 几千 (several thousand), 几十 (several tens/dozens). This is a very natural way to express approximate quantities. Compare: 几个 (a few), 几十个 (dozens), 几百个 (hundreds), 几千个 (thousands). 几 can also be a question word: 几个? (how many?).

items.question: 大约有_____人参加了会议。
items.translation: (Approximately fifty people attended the meeting.)
items.explanation: Number + 多 means 'more than/over that number': 五十多 = 50-something, over 50. It's placed after the number: 三十多岁 (thirty-something years old), 一百多块 (over 100 yuan). For more precision: 五十来个 (around 50), 五十左右 (roughly 50). 大约 (dàyuē) at the beginning adds 'approximately' for extra hedging.

items.question: 他是我们学校第_____名。
items.translation: (He is number one in our school.)
items.explanation: 第 (dì) + number creates ordinal numbers: 第一 (first), 第二 (second), 第三 (third). Unlike English with its irregular ordinals (first, second, third), Chinese is perfectly regular: just add 第 before any number. 第一百 (100th), 第一千 (1000th). Used for rankings: 第一名 (1st place), 第二次 (2nd time), 第三个 (the 3rd one).

items.question: 这件衣服打_____折。
items.translation: (This piece of clothing is 20% off.)
items.explanation: Chinese discounts work opposite to English! 打八折 = 80% of the price = 20% off. 折 (zhé) means the fraction you PAY, not save. So: 打九折 (10% off), 打七折 (30% off), 打五折 (50% off/half price), 打对折 (also half price). This reverse logic is a common source of confusion. Remember: higher 折 number = smaller discount.

items.question: 我的手机号是一三_____二五六七八九〇。
items.translation: (My phone number is 139-2567-890.)
items.explanation: Phone numbers and IDs are read digit by digit, not as full numbers. 一三九 = 1-3-9, not 'one hundred thirty-nine.' Special rule: 1 is read as 'yāo' (幺) instead of 'yī' in phone numbers and room numbers to avoid confusion with 七 (qī). So 110 is 'yāo yāo líng.' This is unique to sequential number reading contexts.

items.question: 他_____次都没有通过考试。
items.translation: (He didn't pass the exam either time.)
items.explanation: 两次 = two times/both times. With 次 (cì, times/occurrences), use 两 not 二. The pattern: 两 before most measure words (两个, 两次, 两天), 二 in ordinals (第二次), math (二加三), and certain fixed expressions (二月, 二楼). 俩 (liǎ) = 两个, so 俩人 works but 俩次 doesn't. 双 (shuāng) means 'a pair' and is used for things that come in pairs: 一双鞋.

items.question: _____都可以参加这个活动。
items.translation: (Everyone can participate in this activity.)
items.explanation: 谁都 = everyone/anyone. Chinese question words double as indefinite pronouns when followed by 都 or 也! 谁都 (everyone), 什么都 (everything), 哪里都 (everywhere), 哪个都 (whichever). This is a fundamental feature of Chinese: the same word serves both functions. Context and 都/也 determine whether it's a question or indefinite: 谁来了? (Who came?) vs 谁都来了 (Everyone came).

items.question: 我什么_____不想吃。
items.translation: (I don't want to eat anything.)
items.explanation: 什么都不 = nothing (literally: whatever-all-not). The pattern: question word + 都/也 + 不/没 = nothing/no one/nowhere. 什么都不要 (don't want anything), 谁也没来 (nobody came), 哪儿都没去 (didn't go anywhere). Both 都 and 也 work here, but 都 emphasizes totality while 也 adds a tone of 'even/not even one.'

items.question: 这件事_____人知道。
items.translation: (Some people know about this matter.)
items.explanation: 有些 (yǒuxiē) = some (people/things). 有些人 (some people), 有些东西 (some things). Compare with 有的 (yǒude): 有的人喜欢，有的人不喜欢 (some people like it, some don't). 有些 emphasizes a portion of a group, while 有的 often appears in contrasting pairs. Another option: 某些 (mǒuxiē) = certain, more formal.

items.question: 我哪儿_____不想去。
items.translation: (I don't want to go anywhere.)
items.explanation: 哪儿都不 = nowhere/not anywhere. 哪儿 (nǎr) or 哪里 (nǎlǐ) as indefinite means 'anywhere/everywhere.' Pattern: 哪儿都去过 (been everywhere), 哪儿都没去 (went nowhere). Note the word order: the question word comes first, then 都, then the negation. This is the opposite of English: 'not... anywhere' becomes 'anywhere... all... not.'

items.question: _____个学生都通过了考试。
items.translation: (Every student passed the exam.)
items.explanation: 每 (měi) = every/each. 每 + measure word + noun: 每个人 (everyone), 每天 (every day), 每次 (every time), 每年 (every year). 每 emphasizes individual members. Compare: 每个 (each one individually), 所有 (all, as a group), 全部 (the entirety). 每 often pairs with 都: 每个人都来了 (each person came, emphasizing that not a single person was missing).

items.question: 你想喝_____吗？
items.translation: (Do you want to drink something?)
items.explanation: 点什么 = something (in questions/offers). (想)喝点什么 is a polite way to offer a drink. 点 here means 'a bit/some' and softens the question. Alternatives: 喝些什么 (drink something), 喝点儿什么 (with 儿 suffix, Beijing dialect). In statements: 我想喝点水 (I want to drink some water). 点 + noun is softer and more polite than directly naming what you want.

items.question: 他_____也不关心。
items.translation: (He doesn't care about anything.)
items.explanation: 什么也不 = don't... anything. 什么也不关心 = doesn't care about anything. Here 也 instead of 都 adds emphasis on 'not even one thing.' Common patterns: 什么也没说 (didn't say anything), 什么也不会 (can't do anything), 什么也不怕 (not afraid of anything). The nuance: 什么都 (absolutely everything) vs 什么也 (not even one thing).

items.question: 请_____一个人来帮忙。
items.translation: (Please find someone to come help.)
items.explanation: 找一个人 = find someone/a person. When Chinese uses 'a person' it's literally 一个人. Chinese doesn't have articles (a/an/the), so 一个人 can mean 'a person' or 'one person' depending on context. For 'someone' in questions: 有人吗? (Is someone there?). 有人 = someone exists/there is someone. 没有人/没人 = no one. 某人 (mǒurén) = a certain person (more formal).

items.question: 你怎么_____不高兴？
items.translation: (Why do you always seem unhappy?)
items.explanation: 总是 (zǒngshì) = always/invariably. While not strictly an indefinite pronoun, 总是 expresses the indefinite concept of 'at all times.' Compare frequency words: 总是 (always, sometimes with mild complaint), 一直 (always/continuously, neutral), 经常 (often), 有时候 (sometimes), 偶尔 (occasionally), 从来不 (never). 总是 here implies slight frustration.

items.question: 无论_____困难，我们都能克服。
items.translation: (No matter what difficulty, we can overcome it.)
items.explanation: 无论什么 = no matter what/whatever. 无论 (wúlùn) or 不论 (bùlùn) + question word + 都/也 creates 'no matter what/who/where' constructions. 无论谁都 (whoever), 无论哪里都 (wherever), 无论怎么样都 (however). More colloquial: 不管 (bùguǎn) replaces 无论 in casual speech. These create powerful concessive clauses essential in formal Chinese.

items.question: 任何人_____不能违反法律。
items.translation: (No person may violate the law.)
items.explanation: 任何 (rènhé) = any (formal). 任何人都不 = no one may/nobody can. 任何 is more formal than using question words: 任何人 (anyone, formal) vs 谁都 (anyone, colloquial). Used in rules, laws, and formal writing: 任何情况下 (under any circumstances), 任何时候 (at any time), 任何地方 (any place). Always pairs with 都 or 也 in negative sentences.

items.question: 我在路上碰到了_____朋友。
items.translation: (I ran into a friend on the road.)
items.explanation: 一个朋友 = a friend / a certain friend. In Chinese, 一个 serves the role of English 'a/an' for introducing new or unspecified things. 碰到 (pèngdào) = ran into / bumped into. Note: Chinese has no articles, so context determines definiteness. 我的朋友 = my friend (specific), 一个朋友 = a friend (non-specific), 那个朋友 = that friend (demonstrative).

items.question: 别的_____我都不想要。
items.translation: (I don't want anything else.)
items.explanation: 别的东西 = other things. 别的 (biéde) = other/different. Common patterns: 别的人 (other people), 别的地方 (other places), 别的办法 (other methods). Synonyms: 其他 (qítā, other, slightly more formal), 另外 (lìngwài, additionally/another). 别 alone means 'don't': 别走 (don't go). Context distinguishes 别的 (other) from 别 (don't).

items.question: 这里_____人也没有。
items.translation: (There is nobody here at all.)
items.explanation: 一个人也没有 = not a single person. 一 + measure word + 也没/也不 is a strong negation meaning 'not even one.' 一块钱也没有 (not even one yuan), 一句话也没说 (didn't say a single word), 一次也没去过 (never went even once). This pattern uses 也 (not 都) and is more emphatic than simple negation. It corresponds to English 'not a single...'

items.question: 不管你去_____，我都跟着你。
items.translation: (No matter where you go, I'll follow you.)
items.explanation: 不管...哪儿都 = no matter where. 不管 (bùguǎn) + question word + 都/也 expresses 'regardless of.' 不管谁 (whoever), 不管什么时候 (whenever), 不管怎么样 (no matter what). 不管 is colloquial, 无论 is formal, 不论 is in between. Note: 哪儿 (where, indefinite) vs 那儿 (there, demonstrative) — the tone mark matters: nǎr vs nàr!

items.question: 他学习非常努力，真是_____。
items.translation: (He studies very hard, truly tireless.)
items.explanation: 废寝忘食 (fèi qǐn wàng shí) = 'forget to sleep and eat' — so dedicated one neglects basic needs. Most chengyu (成语) are four-character idiomatic expressions from classical Chinese. They're used in both formal writing and everyday speech to sound educated and expressive. This one praises extreme diligence. Structure: 废 (abandon) + 寝 (sleep) + 忘 (forget) + 食 (food).

items.question: 你这样做简直是_____。
items.translation: (What you're doing is simply adding legs to a snake.)
items.explanation: 画蛇添足 (huà shé tiān zú) = 'draw a snake and add feet' — to ruin something by overdoing or adding unnecessary elements. From an ancient story where people competed to draw a snake; the winner added feet and was disqualified since snakes don't have feet. Used to criticize unnecessary additions: 你的文章已经很好了，别画蛇添足 (Your essay is great, don't over-embellish it).

items.question: 虽然犯了错误，但_____还不晚。
items.translation: (Although mistakes were made, it's not too late to fix things.)
items.explanation: 亡羊补牢 (wáng yáng bǔ láo) = 'mend the pen after losing sheep' — it's never too late to fix a problem. 亡 (lose), 羊 (sheep), 补 (mend), 牢 (pen/enclosure). From a fable about a farmer who lost sheep because of a hole in the fence. A neighbor suggested fixing it, and it worked. Often used encouragingly: 现在开始学还来得及，亡羊补牢嘛 (It's not too late to start learning now).

items.question: 跟他解释这些就像_____一样。
items.translation: (Explaining this to him is like playing music to a cow.)
items.explanation: 对牛弹琴 (duì niú tán qín) = 'play the lute to a cow' — speaking to someone who can't or won't understand. Similar to English 'casting pearls before swine.' 对 (to/facing), 牛 (cow), 弹 (play), 琴 (instrument). Used when your audience clearly can't appreciate what you're saying. Slightly rude if said directly about someone: implies they lack the ability to understand.

items.question: 他不努力，只想_____。
items.translation: (He doesn't work hard, just wants to wait for luck.)
items.explanation: 守株待兔 (shǒu zhū dài tù) = 'guard a tree stump waiting for a rabbit' — relying on luck instead of effort. From a story: a farmer saw a rabbit crash into a tree stump and die. He then waited by the stump every day hoping it would happen again, neglecting his fields. Used to criticize laziness and reliance on luck: 你不能守株待兔，要主动去找机会 (You can't just wait for luck, actively seek opportunities).

items.question: 他的见识太少了，真是_____。
items.translation: (His knowledge is too limited, truly a frog in a well.)
items.explanation: 井底之蛙 (jǐng dǐ zhī wā) = 'frog at the bottom of a well' — someone with a narrow worldview. 井 (well), 底 (bottom), 之 (classical 的), 蛙 (frog). The frog thinks the small circle of sky it sees is the entire sky. Used to describe someone ignorant of the wider world. 之 is the classical Chinese equivalent of 的, preserved in many chengyu.

items.question: 这件事我们可以_____。
items.translation: (We can kill two birds with one stone with this.)
items.explanation: 一举两得 (yī jǔ liǎng dé) = 'one move, two gains' — achieve two goals with one action. Chinese equivalent of 'kill two birds with one stone.' 一举 (one action), 两得 (two gains). Used positively to praise efficiency. Related: 一箭双雕 (one arrow, two eagles) has the same meaning. Example: 骑自行车上班，锻炼身体又省钱，一举两得 (Biking to work exercises the body and saves money — two birds, one stone).

items.question: 做事不能_____，要坚持到底。
items.translation: (You can't give up halfway, persist to the end.)
items.explanation: 半途而废 (bàn tú ér fèi) = 'abandon halfway' — give up before finishing. 半途 (halfway), 而 (and then), 废 (abandon). A very common chengyu used to encourage perseverance. 做什么事都不能半途而废 (Whatever you do, don't give up halfway). Related: 坚持到底 (persist to the end), 有始有终 (have a beginning and an end = see things through).

items.question: 他做事_____，不够专注。
items.translation: (He does things half-heartedly, not focused enough.)
items.explanation: 三心二意 (sān xīn èr yì) = 'three hearts, two minds' — indecisive, not focused. Criticizes someone who can't commit. Its opposite is 一心一意 (yī xīn yī yì) = 'one heart, one mind' — wholeheartedly devoted. Number-based chengyu are common: 七上八下 (anxious, 7 up 8 down), 五颜六色 (colorful, 5 colors 6 hues), 千方百计 (by every means, 1000 ways 100 plans).

items.question: 他_____地掩盖事实。
items.translation: (He foolishly tried to cover up the facts.)
items.explanation: 掩耳盗铃 (yǎn ěr dào líng) = 'cover ears to steal a bell' — self-deception, fooling only yourself. A thief covered his own ears while stealing a bell, thinking if he couldn't hear it, neither could anyone else. Used to mock someone who deceives only themselves: 不学习却说自己准备好了，这不就是掩耳盗铃吗 (Not studying but saying you're prepared — isn't that just self-deception?).

items.question: 他的方法太死板了，简直是_____。
items.translation: (His method is too rigid, simply marking the boat to find the sword.)
items.explanation: 刻舟求剑 (kè zhōu qiú jiàn) = 'carve the boat to find the sword' — stubbornly using outdated methods. Story: a man dropped his sword in a river and carved a mark on the boat where it fell, planning to retrieve it later — not realizing the boat had moved. Criticizes inflexible thinking: 时代变了，不能刻舟求剑 (Times have changed, you can't use outdated methods).

items.question: 他看到影子就害怕，真是_____。
items.translation: (He's afraid of shadows, truly seeing snakes in every bow reflection.)
items.explanation: 杯弓蛇影 (bēi gōng shé yǐng) = 'see a bow's reflection in a cup and mistake it for a snake' — being overly suspicious or paranoid. Story: a man saw the reflection of a bow in his wine cup, mistook it for a snake, and became sick from fear. Used when someone is frightened by imaginary threats: 别杯弓蛇影了，没什么好怕的 (Stop being paranoid, there's nothing to fear).

items.question: 他们两个的水平_____。
items.translation: (The two of them are equally matched in skill.)
items.explanation: 不相上下 (bù xiāng shàng xià) = 'neither above nor below each other' — equally matched, on par. 相 (mutually), 上下 (above/below). Used for comparing skills, quality, or performance: 这两个队伍不相上下 (These two teams are evenly matched). Related: 势均力敌 (evenly matched in power), 旗鼓相当 (flags and drums equal = equally matched).

items.question: 这里的风景真是_____。
items.translation: (The scenery here is truly beautiful beyond words.)
items.explanation: 美不胜收 (měi bù shèng shōu) = 'so beautiful one cannot take it all in.' 美 (beautiful), 不胜 (cannot fully), 收 (take in/absorb). Used for overwhelming beauty in nature, art, or displays. Perfect for travel descriptions: 桂林山水美不胜收 (Guilin's landscapes are breathtakingly beautiful). 胜 in classical Chinese means 'to be able to bear/handle.'

items.question: 他们俩_____，关系很好。
items.translation: (The two of them are inseparable, their relationship is great.)
items.explanation: 形影不离 (xíng yǐng bù lí) = 'form and shadow never part' — inseparable, always together. 形 (form/body), 影 (shadow), 不离 (not separate). Describes very close friends or couples: 他们从小形影不离 (They've been inseparable since childhood). Body-shadow metaphors are common in Chinese: 如影随形 (follow like a shadow), 形单影只 (lonely, just one's body and shadow).

items.question: 我_____去过中国。
items.translation: (I have been to China.)
items.explanation: 曾经 (céngjīng) = 'once/formerly/have ever.' Used to describe past experiences, often with 过: 曾经去过 (have been to), 曾经见过 (have seen). 曾经 emphasizes that the experience happened at some point in the past. Compare: 曾经 (at some point, narrative) vs 已经 (already, completed), 正在 (currently), 将要 (about to). Literary flavor: 曾经沧海难为水 (Having seen the ocean, no river impresses).

items.question: 你吃_____北京烤鸭吗？
items.translation: (Have you eaten Beijing roast duck?)
items.explanation: Verb + 过 (guo) marks past experience — 'have ever done.' 吃过 (have eaten), 去过 (have been to), 看过 (have seen). This is Chinese's way of expressing the English present perfect for experiences. Key difference from 了: 吃了 (ate, completed action) vs 吃过 (have eaten before, life experience). Negative: 没(有)...过: 我没吃过 (I've never eaten it).

items.question: 她从来_____坐过飞机。
items.translation: (She has never flown on an airplane.)
items.explanation: 从来没...过 = have never... 从来 (cónglái) means 'ever/never' (in negative contexts). 从来没去过 (have never been to), 从来没见过 (have never seen). 从来 intensifies 没...过 to emphasize 'not even once, ever.' Positive form: 从来都是 (have always been). Both 没 and 没有 work here; 没 is shorter and more common in speech.

items.question: 我小时候_____在农村生活。
items.translation: (When I was little, I once lived in the countryside.)
items.explanation: 曾 (céng) is the literary/shortened form of 曾经, meaning 'once/formerly.' Used in slightly more formal or written contexts. 我曾在那里工作 (I once worked there). 曾 carries a nostalgic or reflective tone. Appears in many classical expressions: 似曾相识 (seems to have met before = déjà vu). In everyday speech, 曾经 is more common than 曾 alone.

items.question: 你有没有_____过这种情况？
items.translation: (Have you encountered this kind of situation?)
items.explanation: 遇到过 = have encountered. 遇到 (yùdào) means 'to encounter/run into.' The 有没有...过 pattern is an alternative way to ask about experience: 你有没有去过? = 你去过没有? = 你去过吗? (Have you been?). This 有没有 pattern is very common in Southern Chinese and Taiwanese Mandarin. All three question forms are grammatically correct.

items.question: 这是我第一_____出国。
items.translation: (This is my first time going abroad.)
items.explanation: 第一次 = the first time. 次 (cì) is the measure word for occurrences/times. 第一次见面 (first time meeting), 第二次来 (second time coming), 第三次尝试 (third attempt). 回 (huí) also means 'time/occasion' but is more colloquial: 这是第几回了? (How many times has this been?). 趟 (tàng) is specifically for trips: 去了一趟 (made one trip).

items.question: 我_____没有这样的经历。
items.translation: (I have never had such an experience before.)
items.explanation: 以前 (yǐqián) = before/previously/in the past. 以前没...过 expresses 'never before.' 以前 can also start a sentence for 'In the past...': 以前我住在上海 (I used to live in Shanghai). Compare: 以前 (before/in the past, general), 之前 (before a specific time), 以后 (after/in the future), 之后 (after a specific time). Time words come at the start or after the subject.

items.question: 他_____跑过马拉松。
items.translation: (He has run a marathon before.)
items.explanation: 以前跑过 = have run before. Here 以前 specifies when the experience happened (in the past), and 过 marks it as an experience. Double marking past experience (以前 + 过) is natural and emphasizes that this is a past achievement. You could also say 他跑过马拉松 (past tense implied by 过) without 以前, but adding it makes the time frame more explicit.

items.question: 我还_____见过这么美的地方。
items.translation: (I have never seen such a beautiful place.)
items.explanation: 还没...过 = have not yet... / have never yet... 还 (hái) here adds 'yet/still' to the negation, implying this is the most extreme experience: 还没见过 = haven't yet/ever seen. This pattern expresses superlative experiences: 我还没吃过这么好吃的 (I've never tasted anything this delicious). 还没 implies the possibility remains open for the future.

items.question: 据我的_____，这种方法很有效。
items.translation: (Based on my experience, this method is very effective.)
items.explanation: 经验 (jīngyàn) = experience (knowledge/skill gained). Compare three types of 'experience' in Chinese: 经验 (practical knowledge: 工作经验 work experience), 经历 (life experience/event: 人生经历 life journey), 体验 (first-hand experience/trying: 体验生活 experience life). 据 (jù) = according to/based on, a formal connector: 据说 (it is said), 据我所知 (as far as I know).

items.question: 我们_____过很多困难。
items.translation: (We have been through many difficulties.)
items.explanation: 经历过 = have experienced/been through. 经历 (jīnglì) as a verb means 'to go through/experience (events).' As a noun: 一段难忘的经历 (an unforgettable experience). Compare: 经历困难 (go through difficulties), 经历战争 (live through war). 经过 (jīngguò) means 'to pass by/through': 经过学校 (pass by the school) or 'through/via': 经过努力 (through effort).

items.question: 我_____想起来我见过他。
items.translation: (I suddenly remembered that I had met him.)
items.explanation: 突然 (tūrán) = suddenly. 突然想起来 = suddenly remembered/recalled. Used to narrate moments of realization about past experiences. Related: 忽然 (hūrán) is a synonym with slightly more literary flavor. 想起来 (xiǎng qǐlái) = recall/remember (the memory comes up). Compare: 想起来 (recall, memory surfaces) vs 记得 (remember, continuous knowledge) vs 记住 (memorize, commit to memory).

items.question: 你_____经学过中文吗？
items.translation: (Have you previously studied Chinese?)
items.explanation: 曾经学过 = have previously studied. The full form is 曾经...过, but 曾 can replace 曾经 in questions. This asks about past educational experience. 学过 alone already means 'have studied,' but 曾经 adds emphasis on 'at any point in the past.' In interviews: 你曾经在哪里工作过? (Where have you previously worked?). The 经 in 曾经 is often dropped in casual speech.

items.question: 我到现在_____记得那件事。
items.translation: (I still remember that incident to this day.)
items.explanation: 到现在还 = until now, still. 还 (hái) = still/yet. 到现在还记得 emphasizes that the memory has persisted from the past until now. 还 is essential for expressing continuity: 还在 (still at/doing), 还没 (not yet), 还是 (still is/or). Common for memorable experiences: 到现在想起来还觉得害怕 (Even now I still feel scared thinking about it).

items.question: 那次旅行给我留下了深刻的_____。
items.translation: (That trip left a deep impression on me.)
items.explanation: 印象 (yìnxiàng) = impression. 深刻的印象 = deep impression. 给...留下印象 = leave an impression on someone. This is a very common expression: 第一印象 (first impression), 印象深刻 (deeply impressed). Compare: 印象 (impression, how something strikes you), 回忆 (memories, what you remember), 感觉 (feeling, emotional response), 经验 (experience, practical knowledge gained).

items.question: 我_____你多休息一下。
items.translation: (I suggest you rest more.)
items.explanation: 建议 (jiànyì) = suggest/recommend. 我建议你... is the most direct way to give a suggestion. Compare politeness levels: 建议 (suggest, neutral), 提议 (propose, slightly formal), 推荐 (recommend, for things/people). 建议 can be a verb or noun: 给你一个建议 (give you a suggestion). More polite: 我想建议... (I'd like to suggest...) or 我有个建议 (I have a suggestion).

items.question: 你_____去看看医生。
items.translation: (You should go see a doctor.)
items.explanation: 应该 (yīnggāi) = should/ought to. The most common way to make suggestions. Strength scale: 可以 (can/may) < 应该 (should) < 必须 (must) < 一定要 (absolutely must). 应该 implies a recommendation without forcing: 你应该早点睡 (You should sleep earlier). Negative: 不应该 (shouldn't): 你不应该说那种话 (You shouldn't say those things).

items.question: 你_____不要喝太多咖啡。
items.translation: (You'd better not drink too much coffee.)
items.explanation: 最好 (zuìhǎo) = had better/it would be best. A gentle but firm suggestion. 最好 + verb: 你最好早点来 (You'd better come early). 最好不要 is softer than 不要 alone: 最好不要迟到 (Better not be late) vs 不要迟到! (Don't be late!). 最好 literally means 'most good/best' and frames the suggestion as the optimal choice rather than an order.

items.question: 我们去散步，_____？
items.translation: (Let's go for a walk, okay?)
items.explanation: Statement + 好吗 = ..., okay? A polite way to make suggestions seeking agreement. Compare suggestion-ending particles: 好吗 (okay?, seeking permission), 好不好 (okay or not?, more casual), 怎么样 (how about it?, open-ended), 行吗 (is that doable?). This pattern transforms a statement into a gentle proposal. Very common in daily life: 我们明天去，好吗? (Let's go tomorrow, okay?).

items.question: 我们_____去爬山吧。
items.translation: (How about we go hiking!)
items.explanation: 一起 (yīqǐ) = together. 我们一起...吧 is the classic pattern for suggesting group activities. 吧 (ba) at the end softens the suggestion: 我们走吧 (Let's go), 吃饭吧 (Let's eat). Without 吧, it sounds more like a command. 一起 is versatile: 一起吃 (eat together), 一起学 (study together), 一起玩 (play together). Tip: 一块儿 (yīkuàir) is a colloquial synonym.

items.question: 你_____试试这个方法。
items.translation: (Why don't you try this method?)
items.explanation: 不妨 (bùfáng) = might as well / why not. A polite, encouraging suggestion. 不妨试试 (why not give it a try), 不妨考虑 (you might want to consider). 妨 means 'to hinder'; 不妨 literally means 'it won't hurt to.' More formal and literary than 可以试试. Used when you want to suggest something without pressure: 如果有时间，不妨来参观 (If you have time, why not come visit).

items.question: 要不我们_____看电影？
items.translation: (How about we go watch a movie?)
items.explanation: 要不 (yàobù) = how about / what if / or else. An informal, colloquial way to suggest alternatives. 要不我们走吧 (How about we leave?). Very natural in daily conversation. Similar: 要不然 (yàobùrán) = otherwise/or else. 要不 can also introduce backup plans: 要不你先走 (How about you go first?). Tone is casual and friendly — perfect among friends.

items.question: 我觉得你_____多练习。
items.translation: (I think you need to practice more.)
items.explanation: 需要 (xūyào) = need to. Framing suggestions with 我觉得你需要... ('I think you need to...') is polite because 我觉得 softens it as personal opinion. Compare: 需要 (need, neutral), 得 (have to, colloquial), 必须 (must, strong), 应该 (should, moderate). Adding 我觉得/我认为 before any suggestion makes it less imposing — a key politeness strategy.

items.question: 你看这样做_____不行？
items.translation: (Do you think doing it this way works or not?)
items.explanation: 行不行 (xíng bù xíng) = will it work or not? A/not-A questions (V不V) are a fundamental Chinese question pattern. 行不行 asks for approval of a suggestion. Variations: 好不好 (okay?), 可以不可以 (can or can't?), 对不对 (right?). 你看 (nǐ kàn) = 'in your view/do you think' — adds deference by soliciting the listener's opinion first.

items.question: 如果我_____你，我会去旅行。
items.translation: (If I were you, I would go traveling.)
items.explanation: 如果我是你 = if I were you. Chinese hypotheticals use 如果 (rúguǒ) or 假如 (jiǎrú) + 的话 (optionally). Unlike English subjunctive ('were'), Chinese uses regular 是. Suggestion pattern: 如果我是你，我会... (If I were you, I would...). This is indirect and polite. 会 indicates willingness/likelihood. Related: 换做是我 (if it were me, swap perspective) is more colloquial.

items.question: 你为什么不_____换个工作？
items.translation: (Why don't you consider changing jobs?)
items.explanation: 为什么不考虑 = why not consider. 考虑 (kǎolǜ) = to consider/think about. Rhetorical question format for suggestions: 为什么不...? (Why not...?). This implies you should seriously think about it. 考虑一下 (consider for a bit) is softer. Related: 想想 (think about it, casual), 好好考虑 (think carefully), 慎重考虑 (consider carefully/seriously).

items.question: 我_____你应该先道歉。
items.translation: (I think you should apologize first.)
items.explanation: 认为 (rènwéi) = think/believe/consider. More formal than 觉得 for expressing opinions and suggestions. Hierarchy: 觉得 (feel/think, casual) < 认为 (believe/think, formal) < 以为 (thought/assumed, often for wrong assumptions). 我认为 frames the suggestion as a reasoned opinion. 以为 has a special nuance: 我以为你走了 (I thought you had left — but you didn't). Don't confuse 认为 and 以为.

items.question: 不如我们_____坐地铁吧。
items.translation: (Why don't we take the subway instead?)
items.explanation: 不如 (bùrú) = it would be better to / why don't we. 改 (gǎi) = change/switch to. 不如改坐地铁 = better to switch to the subway. 不如 compares the suggested option favorably: literally 'not as good as (the alternative).' Common: 不如回家 (might as well go home), 不如试试 (why not try). More elegant than 还是...吧. 不如 is slightly literary, 还是 is more casual.

items.question: 依我_____，你还是留下来比较好。
items.translation: (In my opinion, you'd better stay.)
items.explanation: 依我看 (yī wǒ kàn) = in my opinion/as I see it. A formal way to preface suggestions. 依 = according to, 看 = view. Variations: 在我看来 (from my perspective), 照我说 (if you ask me), 我看 (I think, casual). These preambles are important in Chinese because they signal that you're about to offer an opinion, giving the listener preparation. 比较好 = comparatively better (soft suggestion).

items.question: 你_____把这件事告诉老师。
items.translation: (You might want to tell the teacher about this.)
items.explanation: 可以 (kěyǐ) = can/may. Using 可以 for suggestions is the gentlest approach — it presents the action as a possibility, not a directive. 你可以... = you could/might want to. Contrast: 你可以 (you could, gentle) < 你应该 (you should, moderate) < 你必须 (you must, strong) < 你一定要 (you absolutely must, emphatic). 千万 and 绝对 are for warnings: 千万别去 (definitely don't go).

name: Advanced Time Clauses (时间从句进阶)

items.question: 我_____到家就给你打电话。
items.translation: (I'll call you as soon as I get home.)
items.explanation: 一...就... (yī...jiù...) means 'as soon as.' It's a two-part structure: 一 + Verb1 + 就 + Verb2. 我一到家就给你打电话 = 'As soon as I arrive home, I'll call you.' This pattern emphasizes immediacy — the second action follows the first instantly. Compare: 一看到他就笑了 (laughed as soon as I saw him). It can describe habitual, past, or future events. Very common in daily speech and formal writing alike.

items.question: 他每次_____来，我们都很高兴。
items.translation: (Every time he comes, we are very happy.)
items.explanation: 每次一来 combines 每次 (měi cì, every time) with 一...就 pattern. The 一 here marks the trigger condition — whenever he arrives. 每次 sets the frequency (every time), and 就 (implied after 来) introduces the result (我们都很高兴). This pattern is habitual. Compare: 每次一下雨就堵车 (every time it rains there's traffic). 都 reinforces the 'always' meaning in the result clause.

items.question: 她_____毕业以后就去了北京。
items.translation: (She went to Beijing right after graduating.)
items.explanation: 一 + Verb + 以后 (yǐhòu) creates a 'right after' structure. 一毕业以后 means 'right/immediately after graduating.' Without 一, 毕业以后 just means 'after graduating' without the immediacy. The 一 emphasizes there was no delay between graduation and going to Beijing. Alternative: 一毕业就去了 (went right after graduating) — this drops 以后 but keeps the same meaning via 一...就 pattern.

items.question: 等你_____好了再说吧。
items.translation: (Let's talk after you've prepared/gotten ready.)
items.explanation: 等...好了再... (děng...hǎo le zài...) means 'wait until...is done, then...' 准备好了 = 'have prepared/are ready.' The 好 is a result complement indicating completion. 再说 means 'talk about it later/then.' This is a polite way to defer a discussion. Compare: 等你想好了再决定 (decide after you've thought it through), 等我做好了再吃 (eat after I've finished cooking).

items.question: 自从他_____以后，我一直很想他。
items.translation: (Ever since he left, I have missed him all along.)
items.explanation: 自从 (zìcóng) means 'since/ever since' and marks the starting point of an ongoing state. 自从他走了以后 = 'ever since he left.' 一直 (yīzhí) means 'all along/continuously,' reinforcing the duration. This structure: 自从 + Event + 以后 + continuous result. Compare: 自从搬家以后一直很忙 (have been busy ever since moving). 自从 usually pairs with 以后/以来 and describes states that persist to the present.

items.question: 趁现在还_____，我们赶快走吧。
items.translation: (While it's still early, let's leave quickly.)
items.explanation: 趁 (chèn) means 'take advantage of a time/situation while it lasts.' 趁现在还早 = 'while it's still early.' 趁 emphasizes seizing an opportunity before conditions change. Common patterns: 趁热吃 (eat while it's hot), 趁年轻多学习 (study more while you're young), 趁天还没黑 (while it's not yet dark). 赶快 (gǎnkuài) = 'hurry.' 趁 always implies the window of opportunity could close.

items.question: 他_____做完作业才能出去玩。
items.translation: (He can go out to play only after finishing homework.)
items.explanation: 得 (děi) here means 'must/have to.' 得做完作业才能出去玩 = 'must finish homework before being able to go out to play.' 才 (cái) emphasizes 'only then/not until.' 才能 = 'only then can.' This structure: 得 + complete action + 才能 + desired action. Compare: 得等他来才能开始 (must wait until he arrives to start). 得 (děi) vs 要 (yào): 得 is more colloquial and implies necessity.

items.question: 在他来_____，我们先准备一下。
items.translation: (Before he comes, let's prepare first.)
items.explanation: 在...之前 (zài...zhīqián) means 'before.' 在他来之前 = 'before he comes.' 先 (xiān) = 'first,' reinforcing the sequence. Compare: 之前 (before) vs 以前 (before/in the past) — 之前 is more precise for sequencing events, while 以前 can also mean 'formerly.' In...之前 patterns: 在吃饭之前洗手 (wash hands before eating), 在出发之前检查一下 (check before departing).

items.question: 我正在吃饭的_____，他来了。
items.translation: (While I was eating, he arrived.)
items.explanation: ...的时候 (de shíhou) means 'when/while' and introduces a time clause. 正在吃饭的时候 = 'while (I was) eating.' 正在 emphasizes the action was in progress. 的时候 works for past, present, or future: 小时候 (when I was little), 考试的时候 (during the exam), 到了的时候 (when you arrive). It's the most versatile time clause connector in Chinese. Compare with 在...的时候 which adds emphasis on the timeframe.

items.question: 直到他解释了我_____明白。
items.translation: (Not until he explained did I understand.)
items.explanation: 直到...才... (zhídào...cái...) means 'not until...then...' 才 indicates the result came late or only after some condition was met. 直到他解释了我才明白 = 'Only after he explained did I understand.' 直到 literally means 'straight until/all the way to.' This is a key pattern for delayed results. Compare: 直到十二点才睡 (didn't sleep until midnight). Without 才, the meaning changes: 直到现在都不知道 (still don't know even now).

items.question: 随着时间的_____，他慢慢习惯了。
items.translation: (As time passed, he gradually got used to it.)
items.explanation: 随着 (suízhe) means 'along with/as,' expressing parallel progression. 随着时间的推移 is a fixed expression meaning 'as time passes.' 推移 (tuīyí) = 'to elapse/shift.' 慢慢 (mànmàn) = 'gradually.' This pattern: 随着 + changing factor + result. Examples: 随着经济的发展 (as the economy develops), 随着年龄的增长 (as age increases). 随着 is formal and commonly used in writing, news, and academic Chinese.

items.question: 每当我想起这件事_____很难过。
items.translation: (Whenever I think of this, I feel very sad.)
items.explanation: 每当...就... (měi dāng...jiù...) means 'whenever/every time.' 每当 is more literary than 每次一, suitable for emotional or reflective contexts. 每当我想起这件事就很难过 = 'Whenever I think of this matter, I feel very sad.' 每当 often appears with emotions: 每当想起你就微笑 (smile whenever I think of you). Compare: 每当 (literary) vs 每次 (conversational) — 每当 implies habitual emotional responses.

items.question: 你什么时候_____好了告诉我一声。
items.translation: (Tell me whenever you're ready.)
items.explanation: 什么时候...好了 combines a time question with a result complement. 准备好了 = 'have prepared/are ready.' This pattern (什么时候 + Verb + 好了) asks 'when will X be done?' and here it's used as a conditional: 'whenever you're ready, let me know.' 告诉我一声 (gàosu wǒ yī shēng) = 'let me know/give me a shout' — 一声 literally means 'one sound' and makes the request casual and polite.

items.question: 从早到_____他都在学习。
items.translation: (From morning to night, he has been studying.)
items.explanation: 从...到... (cóng...dào...) means 'from...to...' for both time and place. 从早到晚 = 'from morning to night/all day long.' 都 emphasizes the action covers the entire duration. Common time patterns: 从小到大 (from childhood to adulthood), 从一月到六月 (from January to June), 从开始到结束 (from start to finish). 一直 can replace 都 for the same meaning: 从早到晚一直在学习.

items.question: 以后有什么问题_____可以找我。
items.translation: (In the future, you can come to me with any questions.)
items.explanation: 以后 (yǐhòu) means 'in the future/afterward.' 什么...都 means 'whatever/any...all.' 有什么问题都可以找我 = 'Whatever questions you have, you can always come to me.' 都 here functions as 'all/in every case,' creating an inclusive, welcoming tone. 找我 = 'find me/come to me.' This is a reassuring, helpful expression. Compare: 什么时候都行 (anytime is fine), 什么都可以 (anything is OK).

items.question: 我昨天睡_____很晚。
items.translation: (I went to sleep very late last night.)
items.explanation: 睡觉 (shuì jiào) is a separable verb (离合词, líhécí). When inserting complements, the verb-object splits: 睡 + complement + 觉. Here 睡得很晚 = 'slept (went to bed) very late.' The 得 introduces a degree/descriptive complement. Separable verbs look like regular verbs but contain a hidden verb-object structure. Other examples: 帮忙 → 帮了个忙 (helped), 游泳 → 游了一个小时的泳 (swam for an hour).

items.question: 你见_____他几次面？
items.translation: (How many times have you met him?)
items.explanation: 见面 (jiàn miàn) splits when adding experience markers or objects. 见过他几次面 = 'have met him several times.' The 过 (experience marker) inserts between 见 and 面, and the person (他) goes between the verb and object. Structure: 见 + 过 + person + frequency + 面. This is a key separable verb pattern. Compare: 见了一面 (met once), 没见过面 (never met). The split follows: Verb + modifier/complement + Object.

items.question: 她每天都_____一个小时的步。
items.translation: (She takes a walk for one hour every day.)
items.explanation: 散步 (sàn bù) = 'take a walk,' splits to insert duration: 散 + duration + 的步. 散一个小时的步 = 'walk for one hour.' The 的 connects the duration to the object 步. This is the standard pattern for inserting duration into separable verbs: Verb + Duration + 的 + Object. Compare: 洗了半个小时的澡 (bathed for half an hour), 跳了两个小时的舞 (danced for two hours). Without splitting, you can't add duration directly.

items.question: 他们结_____十年了。
items.translation: (They have been married for ten years.)
items.explanation: 结婚 (jié hūn) = 'to get married,' a classic separable verb. 结婚十年了 means 'have been married for ten years.' Here it doesn't split because the duration follows the complete verb. But it splits in: 结了两次婚 (married twice), 结过婚 (have been married before), 跟他结的婚 (married him). You cannot say *结婚了他 — the person uses 跟: 跟某人结婚. Key rule: separable verbs cannot take direct objects after them.

items.question: 我帮了他一个大_____。
items.translation: (I did him a big favor.)
items.explanation: 帮忙 (bāng máng) = 'to help/do a favor.' Split form: 帮了他一个大忙 = 'did him a big favor.' The verb 帮 separates from 忙, allowing insertion of the person (他), quantity (一个), and adjective (大). Structure: 帮 + person + 一个 + adj + 忙. Compare: 帮了个小忙 (did a small favor), 帮不了忙 (can't help). 帮忙 is one of the most frequently used separable verbs in daily conversation.

items.question: 她生_____好大的气。
items.translation: (She got very angry.)
items.explanation: 生气 (shēng qì) = 'to get angry,' splits as 生了好大的气. 了 marks completion — the anger happened. 好大的 (hǎo dà de) = 'very big/very much,' intensifying the object 气 (anger/temper). Structure: 生 + 了 + intensifier + 气. Compare: 别生气 (don't be angry), 生他的气 (angry at him), 生了一肚子气 (full of anger — literally 'a belly of anger'). When adding the target of anger: 生谁的气？(angry at whom?).

items.question: 我们握了握_____就走了。
items.translation: (We shook hands briefly and then left.)
items.explanation: 握手 (wò shǒu) = 'shake hands,' separable verb. 握了握手 uses the 'V了V' reduplication pattern meaning a brief or casual action. Structure: Verb + 了 + Verb + Object. This implies the action was quick. Compare: 聊了聊天 (chatted briefly), 跳了跳舞 (danced a bit). The reduplication softens the action, making it sound casual. Without reduplication: 握了手 (shook hands) — neutral completion.

items.question: 你洗_____多长时间的澡？
items.translation: (How long did you shower?)
items.explanation: 洗澡 (xǐ zǎo) = 'to bathe/shower.' Split: 洗了多长时间的澡. 了 marks completed action. Duration question (多长时间) inserts between verb and object. Structure: Verb + 了 + Duration + 的 + Object. Compare: 洗了半个小时的澡 (showered for half an hour). Key rule: with separable verbs, duration must go between verb and object, not after the full verb. Wrong: *洗澡了半个小时. Right: 洗了半个小时的澡.

items.question: 他请_____我们吃饭。
items.translation: (He invited us [asked] to eat.)
items.explanation: 请客 (qǐng kè) = 'to treat someone/be the host.' But here 请 is used independently meaning 'invite/ask.' 请了我们吃饭 = 'invited us to eat.' Note: 请客 splits differently — 请我们的客 means 'treat us.' Compare: 他请客 (he's treating), 请了三次客 (hosted three times). But 请吃饭 is the more natural way to say 'invite to a meal' — here 请 functions as 'invite' rather than the separable verb 请客.

items.question: 别跟他_____气了。
items.translation: (Don't be angry at him anymore.)
items.explanation: 生气 splits to insert the target: 跟他生气 = 'be angry at him.' 别跟他生气了 = 'Don't be angry at him anymore.' The 了 here marks a change of state — stop being angry. Structure for targeting: 跟 + person + 生气. Compare: 别生气了 (stop being angry — no specific target), 你在跟谁生气？(who are you angry at?). The preposition 跟 introduces the person who caused the anger. Common mistake: *生气他 is wrong — must use 跟他生气.

items.question: 我们聊_____一个下午的天。
items.translation: (We chatted for an entire afternoon.)
items.explanation: 聊天 (liáo tiān) = 'to chat,' splits: 聊了一个下午的天 = 'chatted for an entire afternoon.' 了 marks completion, duration (一个下午) inserts between verb and object, with 的 connecting to 天. This follows the standard separable verb duration pattern: V + 了 + Duration + 的 + Object. Compare: 聊了很久的天 (chatted for a long time), 没聊天 (didn't chat — negation keeps it together). In informal speech, 聊了一下午 (without splitting) is also acceptable.

items.question: 你游_____多长时间的泳？
items.translation: (How long did you swim?)
items.explanation: 游泳 (yóu yǒng) = 'to swim,' splits for duration questions: 游了多长时间的泳. Structure: 游 + 了 + duration question + 的 + 泳. Answer: 游了一个小时的泳 (swam for one hour). Compare duration patterns: 游过泳 (have swum before — experience), 游着泳 (swimming — continuous), 没游泳 (didn't swim). Key insight: all VO (verb-object) compound verbs follow this pattern when inserting duration, frequency, or complements.

items.question: 她唱_____几首歌就走了。
items.translation: (She sang a few songs and then left.)
items.explanation: 唱歌 (chàng gē) = 'to sing,' splits: 唱了几首歌. Unlike typical separable verbs, 歌 is replaced by the specific object 几首歌 (a few songs). The measure word 首 (shǒu) is specific to songs and poems. Structure: 唱 + 了 + quantity + measure word + 歌. Compare: 唱了一首很好听的歌 (sang a beautiful song). 就走了 = 'then left' — 就 indicates the action happened soon after. This shows how detail naturally splits VO compounds.

items.question: 他跑_____步跑得很快。
items.translation: (He runs very fast.)
items.explanation: 跑步 (pǎo bù) = 'to run/jog.' Here 跑起步来 would be more natural, but 跑步跑得很快 uses verb repetition: repeat the verb part before 得 complement. Structure: VO + V + 得 + complement. This is the standard way to add descriptive complements to separable verbs. Compare: 唱歌唱得好 (sings well), 跳舞跳得漂亮 (dances beautifully). You repeat only the verb: 跑步跑得快 (runs fast). Wrong: *跑步得很快.

items.question: 你们_____过婚没有？
items.translation: (Have you been married before?)
items.explanation: 结婚 splits for experience: 结过婚没有？= 'Have (you) been married before?' The 过 (experience marker) inserts between 结 and 婚. 没有 forms a yes/no question. Common separable verb experience questions: 见过面没有？(Have you met before?), 吵过架吗？(Have you fought before?), 离过婚吗？(Have you been divorced before?). Answer: 结过 (yes, have been) or 没结过 (no, haven't). The split is mandatory with 过.

items.question: 今天星期_____。
items.translation: (Today is Monday.)
items.explanation: Chinese nominal predicate sentences (名词谓语句) don't need a verb like 是 between subject and predicate when stating dates, ages, weather, or prices. 今天星期一 = 'Today is Monday' — no 是 needed. This is natural Chinese grammar, not an omission. Compare: 今天星期一 (correct) vs 今天是星期一 (acceptable but redundant in spoken Chinese). Other examples: 明天几号？(What date is tomorrow?), 现在三点 (It's three o'clock now).

items.question: 她今年二十_____岁。
items.translation: (She is twenty-five years old this year.)
items.explanation: Age is expressed with nominal predicates: 她今年二十五岁 = 'She is 25 this year.' No 是 needed before the age. 岁 (suì) is the measure word for years of age. Structure: Subject + 今年 + Number + 岁. Common patterns: 你多大？(How old are you? — casual), 您多大年纪？(How old are you? — polite), 他三十多岁 (he's in his thirties). Adding 了 at the end implies a milestone: 他已经三十岁了 (he's already 30).

items.question: 这件衣服三百_____。
items.translation: (This piece of clothing costs 300 yuan.)
items.explanation: Prices use nominal predicates: 这件衣服三百块 = 'This costs 300 kuai.' No verb needed. 块 (kuài) is the informal word for yuan (元). Structure: Item + Price. Common patterns: 这个多少钱？(How much is this?), 苹果五块一斤 (apples are 5 yuan per jin). Price negotiation phrases: 太贵了 (too expensive), 便宜点 (cheaper please). In formal contexts, 元 replaces 块, and 角 replaces 毛 for smaller amounts.

items.question: 明天_____号？
items.translation: (What date is tomorrow?)
items.explanation: Date questions use 几号 (jǐ hào) — 'what date?' No 是 needed. 明天几号？= 'What date is tomorrow?' 几 asks for a small number (typically under 10, but dates are an exception). Compare: 今天几号？(What's today's date?), 今天几月几号？(What month and date is today?). Answer: 明天十五号 (Tomorrow is the 15th). 号 is the spoken form of 日; in formal writing, 日 is preferred: 三月十五日.

items.question: 现在下午两点_____。
items.translation: (It is now 2:30 PM.)
items.explanation: Time expressions are classic nominal predicates: 现在下午两点半 = 'It's 2:30 PM now.' No 是 needed. 半 (bàn) = 'half.' Time vocabulary: 点 (diǎn) = o'clock, 分 (fēn) = minute, 刻 (kè) = quarter. Common times: 三点一刻 (3:15), 差五分六点 (five to six / 5:55). Question: 现在几点？(What time is it?). Adding 了 implies lateness: 已经两点半了 (it's already 2:30).

items.question: 他北京_____。
items.translation: (He is from Beijing.)
items.explanation: Origin uses nominal predicates: 他北京人 = 'He's a Beijing person/He's from Beijing.' No 是 needed in colloquial speech (though 他是北京人 is also correct). Structure: Subject + Place + 人. This pattern is very common: 你哪里人？(Where are you from?), 我上海人 (I'm from Shanghai). 人 functions as both 'person' and a nominalizer for origin. In formal speech, 是 is usually included: 他是北京人.

items.question: 今天_____天。
items.translation: (Today is a sunny day.)
items.explanation: Weather descriptions can be nominal predicates: 今天晴天 = 'Today is a sunny day.' 晴天 (qíng tiān) = 'sunny day.' Other weather nouns: 阴天 (yīn tiān, cloudy day), 雨天 (yǔ tiān, rainy day), 雪天 (xuě tiān, snowy day). Compare verb form: 今天下雨 (it's raining today — uses verb 下). The nominal form sounds more like a weather report, while the verb form describes ongoing weather. Both are natural and common.

items.question: 这个苹果五块钱一_____。
items.translation: (These apples are five yuan per catty.)
items.explanation: Unit pricing uses nominal predicates: 五块钱一斤 = 'five yuan per catty (jin).' 斤 (jīn) is a Chinese weight unit ≈ 500g. Structure: Price + 一 + Unit. Common market phrases: 多少钱一斤？(How much per jin?), 十块钱三个 (10 yuan for three), 两块一瓶 (two yuan per bottle). No verb is needed — the price-unit relationship is self-evident in Chinese. This is essential vocabulary for markets and grocery shopping.

items.question: 她大_____。
items.translation: (She has big eyes.)
items.explanation: Physical descriptions can use nominal predicates: 她大眼睛 = 'She has big eyes' (literally 'She big-eyes'). This is a compact, descriptive style common in Chinese. Structure: Subject + Adj + Body part. Compare: 他长头发 (he has long hair), 她高个子 (she's tall). The more formal version uses 有: 她有一双大眼睛 (she has a pair of big eyes). The nominal predicate version is more vivid and commonly used in informal descriptions or literature.

items.question: 一共_____块钱。
items.translation: (The total is fifty yuan.)
items.explanation: Totals use nominal predicates: 一共五十块钱 = 'The total is fifty yuan.' 一共 (yīgòng) = 'in total/altogether.' No verb needed. Common shopping phrases: 一共多少钱？(How much altogether?), 找你十块 (here's your 10 yuan change — also a nominal predicate). 找 (zhǎo) here means 'to give change.' Structure: 一共 + amount. In receipts and formal contexts: 合计五十元整 (total: 50 yuan exactly).

items.question: 从这儿到学校十分_____。
items.translation: (From here to school is ten minutes.)
items.explanation: Distance/duration between places uses nominal predicates: 从这儿到学校十分钟 = 'From here to school is 10 minutes.' 分钟 (fēnzhōng) = 'minute.' No 是 needed. Structure: 从A到B + time/distance. Compare: 从这儿到机场一个小时 (one hour from here to the airport), 从北京到上海两个小时 (two hours from Beijing to Shanghai). You can add travel method: 坐地铁十分钟 (10 minutes by subway).

items.question: 我们班三十_____同学。
items.translation: (Our class has thirty-two classmates.)
items.explanation: Quantity descriptions can be nominal predicates: 我们班三十二个同学 = 'Our class (has) 32 classmates.' No verb 有 is strictly needed in this structure. Structure: Group/place + quantity + members. Compare: 我们家四口人 (our family has four people), 这个学校两千个学生 (this school has 2000 students). The fuller version with 有 is equally correct: 我们班有三十二个同学. The nominal form is more concise.

items.question: 这个房间二十_____平米。
items.translation: (This room is twenty square meters.)
items.explanation: Measurements use nominal predicates: 这个房间二十平米 = 'This room is 20 square meters.' 平米 (píng mǐ) = 'square meter' — short for 平方米. No verb needed. Measurement vocabulary: 公里/千米 (kilometer), 厘米 (centimeter), 公斤 (kilogram). Structure: Object + Measurement. Compare: 这条路三公里 (this road is 3 km), 他身高一米八 (he's 1.8m tall). Chinese frequently uses approximate measures: 二十多平米 (20-something sqm).

items.question: 他_____性格很好。
items.translation: (He has a good personality.)
items.explanation: Nominal predicates can describe characteristics: 他这个人性格很好 = 'He (this person) has a great personality.' 这个人 (zhège rén) acts as an appositive, adding emphasis. Structure: Subject + 这个人 + characteristic. Compare: 她这个人很热心 (she's a warm-hearted person), 你这个人太认真了 (you are too serious as a person). This construction carries slight emphasis or evaluation. Without 这个人, it's simpler: 他性格很好.

items.question: 昨天最高气温三十五_____。
items.translation: (Yesterday's highest temperature was 35 degrees.)
items.explanation: Temperature readings use nominal predicates: 最高气温三十五度 = 'highest temperature 35 degrees.' 度 (dù) = 'degree.' No 是 needed. Weather report vocabulary: 最高气温 (highest temperature), 最低气温 (lowest temperature), 零下 (below zero). Examples: 今天零下五度 (today is -5°C), 气温三十度左右 (around 30°). Adding 摄氏 (shèshì) specifies Celsius: 摄氏三十五度. This is standard Chinese weather broadcast style.

items.question: 他大概_____二十岁。
items.translation: (He is approximately twenty years old.)
items.explanation: 大概 (dàgài) = 'approximately/probably.' 大概有二十岁 = 'approximately 20 years old.' 有 here means 'to have reached (an age/amount).' 大概 is the most common way to express approximation. It can precede verbs: 大概需要两个小时 (probably needs 2 hours), or stand alone: 大概吧 (probably). Compare: 大概 (approx.), 大约 (roughly — slightly more formal), 差不多 (more or less). All three are interchangeable in most contexts.

items.question: 这儿到火车站大约十_____公里。
items.translation: (From here to the train station is about 10-something kilometers.)
items.explanation: 十几 (shí jǐ) = 'ten-something/10-19.' 几 after a tens digit creates an open range: 二十几 (20-something), 三十几 (30-something). This is extremely common in Chinese approximation. Compare: 几 (a few, under 10), 十几 (10-19), 二十几 (20-29). 大约 (dàyuē) is slightly more formal than 大概. You can combine: 大约十几个人来了 (about 10-something people came). 几 only works after tens/hundreds, not alone with large numbers.

items.question: 来了三十_____人。
items.translation: (About thirty-some people came.)
items.explanation: 多 (duō) after a number means 'more than/over.' 三十多人 = 'over thirty people / thirty-some people.' Rules: after round numbers (十多, 二十多, 一百多) it means 'X-plus.' 三十多 implies 31-39. After non-round numbers, 多 goes after the measure word: 三个多小时 (over three hours). Key distinction: 三十多个人 (30+ people) vs 三个多人 (more than 3 people — unusual). 多 is the most versatile approximation word in Chinese.

items.question: 他们等了_____一个小时。
items.translation: (They waited for about an hour.)
items.explanation: 差不多 (chàbuduō) = 'almost/about/more or less.' 差不多一个小时 = 'about an hour.' Literally means 'the difference is not much.' 差不多 is versatile: for quantity (差不多一百人, about 100 people), quality (差不多一样, pretty much the same), completion (差不多了, almost done). Compare: 差不多 emphasizes proximity to a value, while 大概 emphasizes uncertainty. In casual speech, 差不多 is one of the most useful approximation expressions.

items.question: 这个包_____百块钱。
items.translation: (This bag costs about a hundred yuan.)
items.explanation: 来 (lái) after a number means 'approximately.' 一百来块钱 = 'about a hundred yuan.' 来 implies slightly more or less than the stated number. Structure: Number + 来 + Measure Word. Examples: 十来个人 (about 10 people), 二十来岁 (around 20 years old), 三十来天 (about 30 days). 来 is more colloquial than 大概 or 大约. It gives a relaxed, conversational tone. Range: typically ±10-20% of the stated number.

items.question: 我们走了两三_____路。
items.translation: (We walked two to three li of road.)
items.explanation: Two consecutive numbers express approximation: 两三 = 'two or three/a couple.' 里 (lǐ) is a Chinese unit of distance (≈500m). Structure: Number1 + Number2 (consecutive) + Measure Word. Common: 三四个 (3 or 4), 五六天 (5 or 6 days), 七八岁 (7 or 8 years old), 十来二十个 is wrong — numbers must be consecutive. This is one of the most natural Chinese approximation patterns, widely used in speech and writing alike.

items.question: 大约需要_____的时间。
items.translation: (It takes approximately half a year.)
items.explanation: 大约 (dàyuē) = 'approximately,' more formal than 大概. 大约需要半年的时间 = 'It takes approximately half a year.' 半年 (bàn nián) = 'half a year.' 的时间 makes it explicit that you're referring to a time period. Compare: 大约半年 (about half a year — concise), 大约需要半年的时间 (it takes about half a year — formal). Common time approximations: 半个月 (half a month), 半天 (half a day), 一年半 (a year and a half).

items.question: 他_____四十岁左右。
items.translation: (He is around forty years old.)
items.explanation: 左右 (zuǒyòu) = 'around/approximately' (literally 'left-right'). 有四十岁左右 = 'is around 40 years old.' 左右 goes after the number/quantity. Structure: Number + 左右. Examples: 三点左右 (around 3 o'clock), 一百块左右 (around 100 yuan), 两个小时左右 (around 2 hours). 左右 implies equal possibility of being slightly above or below. Compare: 多 (at least that much), 左右 (could be above or below), 差不多 (more or less).

items.question: 这个城市_____有一千万人口。
items.translation: (This city has nearly ten million people.)
items.explanation: 将近 (jiāngjìn) = 'nearly/close to.' 将近一千万 = 'nearly ten million.' 将近 emphasizes approaching but not reaching a number — slightly below. Compare: 将近 (just under), 超过 (exceeding/over), 接近 (approaching/nearing). Examples: 将近一百人 (nearly 100 people), 将近十年了 (nearly 10 years). 将近 is more formal/written; in speech, 差不多 or 快 (kuài, soon/almost) serve similar functions: 快到一千万了 (almost at 10 million).

items.question: 他每天锻炼一个小时_____。
items.translation: (He exercises for about an hour every day.)
items.explanation: 左右 after a time duration: 一个小时左右 = 'about an hour.' This is the most natural position for 左右. Compare placement: 三十岁左右 (around 30), 下午两点左右 (around 2 PM), 一百米左右 (about 100 meters). Formally, 上下 (shàngxià) can replace 左右 for ages: 四十上下 (around 40). 以上 (yǐshàng) = 'above/over,' 以下 (yǐxià) = 'below/under' — these are exact bounds, not approximations.

items.question: 这本书_____两百页。
items.translation: (This book is about two hundred pages.)
items.explanation: 大概 before a number: 大概两百页 = 'approximately 200 pages.' 页 (yè) = 'page.' 大概 can be placed before or within the sentence. Compare positions: 这本书大概两百页 (this book is about 200 pages) = 大概这本书两百页 (approximately, this book is 200 pages). For books: 页 (page), 章 (chapter), 篇 (article), 段 (paragraph). All work with approximation: 大概有十几章 (about 10-something chapters).

items.question: 班里有四十_____个学生。
items.translation: (There are about forty students in the class.)
items.explanation: 来 after a round number: 四十来个学生 = 'about 40 students.' The 来 approximation marker goes between the number and the measure word. Position rule: Number + 来 + MW + Noun. Compare: 十来个 (about 10), 百来个 (about 100), but not *千来个 (wrong — 来 typically works with numbers up to hundreds). For thousands: use 大概/大约/差不多 instead. 来 gives a casual, conversational feel suitable for everyday speech.

items.question: 他身高一米七_____。
items.translation: (He is about 1.7-something meters tall.)
items.explanation: 几 approximates the unknown digit: 一米七几 = '1.7-something meters' (between 1.70 and 1.79). This is the standard way to give approximate heights in Chinese: 一米八几 (1.8-something), 一米六几 (1.6-something). Height expressions: 他多高？(How tall is he?), 身高一米七五 (1.75m tall). Chinese typically uses meters and centimeters. 几 here fills in for an unknown last digit, making it a precise type of approximation.

items.question: 上下班_____要一个小时。
items.translation: (The commute probably takes about an hour.)
items.explanation: 估计 (gūjì) = 'estimate/probably.' 估计要一个小时 = 'I estimate it takes about an hour.' 估计 adds a subjective estimation element. Compare approximation words: 大概 (approximately — neutral), 估计 (I estimate — subjective), 大约 (roughly — formal), 差不多 (more or less — casual). 估计 often implies personal judgment: 估计他不会来了 (I reckon he won't come). It bridges approximation and prediction.

items.question: 会议开了两个_____小时。
items.translation: (The meeting lasted a little over two hours.)
items.explanation: 两个多小时 = 'a little over two hours.' When 多 comes after a measure word (个), it means 'a bit more than.' Rule: Number + MW + 多 + Noun = slightly over X. Compare: 三个多月 (a bit over 3 months), 五个多小时 (a bit over 5 hours). But: 三十多个 (30-something — 多 before MW means a larger range). Position matters: 两个多小时 (2+ hours) vs 二十多个小时 (20+ hours). This placement rule is critical for correct approximation.

items.question: 他_____在看书。
items.translation: (He is still reading.)
items.explanation: 还 (hái) = 'still,' indicating an action continues from before. 还在看书 = 'still reading.' 还在 + Verb is the standard 'still doing' pattern. Compare: 还 (still — ongoing), 又 (again — repeated past), 再 (again — future). Examples: 还在下雨 (still raining), 还在睡觉 (still sleeping), 还没吃 (still haven't eaten). 还 can also mean 'also/in addition': 他还会说法语 (he also speaks French). Context determines the meaning.

items.question: 请继续_____下去。
items.translation: (Please continue speaking.)
items.explanation: 继续 (jìxù) = 'to continue.' 继续说下去 = 'continue speaking.' 下去 (xiàqu) is a directional complement meaning 'to continue/go on' when used figuratively. Structure: 继续 + Verb + 下去. Examples: 继续学下去 (continue studying), 继续做下去 (continue doing), 继续走下去 (continue walking). Without 下去: 继续说 also works but 下去 adds emphasis on sustained continuation. 请 makes it a polite request.

items.question: 雨一直_____到晚上。
items.translation: (The rain continued until evening.)
items.explanation: 一直 (yīzhí) = 'continuously/all along.' 一直下到晚上 = 'rained continuously until evening.' 一直...到 = 'continuously until.' 下雨 uses 下 (xià) as the verb. Structure: Subject + 一直 + Verb + 到 + Time. Examples: 一直工作到深夜 (worked until late at night), 一直等到他来 (waited until he came). 一直 emphasizes unbroken continuation. Compare: 一直 (continuously) vs 一向 (always/habitually) — 一直 is duration, 一向 is habit.

items.question: 你应该_____下去，别放弃。
items.translation: (You should keep going, don't give up.)
items.explanation: 坚持 (jiānchí) = 'to persist/insist/keep going.' 坚持下去 = 'keep persisting.' 下去 adds directional continuation — 'keep going forward.' 别放弃 (bié fàngqì) = 'don't give up.' Structure: 坚持 + Verb/下去. Examples: 坚持学习 (persist in studying), 坚持锻炼 (keep exercising). 坚持 implies effort against difficulty. Compare: 继续 (continue — neutral), 坚持 (persist — implies determination or difficulty). 坚持下去 is encouraging and motivational.

items.question: 他_____说着，没有停。
items.translation: (He kept talking, without stopping.)
items.explanation: 一直说着 combines 一直 (continuously) with 着 (ongoing state marker). Double continuation emphasis. 着 marks the action as ongoing, 一直 emphasizes it never stopped. 没有停 (méiyǒu tíng) = 'didn't stop,' reinforcing the meaning. Structure: 一直 + Verb + 着. Examples: 一直站着 (standing the whole time), 一直笑着 (kept smiling), 一直开着灯 (the light stayed on). This pattern describes sustained states rather than momentary actions.

items.question: 她从早上_____现在都没休息。
items.translation: (She hasn't rested from morning until now.)
items.explanation: 从...到... (cóng...dào...) = 'from...to...' expressing continuous duration. 从早上到现在 = 'from morning until now.' 都没休息 = 'hasn't rested at all.' 都 (dōu) here emphasizes the totality — not even once. Structure: 从 + Start Time + 到 + End Time + 都 + Neg + Verb. Examples: 从星期一到星期五都要上班 (work Monday through Friday). Adding 一直 intensifies: 从早上到现在一直都没休息.

items.question: 这首歌一直_____着。
items.translation: (This song has been playing continuously.)
items.explanation: 放 (fàng) = 'to play (music/video).' 一直放着 = 'has been playing continuously.' 着 marks ongoing state. For music: 放歌 (play songs), 放音乐 (play music). 放着 implies the music is on and continues playing. Structure: 一直 + Verb + 着. Compare: 放了 (played — completed), 放着 (playing — ongoing), 在放 (is playing — right now). The 着 version emphasizes the state continues over a period of time.

items.question: 别停，_____做。
items.translation: (Don't stop, keep doing it.)
items.explanation: 接着 (jiēzhe) = 'continue/carry on/then.' 别停，接着做 = 'Don't stop, keep doing it.' 接着 implies continuation without interruption. Compare: 接着 (continue without pause), 继续 (continue — may imply a pause first), 然后 (then — sequential). Examples: 接着说 (carry on speaking), 他走了，我接着做 (he left, I continued doing it). 接着 can also mean 'then/next': 先吃饭，接着去散步 (eat first, then go for a walk).

items.question: 他_____住在这里。
items.translation: (He still lives here.)
items.explanation: 还住在 = 'still lives at.' 还 indicates a state that has continued from the past to the present. 还 + Verb (+ 在) is the standard 'still doing/being' structure. Compare: 还住在这里 (still lives here — state continues) vs 还在这里住 (still living here — action focus). Negation: 不再住在这里了 (no longer lives here). Question: 他还住在这里吗？(Does he still live here?). 还 in continuation often pairs with expectation — implying 'contrary to what you might think.'

items.question: 外面的雪还没_____呢。
items.translation: (The snow outside hasn't stopped yet.)
items.explanation: 还没停 = 'hasn't stopped yet.' 还没 + Verb = 'still haven't / not yet.' 呢 (ne) at the end adds emphasis and slight surprise that the state continues. Structure: 还没 + Verb + 呢. Examples: 还没吃呢 (haven't eaten yet), 还没到呢 (haven't arrived yet), 还没做完呢 (haven't finished yet). Compare: 还没停 (hasn't stopped — continuation in negative) vs 还在下 (still falling — continuation in positive). Both describe ongoing snow.

items.question: 我会一直_____你的。
items.translation: (I will always support you.)
items.explanation: 一直支持 = 'always support/continuously support.' 一直 here projects into the future — 'I will keep supporting you from now on.' 的 at the end adds certainty and emphasis (like a promise). Structure: 会 + 一直 + Verb + 你的. Compare: 会一直等你 (will always wait for you), 会一直陪着你 (will always be with you). These are common in emotional expressions. 一直 covers past-to-present (一直在努力) and present-to-future (会一直支持你).

items.question: 他不停地_____着头。
items.translation: (He kept nodding his head non-stop.)
items.explanation: 不停地 (bùtíng de) = 'non-stop/incessantly.' 点头 (diǎn tóu) = 'nod.' 不停地点着头 = 'kept nodding non-stop.' 地 (de) connects the adverb to the verb. 着 marks ongoing action. Structure: 不停地 + Verb + 着 + Object. Compare: 不停地说 (talking non-stop), 不停地笑 (laughing non-stop). Similar patterns: 不断地 (unceasingly — more formal), 老是 (always — with annoyance), 总是 (always — neutral). 不停 literally means 'not stopping.'

items.question: 我们已经等了他_____个小时了。
items.translation: (We have already been waiting for him for two hours.)
items.explanation: 已经...了...了 double 了 pattern emphasizes duration up to now. 已经等了他两个小时了 = 'have already been waiting for him for 2 hours.' First 了 marks the completed portion of waiting, second 了 indicates the situation continues to the present. Structure: 已经 + Verb + 了 + Duration + 了. This pattern implies frustration or emphasis on elapsed time. Compare: 等了两个小时 (waited 2 hours — may be over) vs 已经等了两个小时了 (have been waiting for 2 hours — still waiting).

items.question: 这个习惯他_____了很多年。
items.translation: (He has kept this habit for many years.)
items.explanation: 保持 (bǎochí) = 'to maintain/keep.' 保持了很多年 = 'maintained for many years.' 了 + duration marks how long the action has lasted. Structure: Verb + 了 + Duration. Compare: 保持 (maintain — implies effort), 坚持 (persist — implies difficulty), 维持 (sustain — more formal). Examples: 保持健康 (maintain health), 保持联系 (keep in touch), 保持安静 (keep quiet). 保持 focuses on keeping a state unchanged over time.

items.question: 这个项目还要_____做两个月。
items.translation: (This project still needs to continue for two more months.)
items.explanation: 继续做两个月 = 'continue doing for two more months.' 还要 (hái yào) = 'still need to,' reinforcing ongoing requirement. Structure: 还要 + 继续 + Verb + Duration.
