Compare continuation words: 继续 (continue — neutral, can resume), 接着 (carry on — no pause), 坚持 (persist — with difficulty), 一直 (continuously — never stopped). 还要 adds 'additionally/still' — implying the work extends beyond what was expected: 还要再等一个小时 (still need to wait another hour).

items.question: 李_____，您好！
items.translation: (Hello, Teacher Li!)
items.explanation: 老师 (lǎoshī) = 'teacher' — used as a respectful title after a surname. 李老师 (Teacher Li) is the standard way to address teachers, professors, and instructors. In Chinese culture, 老师 shows deep respect and is even used beyond schools — for mentors, coaches, and respected experts. The pattern is always Surname + Title: 王老师, 张老师. Never say 老师李 — the order is fixed.

items.question: 请问，_____贵姓？
items.translation: (May I ask, what is your honorable surname?)
items.explanation: 您 (nín) is the polite/formal 'you,' used when addressing elders, superiors, or strangers respectfully. 您贵姓 is the most formal way to ask someone's surname. Compare: 你叫什么名字？(casual) vs. 您贵姓？(formal). 贵 (guì) literally means 'noble/expensive' — 贵姓 = 'noble surname.' The response is always humble: 免贵姓王 (My humble surname is Wang — literally 'no need for honor').

items.question: 王_____，这份合同请您过目。
items.translation: (Mr. Wang, please review this contract.)
items.explanation: 先生 (xiānsheng) = 'Mr./Sir' — the standard formal address for men in business and professional settings. 王先生 = Mr. Wang. This is the most common polite address in modern Chinese. In formal correspondence and business, 先生 is essential. Historical note: 先生 originally meant 'born earlier/senior' — showing respect through age. For women: 女士 (nǚshì, Ms.) or 小姐 (xiǎojiě, Miss — now context-dependent).

items.question: _____，请问地铁站怎么走？
items.translation: (Excuse me, how do I get to the subway station?)
items.explanation: 请问 (qǐng wèn) = 'may I ask' — the standard polite opener when addressing strangers for directions or information. It's more polite than 喂 (wèi, 'hello/hey' — mainly for phone calls). 请问 combines 请 (please) + 问 (ask). Common patterns: 请问洗手间在哪里？(Where's the restroom?), 请问几点了？(What time is it?). Always start with 请问 when approaching strangers — it signals respect.

items.question: 张_____，您的快递到了。
items.translation: (Master Zhang, your delivery has arrived.)
items.explanation: 师傅 (shīfu) = 'master/skilled worker' — used for taxi drivers, repairmen, delivery workers, and craftspeople. 张师傅 is polite and friendly. Originally 师傅 meant 'master craftsman' — now it's the go-to respectful address for service workers. Don't confuse with 师父 (shīfù, martial arts master/religious master). In daily life: 师傅，去机场 (Driver, to the airport), 师傅，这个能修吗？(Master, can you fix this?).

items.question: 小王，帮我_____这个文件。
items.translation: (Xiao Wang, help me print this document.)
items.explanation: 小 (xiǎo) + surname is an informal, friendly address for younger colleagues or juniors. 小王 = 'young Wang/little Wang.' This pattern shows familiarity without disrespect. The opposite is 老 (lǎo) + surname for older colleagues: 老王 (Old Wang — not offensive, shows warmth). Age-based: 小李 (younger), 老李 (older). These are very common in workplaces and among friends. Never use 小 for superiors!

items.question: 各位_____，欢迎来到北京。
items.translation: (Dear guests, welcome to Beijing.)
items.explanation: 来宾 (láibīn) = 'guests/visitors' — formal address for audiences at events. 各位来宾 = 'distinguished guests.' 各位 (gèwèi) means 'everyone/each of you' — a formal collective address. Common formal openings: 各位来宾 (guests), 各位同事 (colleagues), 各位同学 (classmates/students), 各位领导 (leaders). 各位 is never used casually — it signals formal occasions, speeches, and announcements.

items.question: 阿姨，请给我一碗_____。
items.translation: (Auntie, please give me a bowl of noodles.)
items.explanation: 阿姨 (āyí) = 'auntie' — used to address women roughly your parents' age, especially in restaurants, markets, and daily interactions. It's warm and respectful. Chinese culture extends family terms to strangers: 叔叔 (shūshu, uncle — older men), 爷爷 (yéye, grandpa), 奶奶 (nǎinai, grandma). Using family terms for strangers shows respect and closeness — a uniquely Chinese social custom.

items.question: 尊敬的_____，感谢您的来信。
items.translation: (Respected customer, thank you for your letter.)
items.explanation: 尊敬的 (zūnjìng de) = 'respected/dear' — the formal salutation for letters, emails, and official documents. 尊敬的客户 = 'Dear Customer.' This is essential in business Chinese. Compare formality levels: 亲爱的 (qīn'ài de, 'dear' — intimate, for friends/family), 尊敬的 (respected — formal business). In emails: 尊敬的王经理 (Dear Manager Wang). Never mix 亲爱的 in business correspondence!

items.question: 同学们，请_____课本第五页。
items.translation: (Students, please turn to page 5 of the textbook.)
items.explanation: 同学们 (tóngxuémen) = 'students/classmates' — standard classroom address. 翻到 (fān dào) = 'turn/flip to.' Teachers always say 同学们 to address the class. The 们 suffix makes it plural. Variations: 同学 (singular — addressing one student), 各位同学 (more formal). Other classroom phrases: 请举手 (raise your hand), 请安静 (be quiet), 下课了 (class dismissed).

items.question: 大家_____，我是你们的新老师。
items.translation: (Hello everyone, I am your new teacher.)
items.explanation: 大家好 (dàjiā hǎo) = 'hello everyone' — the universal group greeting. 大家 (everyone) is less formal than 各位 but more inclusive. Perfect for presentations, introductions, and casual group settings. Variations by time: 大家早上好 (good morning everyone), 大家下午好 (good afternoon everyone). 大家 can also mean 'everybody' in requests: 大家请坐 (everyone please sit), 大家注意 (everyone pay attention).

items.question: 陈_____，会议几点开始？
items.translation: (Manager Chen, what time does the meeting start?)
items.explanation: 经理 (jīnglǐ) = 'manager' — a professional title used after surnames. 陈经理 = Manager Chen. In Chinese business, you ALWAYS use Surname + Title: 王总 (General Manager Wang), 李主任 (Director Li), 赵局长 (Bureau Chief Zhao). Using titles shows respect for hierarchy. Never call your boss by first name alone in Chinese culture — it's considered extremely rude. Even close colleagues often use titles.

items.question: 亲爱的_____，生日快乐！
items.translation: (Dear mom, happy birthday!)
items.explanation: 亲爱的 (qīn'ài de) = 'dear/beloved' — used for close family and intimate relationships. 亲爱的妈妈 = 'dear mom.' This is the warm, emotional version of address — only for people you love. Family terms in Chinese: 爸爸 (bàba, dad), 妈妈 (māma, mom), 哥哥 (gēge, older brother), 姐姐 (jiějie, older sister), 弟弟 (dìdi, younger brother), 妹妹 (mèimei, younger sister). These double-syllable forms are affectionate.

items.question: 老板，_____多少钱？
items.translation: (Boss, how much is this?)
items.explanation: 老板 (lǎobǎn) = 'boss' — commonly used to address shop owners, restaurant owners, and vendors at markets. It's casual but respectful. At a market: 老板，便宜点 (Boss, make it cheaper). 老板娘 (lǎobǎnniáng) = 'boss's wife/female boss' — used for women running shops. This usage of 老板 is very colloquial and specific to commercial interactions — you wouldn't use it in corporate settings where 经理 or 总 are appropriate.

items.question: 女士们，先生们，_____开始。
items.translation: (Ladies and gentlemen, let's begin.)
items.explanation: 女士们先生们 (nǚshìmen xiānshengmen) = 'ladies and gentlemen' — the most formal group address for ceremonies, speeches, and official events. Note the order: 女士 (ladies) comes first in Chinese etiquette — '女士优先' (ladies first). This phrase is borrowed from Western convention but fully adopted. In ultra-formal Chinese: 各位领导、各位来宾 (leaders and guests). Modern events may use: 各位嘉宾 (honored guests).

items.question: _____，我能问你一个问题吗？
items.translation: (Excuse me, may I ask you a question?)
items.explanation: 打扰了 (dǎrǎo le) = 'sorry to bother/excuse me' — a polite phrase before making a request or interruption. It literally means 'I've disturbed you.' Chinese politeness relies heavily on acknowledging the imposition. Similar phrases: 不好意思 (bùhǎoyìsi, 'embarrassed to ask/sorry'), 麻烦你 (máfan nǐ, 'trouble you'). These 'pre-apology' phrases are essential — they soften requests and show social awareness.

items.question: 麻烦您帮我_____一下。
items.translation: (Could you please help me take a look?)
items.explanation: 麻烦您 (máfan nín) = 'trouble you/please' — one of the most useful polite request phrases. 麻烦您帮我看一下 = 'please help me take a look.' The structure 麻烦 + person + verb makes any request polite. Examples: 麻烦你递给我 (please pass it to me), 麻烦您签个字 (please sign here). Adding 一下 (yīxià) softens the verb — it's like adding 'just a bit.' 看一下 sounds gentler than 看.

items.question: 没关系，不用_____。
items.translation: (It's okay, no need to be polite.)
items.explanation: 不用客气 (bùyòng kèqi) = 'no need to be polite/you're welcome.' This is the standard response to 谢谢 (thank you). 客气 (kèqi) means 'polite/courteous.' Variations: 不客气 (you're welcome — most common), 别客气 (don't be polite), 太客气了 (you're too polite — when someone gives a gift). Chinese courtesy involves a ritual of offering, refusing, insisting, and accepting — 客气 captures this cultural dance.

items.question: 让您_____了，真不好意思。
items.translation: (I made you wait, really sorry about that.)
items.explanation: 让您久等了 (ràng nín jiǔ děng le) = 'sorry to have kept you waiting.' This is a standard apology phrase in service, business, and social contexts. 久等 = 'wait a long time.' Even if someone waited only 2 minutes, saying this shows courtesy. Related phrases: 让您费心了 (troubled you), 让您操心了 (made you worry). The 让 + person + result pattern expresses 'I caused you to...' — a humble acknowledgment.

items.question: 请_____，不要站着。
items.translation: (Please sit down, don't stand.)
items.explanation: 请坐 (qǐng zuò) = 'please sit down' — essential hospitality phrase. Chinese hosts ALWAYS say 请坐 when guests arrive. 请 (qǐng) = 'please/to invite' — the cornerstone of polite Chinese. Common 请 phrases: 请进 (please come in), 请喝茶 (please have tea), 请用 (please help yourself to food), 请说 (please speak). The 请 + verb pattern makes ANY action polite and welcoming.

items.question: 哪里哪里，您_____了。
items.translation: (Not at all, you flatter me.)
items.explanation: 过奖了 (guòjiǎng le) = 'you overpraise/flatter me' — the classic humble response to compliments. Chinese culture values humility deeply — directly accepting praise is considered arrogant. 哪里哪里 (nǎli nǎli) = 'where, where' — another humble deflection meaning 'not at all.' Other humble responses: 不敢当 (I don't deserve it), 还差得远 (still far from it), 一般般 (just so-so). This humility ritual is a core part of Chinese social interaction.

items.question: 感谢您的_____，非常感激。
items.translation: (Thank you for your help, very grateful.)
items.explanation: 感谢 (gǎnxiè) = 'thank/be grateful' — more formal than 谢谢 (xièxie). 感激 (gǎnjī) = 'deeply grateful' — even more intense. Gratitude scale in Chinese: 谢谢 (casual thanks) → 非常感谢 (many thanks) → 感激不尽 (endlessly grateful) → 铭记于心 (engraved in my heart). In formal letters: 衷心感谢 (heartfelt thanks). For small favors: 谢谢 suffices. For major help: use 感激.

items.question: 对不起，是我的_____。
items.translation: (I'm sorry, it's my fault.)
items.explanation: 对不起 (duìbuqǐ) = 'sorry' — literally 'can't face you.' 是我的错 = 'it's my fault.' Chinese apology phrases: 对不起 (I'm sorry — genuine), 不好意思 (sorry/embarrassed — lighter), 抱歉 (apologetic — formal), 请原谅 (please forgive — serious). In daily life, 不好意思 is used far more than 对不起 — it covers minor inconveniences without the weight of a full apology. Reserve 对不起 for real mistakes.

items.question: 请多多_____。
items.translation: (Please give me your guidance/support.)
items.explanation: 请多多关照 (qǐng duōduō guānzhào) = 'please take care of me/please look after me' — said when meeting new colleagues, starting a new job, or entering a new group. This phrase shows humility and willingness to learn. It's borrowed from Japanese culture (よろしくお願いします) but widely used in Chinese. Variations: 请多指教 (please give me guidance), 请多包涵 (please be understanding/forgive my mistakes). These are essential for first impressions.

items.question: 不好意思，能不能_____一下位置？
items.translation: (Excuse me, could you switch seats?)
items.explanation: 不好意思 (bùhǎoyìsi) = 'excuse me/sorry to bother' — the most versatile polite phrase in daily Chinese. It's softer than 对不起 and perfect for small requests. 换一下 = 'switch for a moment.' The 能不能 (néng bù néng) pattern makes requests more polite: 能不能帮我...? (could you help me...?). Even more polite: 能不能麻烦您...? Adding layers of politeness is a Chinese communication specialty.

items.question: 您先请，_____。
items.translation: (After you, please.)
items.explanation: 您先请 (nín xiān qǐng) = 'you first/after you' — used when yielding to someone at doors, elevators, or queues. Adding 我不急 (I'm not in a hurry) reinforces the courtesy. Chinese etiquette emphasizes 让 (ràng, yielding): 让座 (give up your seat on the bus), 让路 (make way). Elders and guests always go first. In elevators: 您先请 + hold the door. This small gesture carries significant social meaning.

items.question: 辛苦了，请喝杯_____。
items.translation: (You've worked hard, please have a cup of tea.)
items.explanation: 辛苦了 (xīnkǔ le) = 'you've worked hard/thank you for your effort' — said to acknowledge someone's hard work. This is deeply Chinese — recognizing effort rather than just results. 请喝茶 (please have tea) is the classic hospitality gesture. Chinese tea culture is central to politeness: offering tea = showing respect. In business meetings, tea is always served first. The phrase 辛苦了 is used daily — to delivery people, colleagues, taxi drivers, anyone working hard.

items.question: 慢走，_____再来。
items.translation: (Take care, come again.)
items.explanation: 慢走 (mànzǒu) = 'walk slowly/take care' — said to departing guests. It literally means 'go slowly' but conveys 'be careful/farewell.' 欢迎再来 (welcome to come again) is the standard farewell for shops and restaurants. Chinese farewells: 再见 (goodbye), 慢走 (take care — host says this), 路上小心 (be careful on the road), 一路平安 (safe journey). The host ALWAYS sees the guest to the door — 送客 (seeing off guests) is essential etiquette.

items.question: 初次见面，请多_____。
items.translation: (First time meeting, please bear with me/guide me.)
items.explanation: 初次见面，请多指教 (chūcì jiànmiàn, qǐng duō zhǐjiào) = 'first meeting, please give me your guidance' — the formal self-introduction closer. 指教 (zhǐjiào) = 'instruct/guide' — using this elevates the other person while humbling yourself. This is essential in professional Chinese. In contrast, casual introductions: 你好，我叫小明 (Hi, I'm Xiao Ming). The formal version adds gravitas and respect, perfect for business dinners and official introductions.

items.question: 承蒙_____，不胜感激。
items.translation: (Thanks to your care, I am deeply grateful.)
items.explanation: 承蒙关照 (chéngméng guānzhào) = 'thanks to your care/patronage' — highly formal gratitude used in business letters, speeches, and formal occasions. 不胜感激 (bùshèng gǎnjī) = 'overwhelmingly grateful.' These are literary-level (书面语) expressions. In formal writing: 承蒙厚爱 (thanks to your generous love), 承蒙指导 (thanks to your guidance). The 承蒙...不胜... pattern is one of the most elegant Chinese gratitude structures — memorize it for formal Chinese.

items.question: 本公司_____于2010年成立。
items.translation: (This company was established in 2010.)
items.explanation: 本 (běn) = 'this (formal)' — used in formal writing instead of 这个. 本公司 = 'this company (our company),' 本人 = 'this person (myself),' 本校 = 'this school.' Formal Chinese replaces everyday words with literary equivalents. Compare: 这个公司 (colloquial) → 本公司 (formal). Other formal self-references: 本部门 (this department), 本单位 (this unit/organization). 成立于 = 'was established in' — 于 replaces 在 in formal contexts.

items.question: 兹_____如下事项。
items.translation: (Hereby [we] notify the following matters.)
items.explanation: 兹 (zī) = 'hereby/now' — an extremely formal word found in official documents, contracts, and government notices. 兹通知 = 'hereby notify.' This is classical Chinese (文言文) surviving in modern bureaucratic language. Similar formal openers: 鉴于 (jiànyú, 'in view of'), 根据 (gēnjù, 'according to'), 依据 (yījù, 'based on'). These words never appear in speech — they're purely written (书面语). Recognizing them is essential for reading official documents.

items.question: 如有疑问，请_____本部门。
items.translation: (If you have questions, please contact this department.)
items.explanation: 联系 (liánxì) = 'contact' — formal yet widely used. In official letters, 联系 replaces casual 找 (zhǎo, find) or 问 (wèn, ask). 如有 (rú yǒu) = 'if there are' — formal for 如果有. Pattern: 如有 + noun + 请 + action. Examples: 如有异议，请及时提出 (if there are objections, raise them promptly), 如有变更，另行通知 (if there are changes, we'll notify separately). 另行 (lìngxíng) = 'separately' — another formal-only word.

items.question: 此_____有效期为一年。
items.translation: (This certificate is valid for one year.)
items.explanation: 此 (cǐ) = 'this' — formal equivalent of 这. 此证 = 'this certificate.' In formal documents: 此致敬礼 (respectfully yours — letter closing), 此令 (this order), 此据 (this receipt). Compare: 这个证件 (casual) → 此证 (formal). 有效期 (yǒuxiàoqī) = 'validity period.' Formal Chinese is much more concise than spoken Chinese — single characters replace multi-character phrases: 此 for 这个, 故 for 所以, 因 for 因为.

items.question: _____上述原因，我们决定延期。
items.translation: (Due to the above reasons, we decided to postpone.)
items.explanation: 鉴于 (jiànyú) = 'in view of/considering' — the most formal causal connector. 上述 (shàngshù) = 'above-mentioned.' Compare formality: 因为 (yīnwèi, because — spoken) → 由于 (yóuyú, due to — semi-formal) → 鉴于 (in view of — highly formal). 鉴于 is used in official decisions, court rulings, and policy documents. Pattern: 鉴于 + reason, + decision. Example: 鉴于天气恶劣，比赛推迟 (Given the bad weather, the match is postponed).

items.question: 特此_____，望周知。
items.translation: (This is hereby announced, please be informed.)
items.explanation: 特此 (tècǐ) = 'hereby/for this purpose' — a formal document phrase. 通告 (tōnggào) = 'public announcement.' 望周知 (wàng zhōuzhī) = 'hope everyone is informed.' This is the standard closing for official notices. 特此 appears in: 特此声明 (hereby declare), 特此证明 (hereby certify), 特此通知 (hereby notify). These are formulaic — memorize them as set phrases rather than analyzing each character. They appear on every official Chinese document.

items.question: 经研究_____，同意该项目。
items.translation: (After research and decision, [we] approve this project.)
items.explanation: 经 (jīng) = 'after/having undergone' — formal for 经过. 经研究决定 = 'after deliberation and decision' — a standard bureaucratic phrase indicating official review. This 4-character pattern is ubiquitous in Chinese government and corporate documents. Similar patterns: 经审查 (after review), 经批准 (after approval), 经讨论 (after discussion). The 经 + process + result structure conveys that proper procedures were followed — important in Chinese institutional culture.

items.question: 请于本月_____日前提交报告。
items.translation: (Please submit the report before the 15th of this month.)
items.explanation: 于 (yú) = 'at/on/in' — the formal equivalent of 在. 请于...前 = 'please before...' Compare: 在十五号之前 (casual) → 于本月十五日前 (formal). 于 appears everywhere in formal texts: 生于1990年 (born in 1990), 毕业于北京大学 (graduated from Peking University), 工作于该公司 (works at that company). 日 (rì) replaces 号 (hào) in formal dates. 本月 (běnyuè) = 'this month' (formal for 这个月).

items.question: 致：各部门_____。
items.translation: (To: All department heads.)
items.explanation: 致 (zhì) = 'to/addressed to' — formal letter salutation. 负责人 (fùzérén) = 'person in charge/head.' In formal correspondence: 致各位同事 (to all colleagues), 致全体员工 (to all employees). 致 is also used in dedications: 致我的父母 (to my parents). The colon-after-致 format is standard for internal memos and official communications. Compare: 给 (gěi, to — casual) → 致 (zhì, to — formal). Formal Chinese has distinct written vocabulary that differs significantly from spoken Chinese.

items.question: 综上所述，我们_____为该方案可行。
items.translation: (In summary, we consider this plan feasible.)
items.explanation: 综上所述 (zōng shàng suǒ shù) = 'to summarize the above/in conclusion' — a formal summary phrase used in reports, papers, and official analyses. 认为 (rènwéi) = 'consider/believe' — more formal than 觉得 (juéde, feel/think). In academic writing: 综上所述...因此 (in summary...therefore). Similar connectors: 总而言之 (in a word), 概括而言 (to summarize), 由此可见 (from this we can see). These transitions are essential for formal Chinese writing.

items.question: 该合同自签订之日_____生效。
items.translation: (This contract takes effect from the date of signing.)
items.explanation: 自...起 (zì...qǐ) = 'from...onward' — formal time expression in legal and official texts. 自 replaces 从 (cóng, from) in formal writing. 签订 (qiāndìng) = 'sign (a contract)' — more formal than 签 alone. 生效 (shēngxiào) = 'take effect.' Legal Chinese pattern: 自 + date/event + 起 + action. Example: 自即日起实施 (implemented from today). The 之 (zhī) in 签订之日 is a classical Chinese possessive marker — formal equivalent of 的.

items.question: 望各部门_____配合。
items.translation: (Hope all departments actively cooperate.)
items.explanation: 望 (wàng) = 'hope/expect' — formal for 希望. 积极 (jījí) = 'actively/positively.' In directives: 望各部门积极配合 conveys a gentle but firm expectation. 望 carries authority without being harsh. Compare: 希望你们合作 (casual) → 望各部门积极配合 (formal). Other 望 patterns: 望知悉 (please be aware), 望准时出席 (please attend on time), 望批准 (hope for approval). 望 is the perfect balance between a request and a command.

items.question: 兹聘请王明先生为本公司_____。
items.translation: (Mr. Wang Ming is hereby appointed as this company's consultant.)
items.explanation: 兹聘请 (zī pìnqǐng) = 'hereby appoint/hire' — official appointment language. 顾问 (gùwèn) = 'advisor/consultant.' This sentence structure is used in appointment letters (聘书, pìnshū). The full format includes: name, title, company, position, and validity period. 聘请 is more formal than 请 or 雇 (gù, employ). In Chinese business: 顾问 (consultant), 总经理 (general manager), 董事长 (chairman of the board). Official titles carry significant weight in Chinese culture.

items.question: 如_____意见，请书面提出。
items.translation: (If you have different opinions, please submit them in writing.)
items.explanation: 如有不同意见 = 'if there are differing opinions' — formal invitation for feedback. 书面 (shūmiàn) = 'in writing/written form' — key formal term. Chinese bureaucracy distinguishes: 口头 (kǒutóu, verbal/oral) vs. 书面 (written). Official requests must be 书面的. Pattern: 请以书面形式提交 (please submit in written form). In formal contexts, 提出 (tíchū, put forward) replaces casual 说 (say). Written Chinese heavily favors these precise, institutional vocabulary choices.

items.question: 此函_____为准。
items.translation: (This letter shall prevail [as the authoritative version].)
items.explanation: 以此函为准 (yǐ cǐ hán wéi zhǔn) = 'this letter shall serve as the standard/prevail.' 以...为准 is a powerful formal pattern meaning 'take...as the standard.' 函 (hán) = 'letter/correspondence' — formal for 信. Examples: 以合同为准 (the contract prevails), 以最终通知为准 (the final notice prevails). This pattern resolves disputes about which version is authoritative. In legal Chinese, precision of reference is paramount — hence these formulaic, unambiguous expressions.

items.question: 桌子上有_____书。
items.translation: (There are many books on the table.)
items.explanation: 很多 (hěn duō) = 'very many/a lot of' — the most common large quantity expression. Unlike English, 很多 directly precedes the noun without 的: 很多书, 很多人, 很多时间. Compare quantities: 很多 (many) > 不少 (quite a few) > 一些 (some) > 几个 (a few) > 很少 (very few). In Chinese, 多 (many) and 少 (few) are adjectives that can stand alone: 人很多 (many people), 钱很少 (very little money). No article is needed.

items.question: 这里_____有三十个学生。
items.translation: (There are approximately 30 students here.)
items.explanation: 大约 (dàyuē) = 'approximately/about' — used for estimated quantities. Position: 大约 comes before the number. Alternatives: 大概 (dàgài, roughly), 左右 (zuǒyòu, around — comes AFTER the number: 三十个左右), 上下 (shàngxià, more or less — also after). Compare placement: 大约三十个 (before) vs. 三十个左右 (after). Both mean 'around 30.' In formal writing: 约 (yuē) alone suffices: 约三十人.

items.question: 每个人都要交_____作业。
items.translation: (Everyone must submit two assignments.)
items.explanation: 两 (liǎng) vs. 二 (èr): Before measure words, ALWAYS use 两, not 二. 两份 = 'two copies/assignments.' 份 (fèn) is the measure word for documents, meals, and portions. Key rule: 两 + MW + noun (两个人, 两本书), but 二 for counting, ordinals, and math (第二, 二十, 1.2). Exceptions: 二百 and 两百 are both acceptable; 二千 and 两千 likewise. But 两个 is ALWAYS correct, 二个 is ALWAYS wrong.

items.question: 他只吃了_____饭就走了。
items.translation: (He left after eating only a little rice.)
items.explanation: 一点儿 (yīdiǎnr) = 'a little bit' — used for small quantities. The 儿 (er) is the Beijing-style suffix (儿化音). Without 儿: 一点 (yīdiǎn) — both are correct. 一点儿 emphasizes smallness. Compare: 一点儿 (a tiny bit) vs. 一些 (yīxiē, some — slightly more) vs. 有点儿 (yǒudiǎnr — 'somewhat,' used with adjectives: 有点儿冷 = somewhat cold). Key difference: 一点儿 modifies nouns, 有点儿 modifies adjectives/verbs.

items.question: _____学生都通过了考试。
items.translation: (All students passed the exam.)
items.explanation: 所有 (suǒyǒu) = 'all/every' — more emphatic than 每 (měi, each). 所有学生 = 'all students' (emphasizes totality). Compare: 每个学生 (each student — individual focus) vs. 所有学生 (all students — collective). 全部 (quánbù) = 'all/entire' — often for things: 全部作业 (all homework). In formal contexts: 所有 is standard. 全 (quán) alone works too: 全班 (the whole class), 全国 (the whole country), 全世界 (the whole world).

items.question: 这种水果_____五块钱。
items.translation: (This kind of fruit is five yuan per catty.)
items.explanation: 一斤 (yī jīn) = 'one catty (500g)' — the standard unit for buying produce in China. Price structure: 五块钱一斤 (five yuan per catty). 斤 is uniquely Chinese — markets, fruit shops, and supermarkets all use it. Weight units: 斤 (500g), 两 (liǎng, 50g — 1/10 of a 斤), 公斤 (gōngjīn, kilogram). At the market: 苹果多少钱一斤？(How much per catty for apples?). Understanding 斤 is essential for daily life in China.

items.question: 教室里_____人都没有。
items.translation: (There's not a single person in the classroom.)
items.explanation: 一个...都没有 = 'not even one' — an emphatic negation pattern. 一 + MW + 都/也 + 没/不 = 'not even a single...' Examples: 一分钱都没有 (don't have even a cent), 一句话都没说 (didn't say even one word), 一次都没去过 (never went even once). This is stronger than simple negation: 没有人 (nobody) vs. 一个人都没有 (not a single person — more emphatic). The 都/也 is essential in this pattern.

items.question: 他_____天都跑步。
items.translation: (He runs every day.)
items.explanation: 每 (měi) = 'every/each' — used with measure words. 每天 = 'every day' (no MW needed for 天). 每 + MW + noun: 每个人 (everyone), 每本书 (every book), 每次 (every time), 每年 (every year). Note: 天, 年, 次 don't need measure words after 每 — they ARE time words. But 每 + countable nouns need MW: 每个学生 (every student), 每件事 (every matter). 每 + 都: 每个人都来了 (everyone came) — 都 reinforces universality.

items.question: 我买了_____苹果。
items.translation: (I bought several apples.)
items.explanation: 几 (jǐ) has two functions: 1) question word: 几个？(how many?), 2) indefinite small number: 几个苹果 = 'several apples' (around 2-9). Context determines meaning — in statements it means 'several,' in questions it means 'how many.' Compare: 买了几个？(How many did you buy? — question) vs. 买了几个 (bought several — statement with neutral tone). 几 is only for small numbers; for larger quantities: 多少 (duōshao, how many/how much).

items.question: 这个班_____有四十人。
items.translation: (This class has a total of forty people.)
items.explanation: 一共 (yīgòng) = 'in total/altogether.' Position: 一共 comes before the verb or number. 一共有四十人 = 'there are forty in total.' Compare: 总共 (zǒnggòng) = 'in total' (more formal), 共 (gòng) = 'altogether' (shortest form). For bills: 一共多少钱？(how much in total?). In math: 一共 helps state sums. Related: 合计 (héjì, total — written/formal), 共计 (gòngjì, total — accounting).

items.question: 水果店里_____有西瓜和草莓。
items.translation: (The fruit shop only has watermelon and strawberries.)
items.explanation: 只 (zhǐ) = 'only/just' — restricts quantity or scope. 只有 (zhǐyǒu) = 'only have.' Compare: 只 (only — general), 仅 (jǐn, only — formal), 光 (guāng, only/just — colloquial). Examples: 我只有十块钱 (I only have 10 yuan), 他只来了一次 (he only came once). Position: 只 comes before the verb. Note: 只 is also a measure word for animals (一只猫, a cat) — context distinguishes the two uses.

items.question: 我们班_____有五个女生。
items.translation: (Our class has at most five female students.)
items.explanation: 最多 (zuìduō) = 'at most/maximum.' 最少 (zuìshǎo) = 'at least/minimum.' These set upper and lower bounds. Examples: 最多能坐十个人 (can seat at most 10), 至少要三天 (needs at least 3 days). 至少 (zhìshǎo) is more formal than 最少. In negotiations: 最多给你八折 (8折 = 20% off max). Range expression: 最少三个，最多五个 (minimum 3, maximum 5). These quantity limiters are essential for shopping, planning, and business.

items.question: 他比我_____两岁。
items.translation: (He is two years older than me.)
items.explanation: 大 (dà) = 'big/older' — for age comparisons, Chinese uses 大 (older) and 小 (xiǎo, younger), not 'old' and 'young.' Pattern: A 比 B 大/小 + number + 岁. 大两岁 = 'older by two years.' Be precise: 我比他大三岁 (I'm 3 years older than him). For height: 高 (gāo, taller) and 矮 (ǎi, shorter). Note: in Chinese, stating specific differences is preferred over vague comparisons: 大两岁 vs. 'a bit older.'

items.question: 今天来了_____二十个人。
items.translation: (More than twenty people came today.)
items.explanation: Number + 多 = 'more than [number].' 二十多个人 = 'more than 20 people' (could be 21-29). Position matters: 二十多 (20+, could be 21-29) vs. 多 before round numbers means different things. Compare: 三百多 (300+), 两千多 (2000+). For 'more than' with exact numbers: 超过 (chāoguò, exceed). 二十几 means 'twenty-something' (20-29, unknown exact). These nuances help express quantity ranges naturally in Chinese conversation.

items.question: 她的书包里_____了很多东西。
items.translation: (Her backpack is stuffed with many things.)
items.explanation: 装满 (zhuāng mǎn) = 'stuffed full/packed full.' 满 (mǎn) = 'full' — a result complement indicating completeness. Quantity through fullness: 装满了 (packed full), 吃饱了 (eaten full/satisfied), 喝够了 (drunk enough), 写完了 (finished writing). These Verb + 满/饱/够/完 patterns express quantity through completion. Compare: 放了很多 (put in many — neutral) vs. 装满了 (packed full — vivid, implies overflowing). Result complements make Chinese description dynamic.

items.question: 他慢慢_____走了过来。
items.translation: (He slowly walked over.)
items.explanation: 地 (de) — the adverbial 'de' — connects manner adverbs to verbs. Pattern: Adverb + 地 + Verb. 慢慢地走 = 'walk slowly.' The three 'de' particles: 的 (attributive: 漂亮的花, beautiful flower), 地 (adverbial: 慢慢地走, walk slowly), 得 (complement: 跑得快, run fast). This is one of the trickiest distinctions in Chinese. Memory aid: 地 goes BEFORE verbs (manner), 得 goes AFTER verbs (degree/result).

items.question: 老师认真_____批改作业。
items.translation: (The teacher carefully grades homework.)
items.explanation: 认真地 (rènzhēn de) = 'seriously/carefully' — adverb + 地 + verb pattern. 认真 (serious/careful) becomes an adverb with 地. Common manner adverbs with 地: 安静地 (quietly), 快速地 (quickly), 仔细地 (carefully), 高兴地 (happily), 努力地 (hard/diligently). In casual speech, 地 is often dropped: 他慢慢走 (casual) = 他慢慢地走 (standard). In writing, always include 地 for proper grammar. Doubled adjectives (慢慢, 轻轻) must use 地.

items.question: 她用筷子_____饭。
items.translation: (She eats with chopsticks.)
items.explanation: 用 (yòng) = 'use/with (an instrument).' 用 + tool + verb is the standard way to express 'doing something with/by means of.' 用筷子吃饭 = 'eat with chopsticks.' Examples: 用电脑工作 (work with a computer), 用中文写 (write in Chinese), 用手机拍照 (take photos with a phone). Unlike English prepositions (with, by), Chinese consistently uses 用. Position: 用 + tool always comes BEFORE the verb — never after.

items.question: 他是_____飞机来的北京。
items.translation: (He came to Beijing by airplane.)
items.explanation: 坐 (zuò) = 'sit/take (transport)' — the standard verb for using transportation. 坐飞机 (take a plane), 坐火车 (take a train), 坐地铁 (take the subway), 坐公交车 (take the bus), 坐出租车 (take a taxi). Exception: 骑自行车 (ride a bicycle) — uses 骑 (qí, ride) because you straddle it. Also: 开车 (drive a car) — uses 开 (kāi, drive/operate). The 是...的 structure emphasizes HOW he came — focus on manner, not the action itself.

items.question: 请你大声_____说一遍。
items.translation: (Please say it again loudly.)
items.explanation: 大声地 (dàshēng de) = 'loudly.' Again, manner adverb + 地 + verb. 大声地说 = 'say loudly.' Volume manner words: 大声 (loudly), 小声 (quietly/softly), 轻声 (gently/softly). 一遍 (yī biàn) = 'one time/once through.' 遍 counts complete cycles: 读一遍 (read through once), 看两遍 (watch/read twice through). Compare: 次 (cì, times — general), 遍 (biàn, times through — complete run-through), 下 (xià, a quick action).

items.question: 孩子们高高兴兴_____去上学了。
items.translation: (The children went to school happily.)
items.explanation: 高高兴兴地 (gāogāo xìngxìng de) = 'happily' — AABB reduplication + 地 for manner. AABB is a vivid adverbial pattern: 开开心心 (joyfully), 清清楚楚 (clearly), 干干净净 (cleanly), 整整齐齐 (neatly), 安安静静 (quietly). These doubled forms are more expressive than single forms. 高兴地 (happily — plain) vs. 高高兴兴地 (happily — vivid, emphasized). AABB + 地 is very natural in Chinese — use it to make your speech more colorful and native-sounding.

items.question: 他_____着眼泪说了这句话。
items.translation: (He said this with tears in his eyes.)
items.explanation: 含着 (hán zhe) = 'holding back/containing' — describes the accompanying state while performing an action. 含着眼泪 = 'with tears (contained/welling up).' The Verb + 着 pattern shows accompanying manner: 笑着说 (say while laughing), 站着吃 (eat while standing), 哭着离开 (leave while crying). This is different from 用: 用 indicates a tool/instrument, while V着 indicates a simultaneous state or action that describes HOW the main action is performed.

items.question: 他跑_____很快。
items.translation: (He runs very fast.)
items.explanation: Verb + 得 (de) + degree/manner describes HOW WELL an action is performed. 跑得快 = 'runs fast.' This is the complement-of-degree pattern: 说得好 (speaks well), 写得漂亮 (writes beautifully), 唱得不错 (sings pretty well). Compare with 地: 快速地跑 (quickly runs — adverb BEFORE verb) vs. 跑得快 (runs fast — complement AFTER verb). Both express manner, but 得 evaluates the result/quality, while 地 describes the approach/style.

items.question: 她把房间打扫_____干干净净。
items.translation: (She cleaned the room spotlessly.)
items.explanation: 打扫得干干净净 = 'cleaned (it) spotlessly clean.' Here 得 introduces the result/degree of cleaning. The 把 + 得 combination is powerful: 把 marks the object receiving the action, 得 describes the result. More examples: 把菜做得很好吃 (cooked the food deliciously), 把作业写得很整齐 (wrote homework neatly), 把衣服洗得很干净 (washed clothes very clean). This pattern emphasizes the thoroughness of the action's effect on the object.

items.question: 她一边听音乐，一边_____作业。
items.translation: (She listens to music while doing homework.)
items.explanation: 一边...一边... (yībiān...yībiān...) = 'while...while...' — doing two things simultaneously. 一边听一边做 = 'listen while doing.' This is the primary structure for expressing simultaneous actions. Examples: 一边走一边说 (walk and talk), 一边吃一边看电视 (eat while watching TV). Compare with 一面...一面... (yīmiàn...yīmiàn...) — slightly more formal, same meaning. Key rule: both actions must be doable at the same time — 一边睡觉一边跑步 doesn't work!

items.question: 他按照老师的要求_____了作业。
items.translation: (He completed homework according to the teacher's requirements.)
items.explanation: 按照 (ànzhào) = 'according to/in accordance with' — expresses conformity to a standard or instruction. 按照要求 = 'according to requirements.' Similar expressions: 根据 (gēnjù, based on — more formal), 按 (àn, per — shorter form), 依照 (yīzhào, in accordance with — legal). Examples: 按照规定 (according to regulations), 按照计划 (according to plan), 根据情况 (based on the situation). These are essential for following instructions in Chinese.

items.question: 她毫不犹豫_____答应了。
items.translation: (She agreed without any hesitation.)
items.explanation: 毫不犹豫地 (háo bù yóuyù de) = 'without any hesitation.' 毫不 (háo bù) = 'not in the slightest' — an emphatic negation. 犹豫 (yóuyù) = 'hesitate.' This 4-character manner expression + 地 is a literary/refined pattern. Similar: 不假思索地 (without thinking), 义无反顾地 (without looking back/resolutely), 全心全意地 (wholeheartedly). These set phrases elevate your Chinese from basic to sophisticated. They're commonly used in writing and formal speech.

items.question: 老人_____地过马路。
items.translation: (The old man carefully crossed the road.)
items.explanation: 小心地 (xiǎoxīn de) = 'carefully/cautiously.' 小心 literally means 'small heart' — being careful/vigilant. Related: 小心翼翼 (xiǎoxīn yìyì) = 'extremely carefully' — a beautiful idiomatic intensification. Manner adverbs from 心: 用心 (yòngxīn, attentively — 'use heart'), 开心 (kāixīn, happily), 伤心 (shāngxīn, sadly — 'hurt heart'), 细心 (xìxīn, meticulously — 'fine heart'), 粗心 (cūxīn, carelessly — 'coarse heart'). Chinese connects emotions to the heart (心) beautifully.

items.question: 他通过_____学会了中文。
items.translation: (He learned Chinese through self-study.)
items.explanation: 通过 (tōngguò) = 'through/by means of' — expresses the method or channel. 通过自学 = 'through self-study.' Compare: 用 (with — tool/instrument), 通过 (through — method/process), 按照 (according to — standard). Examples: 通过考试 (through/by passing an exam), 通过努力 (through hard work), 通过网络 (through the internet). 自学 (zìxué) = 'self-study' — a valued concept in Chinese education culture. 自 prefix means 'self': 自己 (oneself), 自信 (self-confident).

items.question: 这件事应该_____处理。
items.translation: (This matter should be handled properly.)
items.explanation: 妥善 (tuǒshàn) = 'properly/appropriately' — formal manner adverb. 妥善处理 = 'handle properly' — a standard phrase in business and official contexts. Compare formality: 好好处理 (handle well — casual) → 妥善处理 (handle properly — formal) → 审慎处理 (handle prudently — very formal). Other formal manner adverbs: 合理 (hélǐ, reasonably), 有效 (yǒuxiào, effectively), 及时 (jíshí, promptly), 妥当 (tuǒdang, appropriately). These elevate your Chinese for professional communication.

items.question: 他一边_____一边看电视。
items.translation: (He eats while watching TV.)
items.explanation: 一边...一边... (yībiān...yībiān...) is the most common way to express two simultaneous actions. Both actions happen at the same time. 一边吃饭一边看电视 = 'eat while watching TV.' Both verbs must be physically doable at the same time. Common combos: 一边走一边聊 (walk and chat), 一边工作一边听音乐 (work while listening to music). This structure is versatile and used daily.

items.question: 她_____做饭，_____唱歌。
items.translation: (She sings while cooking.)
items.explanation: 一面...一面... (yīmiàn...yīmiàn...) is a more formal/literary alternative to 一边...一边... Both express simultaneous actions, but 一面 appears more in written Chinese and speeches. Compare: 一边吃一边说 (casual) = 一面吃一面说 (literary). In news reports: 代表们一面讨论一面记录 (delegates discussed while taking notes). For everyday conversation, use 一边; for writing or formal speech, 一面 adds elegance.

items.question: 孩子们又跑_____叫。
items.translation: (The children run and shout [at the same time].)
items.explanation: 又...又... (yòu...yòu...) expresses two simultaneous qualities or states. 又跑又叫 = 'both running and shouting.' This differs from 一边...一边...: 又...又... focuses on two co-existing qualities/states, while 一边...一边... focuses on concurrent actions. Examples: 又便宜又好吃 (both cheap and delicious), 又聪明又漂亮 (both smart and pretty). For adjectives especially, 又...又... is preferred over 一边...一边...

items.question: 他_____说话_____比划。
items.translation: (He talks while gesturing.)
items.explanation: 连...带... (lián...dài...) = 'doing...along with...' — expresses vigorous, simultaneous actions with higher intensity than 一边...一边... 连说带比划 = 'talking while wildly gesturing.' This structure implies energetic, enthusiastic simultaneous activity. Examples: 连跑带跳 (running and jumping), 连吃带喝 (eating and drinking heartily), 连哭带闹 (crying and making a scene). It's colloquial, vivid, and paints a dynamic picture.

items.question: 我们边走_____聊吧。
items.translation: (Let's walk and chat at the same time.)
items.explanation: 边...边... (biān...biān...) is the shortened form of 一边...一边... More casual and colloquial. 边走边聊 = 'walk and chat.' This abbreviated form is extremely common in daily speech. Compare three levels: 一边走一边聊 (full form), 边走边聊 (shortened — most natural in speech), 一面走一面聊 (formal). The shortened 边...边... is the default in casual Mandarin — using the full 一边 can sound slightly textbook-ish.

items.question: 她正在打电话的_____还在看文件。
items.translation: (While she's on the phone, she's also looking at documents.)
items.explanation: 同时 (tóngshí) = 'at the same time/simultaneously' — a formal way to express concurrent activities. V的同时 = 'while V-ing.' Examples: 学习的同时也要注意休息 (while studying, also pay attention to rest). 同时 can also start a new sentence as a discourse connector: '同时，我们也需要考虑...' (At the same time, we also need to consider...). In formal writing, 同时 is preferred over 一边...一边...

items.question: 他_____工作_____照顾家庭。
items.translation: (He manages both work and taking care of family.)
items.explanation: 既...又... (jì...yòu...) = 'both...and...' — expresses two coexisting situations someone manages. 既工作又照顾家庭 = 'both works and takes care of family.' This structure emphasizes successful balance. Examples: 她既聪明又勤奋 (she's both smart and hardworking), 这里既安静又方便 (this place is both quiet and convenient). Unlike 又...又..., 既...又... is slightly more formal and literary.

items.question: 趁_____，我们出去走走吧。
items.translation: (While the weather is good, let's go for a walk.)
items.explanation: 趁 (chèn) = 'while/taking advantage of' — expresses doing something while a favorable condition exists. 趁天气好 = 'while the weather is good.' 趁 implies seizing an opportunity. Examples: 趁年轻多学习 (study more while you're young), 趁热吃 (eat while it's hot), 趁早 (as soon as possible/while it's still early). 趁 carries urgency — the window of opportunity might close. Compare: 趁 (seize the moment) vs. 当 (during/when — neutral timing).

items.question: 他一_____看书一_____做笔记。
items.translation: (He reads while taking notes.)
items.explanation: 一边看书一边做笔记 — here we see the full pattern with productive combination. Taking notes while reading is a quintessential Chinese study habit (边读边记). This pattern also appears in Chinese learning advice: 一边听一边跟读 (listen while repeating), 一边看一边写 (look while writing). Study-related simultaneous actions are emphasized in Chinese education culture. The 一边...一边... structure is versatile — it works with any two compatible actions.

items.question: 下雨的时候，他_____打伞_____跑。
items.translation: (When it rained, he ran while holding an umbrella.)
items.explanation: 一手...一手... = 'with one hand...with the other hand...' — specifically describes doing two things simultaneously with separate hands. 一手打伞一手提包 (hold umbrella with one hand, carry bag with the other). This is more specific than 一边...一边... — it emphasizes the physical division. Other body-part patterns: 一只手...另一只手... (one hand...the other hand...). These vivid patterns create clear mental images.

items.question: 随着经济发展，人们的生活水平_____提高了。
items.translation: (Along with economic development, people's living standards have also improved.)
items.explanation: 随着 (suízhe) = 'along with/as' — expresses two things changing simultaneously over time. 随着经济发展，生活水平也提高了 = 'as the economy developed, living standards also improved.' 随着 is key for expressing parallel changes: 随着时间推移 (as time passes), 随着技术进步 (with technological progress). This is essential in academic and news Chinese. Position: 随着 + change → result. It shows causally linked simultaneous progressions.

items.question: 他_____喝咖啡_____看报纸。
items.translation: (He reads the newspaper while drinking coffee.)
items.explanation: 一边喝咖啡一边看报纸 — a classic morning routine described with simultaneous actions. Notice that some actions pair naturally: 喝 + 看 (drink + look — compatible), 吃 + 说 (eat + talk — compatible but rude!). Chinese etiquette note: 一边吃饭一边说话 is considered bad manners (吃饭不说话 — don't talk while eating). Cultural awareness about which simultaneous actions are appropriate adds depth to language learning.

items.question: 与此_____，另一个问题也出现了。
items.translation: (At the same time, another problem also appeared.)
items.explanation: 与此同时 (yǔ cǐ tóngshí) = 'at the same time/meanwhile' — a formal discourse connector linking simultaneous events in different contexts. This is the written/news Chinese equivalent of 'meanwhile.' Examples: 公司在扩张，与此同时，竞争也在加剧 (the company is expanding; meanwhile, competition is intensifying). 与此同时 connects parallel narratives — essential for essays, reports, and news writing. It's more sophisticated than simple 同时.

items.question: 她上课的时候还偷偷_____手机。
items.translation: (She secretly plays on her phone during class.)
items.explanation: 偷偷 (tōutōu) = 'secretly/sneakily' — adds a hidden/covert dimension to simultaneous actions. 偷偷玩手机 = 'secretly play on phone.' The 的时候 (de shíhou) = 'when/during' — another way to frame simultaneity. Pattern: A的时候 + B = 'doing B during A.' Examples: 上课的时候聊天 (chat during class), 考试的时候作弊 (cheat during exams). 偷偷 is a reduplicated adverb that makes actions secretive and vivid.

items.question: 他在_____中学到了很多。
items.translation: (He learned a lot in the process of practice.)
items.explanation: 在...中 (zài...zhōng) = 'in the process of/during' — expresses learning or change happening within an ongoing activity. 在实践中学到 = 'learned during practice.' This structure emphasizes that two things happen within the same timeframe. Examples: 在工作中成长 (grow through work), 在学习中发现问题 (discover problems while studying), 在生活中体会 (experience through living). This is a mature, essay-level way to express simultaneity — very common in Chinese educational philosophy.

items.question: 我_____吃饭，_____去上课。
items.translation: (I'll eat first, then go to class.)
items.explanation: 先...再... (xiān...zài...) = 'first...then...' — the most common sequential pattern. 先吃饭再去上课 = 'eat first, then go to class.' 再 here means 'then/afterwards' (not 'again'). Compare sequential connectors: 先...再... (first...then — future), 先...然后... (first...then — neutral), 先...后... (first...after — written). Examples: 先洗手再吃饭 (wash hands first, then eat), 先想想再说 (think first, then speak).

items.question: 他先做了作业，_____出去玩了。
items.translation: (He did homework first, then went out to play.)
items.explanation: 然后 (ránhòu) = 'then/after that' — the most versatile sequence connector. It works for past, present, and future sequences. 先...然后... is the neutral, all-purpose version. 然后 can also chain multiple steps: 先A，然后B，然后C (first A, then B, then C). Be careful: overusing 然后 is a common learner mistake — it's like saying 'and then...and then...' in English. Mix with other connectors for natural flow.

items.question: 做完作业_____，我就出去玩。
items.translation: (After finishing homework, I'll go out and play.)
items.explanation: ...以后 (...yǐhòu) = 'after...' — marks the completion of one event before another begins. V + 以后/之后 = 'after V-ing.' Compare: 以后 (after — general) vs. 之后 (zhīhòu, after — slightly more formal). Position: time/event + 以后 + next action. Examples: 毕业以后 (after graduation), 吃饭以后 (after eating), 三天以后 (after three days). Note: 以后 can also mean 'in the future' without a preceding verb: 以后我要去中国 (in the future I want to go to China).

items.question: 出门_____要检查有没有带钥匙。
items.translation: (Before going out, check if you have your keys.)
items.explanation: ...之前 (...zhīqián) = 'before...' — marks what should happen before the main event. V + 之前 = 'before V-ing.' Compare: 之前 (before — formal) vs. 以前 (yǐqián, before — also common). Examples: 上课之前 (before class), 出发之前 (before departing), 做决定之前 (before making a decision). Important: 以前 and 之前 are basically interchangeable when placed after a verb phrase. But 以前 can mean 'in the past' independently: 以前我住在北京 (I used to live in Beijing).

items.question: 你_____把这个做完，我等你。
items.translation: (You finish this first, I'll wait for you.)
items.explanation: 先 (xiān) = 'first' — can stand alone as a sequence marker. 你先做 = 'you do it first.' 先 is extremely versatile: 先走了 (leaving first/goodbye — casual), 你先说 (you speak first), 先别急 (don't rush/wait a moment). In service contexts: 先生先请 (please go first, sir). 先 implies priority — what should happen before other things. It can express politeness: 你先来 (you first) or urgency: 先把这个解决了 (resolve this first).

items.question: 他刚到家，_____接到了电话。
items.translation: (He had just arrived home when he received a phone call.)
items.explanation: 刚...就... (gāng...jiù...) = 'just/barely [did]...when...' — expresses two events happening in rapid succession. 刚到家就接电话 = 'barely got home when received a call.' The 刚 marks the first action as barely completed, and 就 marks the immediate follow-up. Examples: 刚坐下就要走 (just sat down and has to leave), 刚说完就后悔了 (just finished saying it and regretted it). This pattern emphasizes surprising or inconvenient timing.

items.question: 首先自我介绍，_____开始讨论。
items.translation: (First introduce yourself, then start the discussion.)
items.explanation: 接着 (jiēzhe) = 'next/then/following that' — a smooth sequence connector implying immediate continuation. 首先...接着... (first...next...) is common in presentations and instructions. Compare: 然后 (then — general pause), 接着 (then — immediate continuation), 随后 (suíhòu, subsequently — formal). For multi-step sequences: 首先...接着...最后... (first...then...finally...). 接着 literally means 'catching/receiving the next thing' — creating a flowing, connected sequence.

items.question: 等你准备_____了，我们就出发。
items.translation: (Once you're ready, we'll set off.)
items.explanation: 等...好了 = 'once/when...is ready/done.' 准备好了 = 'ready/prepared.' The 等 (děng) = 'wait until/once' structure sets up a condition before the next action. Pattern: 等 + condition + 就 + action. Examples: 等雨停了就走 (once the rain stops, we'll go), 等我回来就告诉你 (I'll tell you when I get back). 好 as a result complement means 'properly done': 做好 (done/made properly), 写好 (written/completed), 穿好 (dressed/put on properly).

items.question: 最后，请大家_____自己的意见。
items.translation: (Finally, please everyone share your opinions.)
items.explanation: 最后 (zuìhòu) = 'finally/lastly' — marks the end of a sequence. In presentations: 首先...其次...最后... (first...second...finally...) is the standard structure. 发表意见 (fābiǎo yìjiàn) = 'express/share opinions' — formal phrasing. Full sequence vocabulary: 首先 (first), 其次 (qícì, secondly), 再次 (zàicì, thirdly), 接着 (next), 最后 (finally). Using these connectors makes your Chinese sound organized and professional.

items.question: 一_____做完就_____交给老师。
items.translation: (As soon as it's done, hand it to the teacher immediately.)
items.explanation: 一...就... (yī...jiù...) = 'as soon as...then...' — the tightest sequential connection. 一做完就交 = 'submit immediately upon completion.' This is stronger than 刚...就... — it implies ZERO gap. Examples: 一到就通知我 (notify me as soon as you arrive), 一看就懂 (understand at first glance), 一听就会 (can do it after hearing once). 一...就... can also express habitual immediacy: 他一喝酒就脸红 (his face turns red whenever he drinks).

items.question: 他_____把行李放好，_____去前台登记。
items.translation: (He first put down his luggage, then went to the front desk to register.)
items.explanation: 先把行李放好，再去前台 — here 先...再... combines with 把 (bǎ) construction. The 把 + object + result pattern fits naturally in sequential instructions: 先把门关好 (first close the door properly), 再把灯关了 (then turn off the lights). This combination of 先/再 with 把 is extremely common in giving instructions: recipes (先把菜洗干净), assembly (先把零件准备好), cleaning (先把桌子擦干净).

items.question: 吃完饭_____，我们去散步吧。
items.translation: (After finishing the meal, let's go for a walk.)
items.explanation: V完 + 之后 = 'after finishing V.' 吃完饭之后 = 'after finishing eating.' The 完 (wán) completion complement + 之后 time marker is a powerful combination. Compare: 吃完以后 = 吃完之后 (both mean 'after eating'). The 完 makes the completion explicit — 吃之后 (after eating — might be mid-meal) vs. 吃完之后 (after finishing eating — meal is done). This precision is characteristic of Chinese: completion must be marked explicitly when it matters.

items.question: 他把信交给我_____就走了。
items.translation: (After handing me the letter, he left.)
items.explanation: ...之后就... = 'after...immediately...' The 就 adds immediacy to the sequence — it didn't just happen after, it happened RIGHT after. Compare: 交给我之后走了 (left after giving it — neutral timing) vs. 交给我之后就走了 (left right after giving it — immediate). The 就 in sequences always implies 'sooner than expected' or 'immediately following.' This nuance is important for storytelling and describing events with precise timing.

items.question: 请按照_____完成以下步骤。
items.translation: (Please complete the following steps in order.)
items.explanation: 按照顺序 (ànzhào shùnxù) = 'in order/sequentially.' 顺序 = 'sequence/order.' This meta-phrase is used when giving multi-step instructions. Related: 依次 (yīcì, one by one/in turn — formal), 按步骤 (àn bùzhòu, step by step), 循序渐进 (xúnxù jiànjìn, progress step by step — idiom). In teaching: 按照顺序回答问题 (answer questions in order). 步骤 (bùzhòu) = 'steps/procedure' — essential vocabulary for instructions, recipes, and processes.

items.question: 早上起来，_____刷牙洗脸。
items.translation: (After getting up in the morning, first brush teeth and wash face.)
items.explanation: Daily routine sequences in Chinese follow a clear 先...再...然后... pattern: 先刷牙洗脸 (first brush teeth and wash face), 再吃早饭 (then eat breakfast), 然后出门上班 (then go out to work). Chinese culture emphasizes orderly routines, and the sequence vocabulary reflects this. 刷牙洗脸 (brush teeth and wash face) is a set phrase — always in this order. Similar fixed sequences: 穿衣打扮 (dress and groom), 收拾行李 (pack luggage). The sequencing of daily activities is a rich source of natural Chinese expressions.

items.question: 这个价格_____早餐。
items.translation: (This price includes breakfast.)
items.explanation: 包括 (bāokuò) = 'include/including' — the standard word for expressing that something is part of a group. 包括早餐 = 'including breakfast.' In hotels: 房价包括早餐吗？(Does the room rate include breakfast?). Compare: 包括 (include — neutral), 包含 (bāohán, contain — slightly more formal), 含 (hán, contain — shortest, used on signs/menus). Negation: 不包括 (not including), 不含税 (tax not included). Essential for shopping, travel, and business.

items.question: _____他在内，一共有十个人。
items.translation: (Including him, there are ten people in total.)
items.explanation: 算...在内 (suàn...zài nèi) = 'counting...as included.' 算他在内 = 'counting him in.' 算 (suàn) literally means 'calculate/count.' The pattern 算上 (suàn shàng) also means 'counting in': 算上我一共六个人 (counting me, six people total). Opposite: 不算 (not counting): 不算周末还有三天 (not counting weekends, three more days). This casual math-like inclusion is very natural in spoken Chinese.

items.question: _____老师_____学生都去了。
items.translation: (Both teachers and students went.)
items.explanation: 连...和... or simply ...和...都... = 'both...and...' For expressing comprehensive inclusion. Even simpler: 老师和学生都去了 (both teachers and students went). 都 (dōu) is the key — it means 'all/both' and signals universality. Examples: 大人和小孩都喜欢 (both adults and children like it), 男生和女生都可以参加 (both boys and girls can participate). 都 makes the statement about ALL members of the mentioned groups.

items.question: 这家餐厅什么菜_____有。
items.translation: (This restaurant has every kind of dish.)
items.explanation: 什么...都... = 'everything/anything...all...' — expresses total inclusion using question words. 什么菜都有 = 'has all kinds of dishes.' This structure works with all question words: 谁都知道 (everyone knows), 哪里都去过 (been everywhere), 什么时候都行 (any time works), 怎么做都好 (however you do it is fine). Question word + 都/也 = universal statement. This is one of the most powerful grammar patterns in Chinese.

items.question: 从小孩到老人，_____喜欢这个节目。
items.translation: (From children to elderly, everyone likes this show.)
items.explanation: 从...到... (cóng...dào...) = 'from...to...' — expresses a complete range. 从小孩到老人 = 'from children to the elderly' (= everyone). This pattern creates inclusive ranges: 从早到晚 (from morning to night = all day), 从北到南 (from north to south = everywhere), 从一月到十二月 (from January to December = all year). Adding 都 emphasizes that the statement applies to the ENTIRE range without exception.

items.question: 除了中文，他_____会说英文和日文。
items.translation: (Besides Chinese, he can also speak English and Japanese.)
items.explanation: 除了...还/也... = 'besides/in addition to...also...' — ADDS to the scope. 除了中文还会英文 = 'besides Chinese, also speaks English.' CRITICAL: 除了 has TWO opposite meanings depending on context: 1) 除了...还... (besides — adds), 2) 除了...都... (except for — excludes). Compare: 除了他还有谁？(besides him, who else?) vs. 除了他都来了 (everyone came except him). The word after 除了 determines the meaning: 还/也 = inclusive, 都 = exclusive.

items.question: 不管是谁，_____要遵守规则。
items.translation: (No matter who it is, [everyone] must follow the rules.)
items.explanation: 不管...都... (bùguǎn...dōu...) = 'no matter...all...' — universal inclusion regardless of conditions. 不管是谁都要 = 'no matter who, everyone must...' 不管 introduces conditions that don't change the outcome: 不管多难都要试 (no matter how hard, must try), 不管下不下雨都去 (going whether it rains or not). Similar: 无论...都... (wúlùn...dōu... — more formal), 管他...都... (colloquial). These express unconditional universality.

items.question: 全班同学_____参加了运动会。
items.translation: (The entire class participated in the sports meet.)
items.explanation: 全 (quán) = 'entire/whole' + 都 = 100% inclusion. 全班都参加了 = 'the entire class participated.' 全 expressions: 全家 (whole family), 全校 (whole school), 全国 (whole country), 全世界 (whole world), 全体 (quántǐ, all members — formal). 全...都... is emphatic: 全部做完了 (finished everything), 全都知道了 (everyone knows). Compare: 所有人都... (all people...) ≈ 全部人都... (everyone...) — slightly different nuance but same meaning.

items.question: 这个套餐_____一杯饮料和一份甜点。
items.translation: (This set meal includes a drink and a dessert.)
items.explanation: 含 (hán) = 'contain/include' — the shortest form of 包含. In menus and price lists: 含税 (tax included), 含运费 (shipping included), 含早餐 (breakfast included). 含 is concise and widely used in commercial contexts. Written style: 本价格含增值税 (this price includes VAT). Contrast: 不含 (not included): 价格不含服务费 (price doesn't include service charge). On product labels: 含糖 (contains sugar), 不含防腐剂 (no preservatives). 含 is essential for reading Chinese menus, labels, and contracts.

items.question: 每个人_____有机会发言。
items.translation: (Everyone has an opportunity to speak.)
items.explanation: 每个人都 = 'every person (all) ...' — 每 (each) + 都 (all) creates emphatic universal inclusion. The 都 is essential — 每个人有机会 (less emphatic) vs. 每个人都有机会 (everyone has — no exceptions). Grammar rule: 每 requires 都 to complete the universal meaning. 每 alone means 'each' (individual focus); 每...都 means 'every...without exception' (totality). Examples: 每天都学 (study every day), 每次都迟到 (late every time).

items.question: 不论_____，他都能解决。
items.translation: (No matter what problem, he can solve it.)
items.explanation: 不论...都... (bùlùn...dōu...) = 'regardless of...all...' — formal version of 不管...都... Both express unconditional inclusion. 不论什么问题都能解决 = 'can solve any problem regardless.' Formality scale: 不管 (casual) → 不论 (standard) → 无论 (formal/literary). All three work the same way. Examples: 无论如何都要完成 (must finish no matter what), 不论成功与否 (regardless of success or failure). 与否 (yǔfǒu) = 'or not' — a formal alternative to 不.

items.question: 他的书架上_____是中文书。
items.translation: (His bookshelf is entirely Chinese books.)
items.explanation: 全是 (quán shì) = 'entirely/all are' — expresses that something consists of 100% one type. 全是中文书 = 'all Chinese books (nothing else).' 全 as an adverb before 是 is very common: 全是假的 (all fake), 全是我的错 (all my fault), 全是新的 (all new). Compare: 都是 (all are — more common in speech) vs. 全是 (entirely — slightly more emphatic). Both work, but 全 emphasizes completeness/uniformity more strongly.

items.question: 凡是来的客人_____受到了欢迎。
items.translation: (Every guest who came was welcomed.)
items.explanation: 凡是...都... (fánshì...dōu...) = 'every single one that...all...' — the most emphatic universal inclusion pattern. 凡是 means 'every/all that meet a condition.' 凡是来的都欢迎 = 'everyone who comes is welcome.' 凡是 is more formal and emphatic than 所有: 凡是迟到的都要罚款 (everyone who's late will be fined), 凡是符合条件的都可以申请 (all who meet the criteria can apply). 凡是 implies a rule or principle — it's authoritative.

items.question: 连小孩子_____知道这个道理。
items.translation: (Even children understand this principle.)
items.explanation: 连...都... (lián...dōu...) = 'even...all...' — expresses extreme inclusion. If EVEN the least expected member is included, then obviously everyone is. 连小孩都知道 = 'even children know (so everyone does).' 连 highlights the most surprising/extreme case: 连老师都不会 (even the teacher can't), 连一分钱都没有 (doesn't have even a cent), 连名字都忘了 (forgot even the name). This pattern creates emphasis through unexpectedness.

items.question: 这个活动面向_____市民开放。
items.translation: (This activity is open to all citizens.)
items.explanation: 面向所有...开放 = 'open to all...' — formal expression of universal inclusion. 面向 (miànxiàng) = 'aimed at/targeted toward.' 面向所有市民 = 'for all citizens.' This pattern is common in official announcements: 面向全国招生 (enrolling students nationwide), 面向社会公开 (open to the public). Compare: 对所有人开放 (open to everyone — simpler) vs. 面向所有人开放 (aimed at and open to everyone — more official/formal).

items.question: 虽然很累，_____他还是坚持跑完了。
items.translation: (Although very tired, he still persisted and finished running.)
items.explanation: 虽然...但是... (suīrán...dànshì...) = 'although...but...' — the most fundamental concession pattern. Unlike English where 'although' and 'but' don't appear together, Chinese REQUIRES both: 虽然 (concession) + 但是 (contrast). This is a key difference! 还是 (háishi) = 'still/nevertheless' adds emphasis on persistence despite the obstacle. Variations: 虽然...可是... (slightly softer), 虽然...然而... (formal), 虽然...不过... (milder contrast).

items.question: _____下雨，他也要出去。
items.translation: (Even if it rains, he wants to go out.)
items.explanation: 即使...也... (jíshǐ...yě...) = 'even if...still...' — concession for hypothetical situations. 即使下雨也要出去 = 'even if it rains, will still go out.' 即使 is stronger than 虽然 — it deals with hypothetical or extreme conditions. Compare: 虽然 (although — actual fact) vs. 即使 (even if — hypothetical/extreme). Examples: 即使失败也不后悔 (even if I fail, no regrets), 即使再忙也要锻炼 (even if super busy, must exercise). 即使 shows determination.

items.question: 尽管困难很多，我们_____没有放弃。
items.translation: (Despite many difficulties, we did not give up.)
items.explanation: 尽管...还是... (jǐnguǎn...háishi...) = 'despite...still...' — for acknowledging real obstacles while emphasizing what happened anyway. 尽管 is more emphatic than 虽然 — it acknowledges the challenge more fully. Compare: 虽然难，但做了 (although hard, did it — neutral) vs. 尽管难，还是做了 (despite difficulty, still did it — emphasizes overcoming). 尽管 often pairs with 还是/仍然 (réng rán, still/nevertheless).

items.question: 就算他不来，我们_____继续。
items.translation: (Even if he doesn't come, we'll continue anyway.)
items.explanation: 就算...也... (jiùsuàn...yě...) = 'even if/even supposing...still...' — colloquial alternative to 即使...也... 就算 is more conversational: 就算他不同意也没办法 (even if he disagrees, there's nothing to do). Compare formality: 就算 (casual) → 即使 (standard) → 纵使 (zòngshǐ, literary). All mean 'even if.' In arguments: 就算你是对的，态度也不好 (even if you're right, your attitude is bad). 就算 is the go-to form in daily conversation.

items.question: 不管/无论_____，我都支持你。
items.translation: (No matter what happens, I support you.)
items.explanation: 无论/不管...都... = 'no matter...all...' — unconditional concession. 无论发生什么都支持你 = 'support you no matter what happens.' This pattern combines concession with universality — the result is the same regardless of ANY condition. Examples: 无论成功还是失败 (whether success or failure), 不管别人怎么说 (no matter what others say). The question word (什么/谁/哪里/怎么) makes it universal — covering ALL possibilities.

items.question: 话虽这么说，_____做起来不容易。
items.translation: (That said, it's not easy to do in practice.)
items.explanation: 话虽这么说，但... = 'that said/having said that, but...' — a sophisticated concession that acknowledges a previous point while introducing a contrasting reality. This is upper-intermediate/advanced Chinese. 虽 here is a condensed 虽然. Similar patterns: 道理是这样，但是... (the principle is like this, but...), 理论上是可以的，但实际上... (theoretically possible, but in practice...). These show nuanced thinking — essential for debates and discussions.

items.question: 他_____不太聪明，可是很努力。
items.translation: (He's admittedly not very smart, but works very hard.)
items.explanation: 固然...可是... (gùrán...kěshì...) = 'admittedly...but...' — a formal concession that grants a point while countering with something more important. 固然 literally means 'certainly/inherently true.' Examples: 价格固然重要，可是质量更重要 (price is admittedly important, but quality is more important). 固然 is more literary than 虽然 — it shows you're intellectually conceding the point rather than just acknowledging a fact. Used in essays, debates, and formal discussions.

items.question: 哪怕只有一点希望，我们_____不会放弃。
items.translation: (Even if there's only a tiny bit of hope, we won't give up.)
items.explanation: 哪怕...也... (nǎpà...yě...) = 'even if (as extreme as)...still...' — the most emotionally charged concession. 哪怕 implies enduring the worst-case scenario. 哪怕只有一点希望也不放弃 = 'won't give up even with minimal hope.' 哪怕 is stronger and more emotional than 即使: 哪怕付出一切 (even if it costs everything), 哪怕等一辈子 (even if waiting a lifetime). It's powerful in motivational and emotional contexts.

items.question: 他的中文_____还不完美，但已经很好了。
items.translation: (His Chinese, while not yet perfect, is already very good.)
items.explanation: 虽然...但已经... = 'although...but already...' — concession + progress acknowledgment. This pattern is encouraging: acknowledging imperfection while highlighting achievement. Common in feedback: 虽然还有改进空间，但进步很大 (although there's room for improvement, great progress). Chinese communication often uses this 'concession-then-encouragement' pattern — similar to the feedback sandwich. It's considerate and motivational.

items.question: 纵然前路艰难，我亦_____前行。
items.translation: (Even though the road ahead is hard, I will still move forward.)
items.explanation: 纵然...亦... (zòngrán...yì...) = 'even though...still...' — literary/poetic concession. 纵然 is the most formal version of 虽然/即使. 亦 (yì) = 'also/still' — classical Chinese for 也. This pattern appears in literature, poetry, speeches, and mottos. Compare formality: 虽然...但是 (standard) → 尽管...仍然 (formal) → 纵然...亦 (literary/poetic). Using 纵然 and 亦 immediately signals educated, literary Chinese — save it for writing and speeches.

items.question: 不过话说回来，这个主意_____不错。
items.translation: (However, come to think of it, this idea isn't bad.)
items.explanation: 不过 (bùguò) = 'however/but' — the mildest contrast connector. 倒 (dào) = 'actually/on the contrary' — adds a surprising reversal. 倒不错 = 'actually not bad.' 不过...倒... creates a turn: acknowledging a negative setup then offering a positive reversal. 话说回来 (huà shuō huílai) = 'come to think of it/that said' — a conversational phrase for reconsidering. This combination shows nuanced, natural Chinese thinking — reconsidering and adjusting one's position mid-conversation.

items.question: 就是_____不同意，他也没别的办法。
items.translation: (Even if he disagrees, he has no other choice.)
items.explanation: 就是...也... (jiùshì...yě...) = 'even if...also...' — casual concession emphasizing inevitability. 就是他不同意也没办法 = 'even if he disagrees, nothing can be done.' 就是 here means 'even if' (not 'precisely'). It's more colloquial than 即使. Used in pragmatic, matter-of-fact concessions: 就是你不说我也知道 (even if you don't say it, I know), 就是再贵我也买 (even if it's more expensive, I'll still buy). Very natural in everyday speech.

items.question: 任凭别人怎么劝，他_____不听。
items.translation: (No matter how others advise him, he won't listen.)
items.explanation: 任凭...就是... (rènpíng...jiùshì...) = 'no matter how much [others do]...[he] just won't...' — expresses stubborn resistance despite concessive conditions. 任凭 (rènpíng) = 'no matter how much/let [things] be as they may.' This pattern describes someone who refuses to change despite all efforts: 任凭你怎么解释他就是不信 (no matter how you explain, he just won't believe). 任凭 conveys a sense of exasperation or admiration at someone's stubbornness.

items.question: 诚然，他有_____，但功大于过。
items.translation: (Admittedly, he has shortcomings, but his achievements outweigh his faults.)
items.explanation: 诚然 (chéngrán) = 'admittedly/indeed' — the most formal concession opener. 诚然...但... = 'admittedly...but...' is used in academic writing, editorials, and formal arguments. 功大于过 (gōng dà yú guò) = 'merits outweigh faults' — a classical Chinese expression. 不足 (bùzú) = 'shortcomings/inadequacies.' This level of Chinese is for essays and formal debates: 诚然存在问题，但整体向好 (admittedly problems exist, but the overall trend is positive).

items.question: 他虽然输了比赛，_____赢得了尊重。
items.translation: (Although he lost the game, he won respect.)
items.explanation: 虽然...却... (suīrán...què...) = 'although...yet/however...' — 却 is a more elegant contrast marker than 但是. 却 (què) implies unexpected contrast — something went opposite to expectations. 输了比赛却赢了尊重 = 'lost the game yet won respect' — poetic contrast. 却 is shorter and more literary than 但是: 他很年轻，却很有经验 (he's young yet very experienced). 却 often creates beautiful paradoxes — it's a favorite in Chinese writing for creating impact.

items.question: 我学中文是_____去中国工作。
items.translation: (I study Chinese in order to work in China.)
items.explanation: 为了 (wèile) = 'in order to/for the purpose of' — the most direct purpose expression. 为了去中国工作 = 'in order to work in China.' Position: 为了 + goal + main verb, or main verb + 为了 + goal. Compare: 为了 (for — purpose) vs. 因为 (because — reason). These are fundamentally different! 为了 looks forward (purpose/goal), 因为 looks backward (cause/reason). Examples: 为了健康而运动 (exercise for health), 为了孩子拼命工作 (work hard for the children).

items.question: 他努力学习，_____考上好大学。
items.translation: (He studies hard so as to get into a good university.)
items.explanation: 以便 (yǐbiàn) = 'so as to/in order to' — more formal than 为了. 以便考上好大学 = 'so as to get into a good university.' 以便 appears in formal instructions, business, and academic writing: 请提前到达以便安排座位 (please arrive early so we can arrange seating). Formality scale: 为了 (standard) → 以便 (formal) → 以期 (yǐqī, literary — 'in hopes of'). 以便 literally means 'so that it's convenient to' — polite and purposeful.

items.question: 他来北京是来_____的。
items.translation: (He came to Beijing to study.)
items.explanation: 是来...的 (shì lái...de) = 'came to [do]...' — the 是...的 construction focuses on the PURPOSE of coming. This split construction emphasizes WHY, not WHAT: 他来北京了 (he came to Beijing — factual) vs. 他是来学习的 (he came TO STUDY — purpose-focused). In conversations: 你是来旅游的吗？(Did you come for tourism?). The 是...的 frame naturally highlights purpose when used with verbs of movement (来/去). It's a question you'll hear often at airports and hotels.

items.question: 为了不迟到，他_____就出门了。
items.translation: (In order not to be late, he left the house early.)
items.explanation: 为了不... = 'in order not to...' — negative purpose. 为了不迟到 = 'in order not to be late.' Chinese negative purposes follow the same pattern as positive: 为了 + 不/别 + verb. Examples: 为了不让她担心 (so she wouldn't worry), 为了避免误会 (to avoid misunderstanding). 避免 (bìmiǎn) = 'avoid' is common in formal negative purpose: 避免出错 (to avoid mistakes). Purpose statements with 为了 can come before OR after the main clause — Chinese word order is flexible here.

items.question: 她打工是为了_____学费。
items.translation: (She works part-time to earn tuition.)
items.explanation: 为了赚学费 = 'in order to earn tuition.' 赚 (zhuàn) = 'earn/make money.' Purpose behind work is a common topic: 打工为了赚零花钱 (part-time job to earn pocket money), 加班为了完成任务 (overtime to finish the task). 打工 (dǎgōng) = 'work part-time/do casual work' — originally Cantonese, now standard Mandarin. It implies working for someone else, often in service jobs. Students commonly: 勤工俭学 (qín gōng jiǎn xué) = 'work-study program.'

items.question: 请大家安静，_____我们开始上课。
items.translation: (Please everyone be quiet so that we can start class.)
items.explanation: 好让 (hǎo ràng) = 'so that' — colloquial purpose connector. 好让我们开始 = 'so that we can start.' 好 here doesn't mean 'good' — it means 'so that/in order to.' This is very natural in speech: 你说慢点好让我听懂 (speak slower so that I can understand), 我写下来好让你记住 (I'll write it down so you can remember). 好 as a purpose marker is informal but extremely common — a hallmark of natural spoken Chinese.

items.question: 他去图书馆是_____查资料。
items.translation: (He went to the library to look up materials.)
items.explanation: 去...为了... or 为了...去... — both orderings work for movement + purpose. 去图书馆为了查资料 = 去图书馆查资料 (went to the library to look up materials). In Chinese, you can often drop 为了 when the purpose is obvious: 去医院看病 (go to the hospital to see a doctor — 为了 implied), 去超市买东西 (go to the supermarket to buy things). The verb of movement (去/来) + purpose verb combination is so common that 为了 becomes optional.

items.question: 公司举办培训的_____是提高员工能力。
items.translation: (The purpose of the company's training is to improve employee abilities.)
items.explanation: 目的是 (mùdì shì) = 'the purpose is' — making purpose the topic of the sentence. 目的 (purpose) is more formal than 为了 and turns purpose into the subject. Pattern: V的目的是... Examples: 学习的目的是应用 (the purpose of learning is application), 改革的目的是发展 (the purpose of reform is development). In academic/business: 本研究的目的是... (the purpose of this study is...). This structure is essay-level — it explicitly names the goal.

items.question: 她省吃俭用，为的是_____一套房子。
items.translation: (She lives frugally in order to buy an apartment.)
items.explanation: 为的是 (wèi de shì) = 'the purpose is/what it's for is' — emphatic purpose marker. 省吃俭用 (shěng chī jiǎn yòng) = 'save on food and be frugal' — a 4-character idiom meaning 'live very frugally.' 为的是买房 = 'all for the purpose of buying a house.' 为的是 is more emphatic than 为了 — it highlights that everything mentioned was specifically FOR this goal. It often follows a description of sacrifice or effort, making the purpose feel hard-won and important.

items.question: 我特意早起，_____赶上第一班车。
items.translation: (I deliberately got up early to catch the first bus.)
items.explanation: 好 (hǎo) = 'so that/in order to' — purpose marker in spoken Chinese. 特意早起好赶上 = 'deliberately got up early so as to catch...' 特意 (tèyì) = 'deliberately/specially' — emphasizes the action was intentional. 好 as purpose marker: 早点走好赶上飞机 (leave early to catch the flight), 多穿点好别感冒 (dress warmly so you don't catch cold). This casual 好 is interchangeable with 以便 in many contexts, but much more natural in conversation.

items.question: 为了_____环境，请不要乱扔垃圾。
items.translation: (To protect the environment, please don't litter.)
items.explanation: 为了保护环境 = 'to protect the environment.' This pattern appears on signs throughout China: 为了您的安全 (for your safety), 为了城市整洁 (for city cleanliness). Public signs use 为了 extensively. 请不要 (qǐng bùyào) = 'please don't' — the polite prohibition. 乱扔 (luàn rēng) = 'throw around carelessly/litter.' 乱 + verb = 'do V randomly/improperly': 乱说 (talk nonsense), 乱写 (scribble), 乱花钱 (spend money carelessly).

items.question: 他这样做是_____帮助别人。
items.translation: (He did this in order to help others.)
items.explanation: 这样做是为了 = 'doing this is for the purpose of' — explains the WHY behind an action. This can be used defensively: 我这样做是为了你好 (I did this for your own good — a classic Chinese parent phrase!). 为了你好 is so common it's become a cultural reference. Similar structures: 这一切都是为了... (all of this is for...), 我所做的一切都是为了... (everything I've done is for...). These purpose expressions often carry emotional weight in Chinese family dynamics.

items.question: 他搬到学校附近，以免_____时间。
items.translation: (He moved near the school to avoid wasting time.)
items.explanation: 以免 (yǐmiǎn) = 'so as to avoid/lest' — formal negative purpose. 以免浪费时间 = 'to avoid wasting time.' 以免 is more formal and concise than 为了不. Examples: 请提前确认以免误解 (please confirm in advance to avoid misunderstanding), 多带衣服以免着凉 (bring extra clothes to avoid catching cold). 免 (miǎn) = 'exempt/avoid.' Related: 免费 (miǎnfèi, free of charge), 免税 (miǎnshuì, tax-free). 以免 is essential in formal Chinese instructions and advice.

items.question: 他拼命工作，_____给家人更好的生活。
items.translation: (He works desperately to give his family a better life.)
items.explanation: 拼命 (pīnmìng) = 'desperately/with all one's might' — literally 'gamble one's life.' 为了给家人更好的生活 = 'to give the family a better life.' This is a deeply resonant theme in Chinese culture — 为了家人 (for the family) is perhaps the strongest motivator. Chinese family-oriented purpose statements: 为了孩子的未来 (for the children's future), 为了父母的健康 (for parents' health). These express core Chinese values of filial piety and family responsibility.

items.question: 请关掉手机，_____影响其他观众。
items.translation: (Please turn off your phone so as not to disturb other audience members.)
items.explanation: 以免影响 = 'so as not to affect/disturb.' This is a standard cinema/theater announcement pattern: 请关闭手机以免影响他人 (please turn off phones so as not to disturb others). Compare purpose words: 为了 (in order to — positive goal), 以便 (so that — positive goal, formal), 以免 (so as not to — negative/avoidance goal), 免得 (miǎnde, lest — colloquial negative). 免得 is the casual version of 以免: 快走吧，免得迟到 (let's go quickly, lest we be late).

items.question: 那栋楼_____拆掉了。
items.translation: (That building was demolished.)
items.explanation: 被 (bèi) is the standard passive marker. 被拆掉了 = 'was demolished.' In Chinese, 被 sentences traditionally carry a negative or unpleasant connotation — something bad happened to the subject. 拆掉 = 'tear down/demolish' (拆 tear + 掉 resultative complement). The 了 marks completion. Passive pattern: Subject + 被 + (agent) + verb + complement. When the agent is unknown or unimportant, it can be omitted as here.

items.question: 蛋糕_____妹妹吃完了。
items.translation: (The cake was finished by little sister.)
items.explanation: 被 + agent + verb + complement: 被妹妹吃完了 = 'was eaten up by little sister.' The agent (妹妹) appears between 被 and the verb. 吃完 = 'eat up/finish eating' (吃 eat + 完 finish). Compare: 蛋糕被吃完了 (cake was eaten up — no agent) vs. 蛋糕被妹妹吃完了 (cake was eaten up by little sister — agent specified). The 被 passive requires a result complement or 了 to show completion.

items.question: 他的手机_____偷走了。
items.translation: (His phone was stolen.)
items.explanation: 被偷走了 = 'was stolen away.' In Chinese, passive constructions almost always need a complement after the verb: 偷走 (steal away), not just 偷. The 走 complement indicates the item was taken away. This is a critical rule: bare 被 + verb is usually insufficient — you need 被 + verb + complement/了.

items.question: 花瓶_____孩子打碎了。
items.translation: (The vase was broken by the child.)
items.explanation: 让 (ràng) in passive use = 'was (done) by.' 让孩子打碎了 = 'was broken by the child.' 让 as a passive marker is more colloquial than 被. Hierarchy of formality: 被 (standard/formal) > 让 (colloquial) > 叫 (very colloquial). All three follow the same pattern: subject + 让/叫/给 + agent + verb + complement.

items.question: 作业_____老师批改完了。
